## 一、"统一"不是物理学的目标，而是人类认知的一种心理压迫症
表面上看，这段对话讲的是从牛顿到麦克斯韦再到夸克的统一史。但有一个被忽略的东西：**为什么人类如此执着于"统一"？** Don Lincoln 反复用"staggering"这个词——苹果落地与月球运行竟是同一件事、闪电与冰箱磁贴竟是同一件事。这种"震惊感"本身才是真正值得追问的现象。
宇宙并没有义务是简洁的。把两个不相关的现象写成一个方程，是宇宙的善意，还是我们大脑的偏好？神经科学告诉我们，人脑在静息状态下也会不断"压缩"信息、寻找模式——所谓的预测编码（predictive coding）。**牛顿的"普适引力"和 Maxwell 的"电磁统一"，可能不只是物理事实，更是大脑压缩算法在最高抽象层的胜利游行**。换句话说，物理学的统一史，本质上是一部"信息熵被一次次重写为更短编码"的历史，而那种"啊哈"的震撼，是熵下降时神经系统释放出的奖励信号。
## 二、最被低估的洞见：物理学的"还原论阶梯"和资本主义的"价值链"，结构同构
Don 描述了一条经典的还原论阶梯：**生物→分子→原子→核子→夸克**。每往下一层

item.title: [Business of Sport] The World of Rugby League: Super League Struggle as NRL Dominates?
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被忽略的「时间地理学」陷阱

表面上看，Super League 的困境是钱的问题——Sky 把转播费从 4000 万砍到 2150 万、Wigan 的营收只有 800 万英镑、和 Grimsby Town（一个英乙俱乐部）相当。但真正的结构性问题藏在一个看似无害的决定里：1996 年把赛季从冬天搬到夏天，是一次成功的战术胜利，却是一次灾难性的战略自杀。

为什么？因为这一搬，Super League 同时做了两件互相矛盾的事：在英国本土避开了足球（聪明），却在全球时区上和 NRL 完全同步了（致命）。当英国夏天 = 澳洲冬天 = NRL 旺季，意味着英澳两地的球员市场、转播窗口、赞助商注意力全部在同一个池子里竞争——而池子里有一头 4.5 亿英镑营收的鲨鱼。换句话说，Super League 用「躲避国内强者」换来了「直接撞上国际更强者」。现在传闻中的"被收购"方案要求他们改回冬季赛，本质上是 NRL 在说：请重新回到我们的阴影之外，但代价是承认我们是太阳。

跨领域连接：橄榄球联盟和「文化产品的伦敦溢价」

这里有一个出人意料的连接：Super League 的商业天花板，和英国独立音乐厂牌、北方戏剧团体、甚至格拉斯哥的视觉艺术生态面临的是同一个结构性问题——不在伦敦，就拿不到全国性广告预算。Charles 提到一个关键点："赞助 Wigan Warriors 只能触及英格兰西北市场"。这不是橄榄球的问题，这是英国所有非伦敦文化资产的共同诅咒。

把这个放在更大的图景里看：Rugby League 1895 年因为工人要补偿工资而独立，130 年后它依然被困在同一个地理-阶级矩阵里——它的诞生原因（工业北方的劳动者经济），恰恰也是它今天无法商业化的原因（北方没有跨国公司的市场部）。这项运动的起源神话，就是它的商业诅咒。它在反抗中诞生，却被反抗的对象（南方资本结构）反噬。

真正反常识的观察：NRL「收购」可能是 Super League 唯一的去殖民化路径

这里有个让人意外的反转：所有人都把 NRL takeover 框定为"英国遗产的丧失"，但仔细看会发现一个悖论——被澳大利亚接管，可能反而是 Super League 摆脱「英国体育殖民结构」的唯一方式。

什么意思？在英国体制内，Rugby League 永远是 Premier League 的小弟、Premiership Rugby 的穷亲戚、媒体地理偏见的受害者。它在英国的天花板是结构性的、文化性的、地理性的——这些东西改不了。但一旦接入 NRL 的全球扩张机器（看看他们在 Vegas 的 600% 增长），Super League 反而第一次有机会跳出"英国体育媒体食物链"这个困住它 130 年的牢笼。

这就像是一种奇怪的镜像：当年 Jonathan Davies 这样的威尔士球员"叛逃"到 Rugby League 是为了赚钱、为了生存；今天整个 Super League 集体"叛逃"到 NRL，可能也是同样的逻辑——当一个系统无法在它的原生土壤里生存时，被外部系统吸收不是失败，而是延续。最讽刺的是：澳大利亚作为前殖民地，正在拯救它前宗主国的工人阶级运动。这是一个完美的历史回旋镖。

📝 转录文本

开场：被忽视的运动

New sports, and one of the most requested ones we have had — Rugby League.

So is is that is that is is that is that good? Rugby League. Rugby League. Rugby League. The Challenge Cup final this weekend. This weekend is the Challenge Cup final.

How many requests a week do we get to speak about Rugby League, Charles?

Ah, many, many, many. Double double digit requests every week.

And abuse as well. "Posh southerners talking about Rugby." You know, do you know what — actually they don't. I've realized that sometimes the "posh southerner" thing they actually mean it as an endearing term, because they are actually quite nice about the show. And some of it...

And then there was another ban, and it really made me open my eyes to the size of this, when someone had a pop at us for every time we said "Rugby" not saying "Rugby Union." So we apologize. I apologize for any misrepresentation of Rugby as a total sport. For my attempt at a Rugby League accent just now — just to get that out on the table before before…

Booted.

Yeah. Before I get absolutely smashed.

But it won't stop at it of course.

It won't. But it's it's — but look, that's part of the point. And I, and I — look, I do want to say: you guys as well, those of you that have been requesting it, undoubtedly will know more about this than than we do. We have a different lens of taking a look at some of these things. I want us to be able to unpack some of the things that you and I have been looking at this week to really understand what makes this sport so special to so many people.

I've actually watched more live Rugby League than I have Rugby Union.

So I've actually done it for myself there basically.

Yes.

Okay, so you're at the level of the northern Rugby audience here, and I'll play the Kirsty.

You're going to play Kirsty.

I'm going to play the Kirsty as well, um, just to get it out on the table. But um, there's probably more reflection on how much Rugby Union I watch actually rather than Rugby League. But um, some some close friends of mine — the family was the previous owners of Wigan Warriors — um, and in all sorts of trips up to the north, I actually have watched a lot of Rugby League.

It's also — we're going to come on to this in a moment — it's also played at a time of the year which is outside of a lot of… it's outside of our football season. So if you're working in the football industry, as I mostly have been for for 15 years, you know, when somebody says on a nice sort of, you know, nice forecast next week, they say, "Well, why don't you come up and watch a rugby league match in Leeds?" I'm like, "You know what? That sounds great. Let's do that."

So, but the fact that we're even having this discussion at the start is part of this discussion. You know, one of the best sports in this country not properly discovered by much of it.

媒体覆盖的萎缩

It's not covered much these days outside of the specifically trade media or the northern local media. So if you look at national media coverage of rugby league, it's not great. It used to be much, much more. So, you know, when I was a newspaper journalist, every newspaper would have its rugby league correspondent, and its matches were reasonably prominently displayed. And that has really dropped off in recent times.

And then — I think that's a sort of slight indication of the dominance um of football, but also in some ways that Prem Rugby has perhaps done a better job over the last 10 to 15 years of pushing itself forward. Maybe it's also a function of a lot of the media being based in the south. So there's a little bit more "Well, I kind of know that Bath are playing Bristol because, you know, that's part of…" If you're a sports editor on a newspaper, um I'm living down here. I'm hearing about Bath and Bristol, whereas perhaps I'm not hearing about Warrington and Castleford, etc.

But, is it worth just unpacking for those of our viewers and listeners who don't have um, you know, necessarily detailed understanding of the sport and of the difference between rugby league and rugby union — is it worth just a quick recap on how this all came about?

Can we play Dominic Sandbrook here?

This is — this is where I say that I know what I'm talking about and then you two take all the pot shots at me along the way.

Basically.

Right, good. Let's do that. Right, rugby league. Rugby league's origin —

Can you do it in an accent?

No. No, I've done that bit and I know I'm going to get slapped for it. Okay.

Rugby League 的起源：1895 年的分裂

So, rugby league's origin: 1895 in Yorkshire. And this was a schism, a split from the Rugby Football Union over the issue of payments to players. That is how long ago this fundamental issue, which persisted until the 1990s, okay? So it was 100 years when rugby league was operating in the north on a professional basis, whilst rugby union continued to be an amateur game, mostly in the south, albeit there were some northern clubs as well — northern rugby union clubs as well.

So the rugby league, which is now called the Rugby Football League, but it was then called the the Northern Rugby Football Union back in those days, okay? They start operating as a fully professional sport 130 years ago — a long, long time ago.

And this was players who wanted compensation from the RFU, from national rugby, because they were having to go away, they were traveling, and they were losing out on their wages, mostly from their industrial jobs in mills and shipyards. And so that was what they were asking for, right? Basically, "We're going to commit to this in an amateur sport, we need some form of financial compensation."

Yeah.

Which was deemed to be totally out of court by the Rugby Football Union, as it was until the 1980s. In the 1980s, the RFU started to allow some sort of forms of payments of the kind that they were asking for back in 1895. So, not full-time sort of professional employment, but yeah, you can get paid your expenses. Yeah, you can take a bit of appearance money. Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.

So, you know, what we're looking at here is a sport that then grew up in a specific part of the country for the first half of the 20th century, and then on into the post-war years, etc., being the the professional side of rugby, but quite very clearly geographically delineated to the northwest of England and Yorkshire, and going over to Hull. Okay? So that's — if you think about that stretch of the country as rugby league country.

Um, and meanwhile, as we covered on a previous breakdown, the rugby union side of the game carried on not just in an amateur state, but it didn't even have a league. They played friendly matches against each other and had an annual knockout cup competition. So the determination of the Rugby Football Union to stay amateur — and not just be amateur, but be very amateurish about the way in which they were doing things — was a huge huge deal.

球员的双向迁徙

And what it meant was is that throughout the 20th century, but particularly after the war and particularly moving into the '70s and '80s, um you would see very prominent rugby union players transferring to rugby league in order to be able to make a full-time living from the game.

So when I was, I don't know, in my mid-teens, probably the the top British rugby union player was a Welsh fly-half called Jonathan Davies, who will be known to many as the BBC's rugby pundit these days. A great character, but was a genius fly-half. Absolute genius of a fly-half. And he transferred from rugby union to rugby league in 1989. And when you think that rugby union started to go properly professional in the mid-1990s, he was sort of, you know, the dying sort of gasp of that old era of rugby union players moving into rugby league.

And this happened particularly for Welsh players, where rugby union players in Wales often came from working-class backgrounds. So if you think about a lot of the English rugby union players, they had all their alternative careers where, you know, Brian Moore was a solicitor, some of them would be in the army, so on and so forth. There were, you know, exceptions like, you know, Wade Dooley being a, you know, Ackford and policeman and these type of things. There were exceptions, but broadly speaking, a lot of the English rugby union side would have quite comfortable livings on as their main job and then would play England rugby and club rugby as a pastime — a very serious hobby, which they were very committed to, but they were drawing a salary from something totally different.

But if you came in in Wales, where rugby union is and always has been more of a working class game, that was always a bit more of a challenge. So you got a world star, a global star, Jonathan Davies, who was a big star with the first rugby union World Cup in 1987. It was full back page news. I remember it when Jonathan Davies translated into rugby league and went to go and play for Widnes.

Um what you then find when rugby union goes professional — and quite quickly, and maybe a little bit too quickly in retrospect — starts paying players quite a bit of money, is you start to see players moving back in the opposite direction for the first time. So if you look at like Jason Robinson, who was a young rugby league star for um for Wigan, moving into rugby union and then becoming a key part of the England World Cup winning side in 2003.

两种橄榄球的规则差异

There are, of course, as the vast majority of our listening viewers will know, significant rule differences between the sports. Rugby union being — and I please, you know, I'm fully understand that there'll be many people going, "For God's sake, we don't need to be told this." But there are others who won't.

  • Rugby union is a 15 person game and rugby league is a 13 person game.
  • In rugby union, the scrum is a central part of the sport. In rugby league, it's fundamentally a way to get the game restarted.
  • Things like rucks are totally different in rugby league compared to what they are in rugby union.

So there are significant differences in the game which give it a very, very different feel and in fact, quite often quite different body shapes, frankly, of the players who play these two games.

[laughter] Fit.

I mean, it's like — I think they've done various tests for overall fitness. I think they've done various tests to say that the most fit sportsmen around — if you take overall fitness including cardiovascular, pace and power, and pure strength — that if you take a combination of all those things, which some people have tried to do, that rugby league players are the fittest sportsmen around.

Do you know what? It'd be interesting, I should have had a look. Ball in play time has been a massive thing around broadcasting people looking at where the other sports have fallen down. Rugby union has a horrendous number, like football does. Be interesting to see how much more the ball is in play in rugby —

Rugby league — but in rugby league the ball's in play almost all the time. Now, these guys are absolute powerhouses and ripped.

Yeah.

You know, I remember going round the Wigan Warriors training center after they'd completed a gym session. And, you know, it was extraordinary physical specimens.

阶级、政治与教练血统

But, looking at this now then, what we really talking about? This is much more than a sport, you know, this is a political element. It's an act of defiance — and even in its formation. It's a class system in its own side as well, you know, you would have had your players which would have been the kind of industrial proletariat versus the kind of public school system that would have done rugby union.

So, yeah, I think it can be sort of encapsulated in the two words, which is called Andy Farrell.

Yeah.

So, Andy Farrell, who is one of the greatest rugby union coaches in the world, um came to play rugby union right at the end of his career, but was absolutely a rugby league legend before that. And, of course, the father of Owen Farrell. And, sometimes you see sneaking into rugby union parlance a little bit of sort of, you know, "Andy Farrell style style of rugby's a bit rugby league." It comes more from that tradition of things.

Shaun Edwards would be another one who's one of the great defense coaches in the rugby union world, but came from a rugby league background. And, again, you'll sit here and people say, "Well, you know, I'm sure it's very good technically, but it rather destroys the fun of the game that Shaun Edwards is so good at defending." Because rugby league traditionally was much more structured and much more professional, as you might imagine.

Super League 的诞生与挑战杯

Is it worth just saying while we're talking about this? All right, so the hook is that this weekend is the Challenge Cup final. That's the equivalent of the FA Cup final.

Historically, rugby league was a winter sport, but when the Super League was formed, which was back in, I think, around about 1996, around about that time, it changed to being a summer sport to try and get it away from the football schedule and get it onto good pitches where free-flowing rugby... And it was all part of the brave new world of Sky Sports. So, "Let's get a proper Sky Sports contract. Let's play the games at the time of year when we're not competing against football. Let's be different. Let's be bold. Let's be better."

And broadly speaking, you know, Super League — that's the main competition in Europe of rugby league, the main club competition of rugby league — has been in quotation marks "a success." Okay? So we haven't seen loads of clubs going bust. We've seen one or two. We haven't seen massive drop-offs in terms of spectator numbers or anything like this.

But at the same time, it has been a little bit left behind, and particularly left behind by the Australian league, right? And the Australian league is the monster of all rugby leagues, bigger even than the French Top 14 rugby union league. It's an absolute beast of a commercial product.

营收对比：刺眼的数字

I've just got some revenue numbers here to leave you with, okay?

  • Super League: annual combined revenues of around about 100–120 million pounds.
  • Prem Rugby: maybe 200 million pounds — more, but not generationally more.
  • NRL (Australian league): 450 million pounds a year.

And this has really started to cause a problem in terms of ability to retain the best players, um retain commercial attention, um and has led to, just in recent weeks, it becoming clear that there might be on the horizon a situation where the NRL, the Australian league, effectively almost takes over Super League.

Yeah. So, I mean, look, that's a good entry point into the fundamental challenges facing it in this country. Because from my view and lack of experience in this, seeing the business side of it is quite eye-opening. The numbers are quite frightening for the longevity of the game in this country if it stays in the same form that it has. And that's where I think there's a lot of question marks at the moment.

The Sky deal that they had previously — 40 million a season. 24 to 26: 21.5 million per season. That's a per club distribution of 1.24 million pounds per club.

Yeah.

So in the context of the Premier League where you're getting 100 million, you're getting a rugby league club here 1.24 million.

If you remember we were talking about rugby union's TV deal the other day and we were saying how it was struggling in comparison to the French league and we were saying that you know it was around about 35 million a year.

Yeah.

So three to four million for the clubs individually.

Four million each of the clubs individually.

So rugby league until relatively recently had a bigger TV deal than rugby union's top division. Now it's significantly smaller TV deal than top division.

And when you look at the revenues of some of the top rugby league clubs, the really famous ones in this country, you know:

  • Wigan Warriors (probably the biggest name of all): total revenues of seven and a half maybe eight million quid this year.
  • St. Helens (their traditional biggest rivals): eight and a half.
  • Leeds (currently the biggest revenue club): 10, 11 maybe.

So you're looking at revenue numbers which roughly equate to a top end league two club in football.

Yeah. I mean, what I said, I think I said to you the Wigan Warriors number was pretty much fair — was very close to the conversation I had with Grimsby Town CEO Paul Hearn recently, which was 6.9 million.

So Wigan Warriors, Grimsby Town. Okay. So if you think about those two — with great respect to Grimsby Town, I've got a lot of affection for Grimsby — but I don't think even Grimsby fans would claim that in national sporting terms it's on a level with Wigan Warriors, and yet their revenues are about the same as Wigan Warriors.

商业现实：体育必须财务自洽

So let's get back to the conversation we have regularly which is: sports need to financially make sense.

Yeah.

And it looks right now like while rugby league may have some fantastic following — and it is, you know, it's 10,000 let's say average fans per week, which is still —

Wigan averages 13 and a half thousand a week.

A half thousand, but that's the top, right?

Top. Yeah.

So you bring it down to an average of 10. That's not enough just to keep this sport thriving.

So, it's going to be interesting what happens this season. The London Broncos has been the subject of significant investment from Australians, okay? And this season have had a really good season in the second division — yeah, the Championship. And it looks possible that they will be applying. It looks possible they will be admitted to Super League.

地理与商业天花板

That geographic divide is a problem. And it's not — it's partially a problem because, you know, in terms of how many clubs there are in a very small geographic area. Whereas, you want to see the thing spread out further.

It's also a problem commercially, frankly. I think one of the biggest issues I — when I looked at these revenues of rugby league clubs — was that their commercial revenues are absolutely tiny. Cuz you've got, you know, they come from a part of the country where there aren't necessarily a large number of private enterprises who have got the marketing budgets to put a lot of money behind a product which is really focusing very much on that local market.

Okay? So if you go and sponsor the Wigan Warriors, you aren't necessarily going to be hitting a big national and international market on a regular basis. You are going to be hitting that northwest of England market. Um and without having that visibility in the capital, which is where a lot of marketing budgets are set for more national type companies, there's definitely a challenge there.

Um and then you've just got the other problem, which is — and this is something I've said before about, you know, professional women's soccer football — is that we're in a tightening of the media rights market, full stop. Where there's a sort of race to the top in terms of those broadcasters who pay these big TV deals, including Sky, have come under pressure as broadly speaking fewer people are paying their monthly subscriptions as the years and the decades go by.

So then they have to start prioritizing, right? If we only have so much money to spend, where are we going to spend it? And they then start to look at some sports and say, "Well, we'd still like to televise you, but we can't afford to pay you much for it cuz where you going to go?" So Sky brings their total amount they're paying you down from 40 million to 25 million a year. What — why do they feel able to do that? Because they're making a bet there isn't much competition. There's only really like TNT who could potentially say, "Okay, well, we'll do that." And you're sort of saying, "Okay, we don't think they're going to do that. So we think we can still get those rights for 25 million. And even if somebody else does do it, we don't care that much."

So you've got: - The commercial challenge — lots of clubs located in a small part of the country that is not necessarily the wealthiest part of the country. - The lack of geographic disparity in terms of commercial partners getting projection throughout at least this country, even if not on a more international basis. - A tightening media rights market. - The Australian league being very commercially strong and able to really push for some of the best players in the world.

So it's not straightforward for the English clubs to say, "Well, what we're going to do is we're going to attract some of the biggest stars to come and play and that's going to really push us forward." That's not going to happen because the Australian clubs can pay so much more money.

球员流失的风险

It's a huge risk right now for teams here. It's losing the best talent to Australia. I think you look at just some of the earning potential of those players. You mentioned it. You know, I think the minimum salaries in Australia are about 70,000 pounds. The minimum salaries here are 35 maybe. Um, the top players in Australia will earn, you know, nicely into six figures. Maybe even the top ones are earning 70.

And they are national figures there. They're the top Australian rugby league players. It's just important to note — and you know, for again, we have many Australian listeners who might be throwing stuff at the screen saying, "Well, we know all this." But it might not be the case in this country. People don't understand that in Australia, which is a massive sports market, rugby league is considerably bigger than rugby union.

Yeah.

Massively so, particularly commercially. It's prime time. Rugby league is real prime time in Australia.

AFL and rugby league, wouldn't you? Yeah, I think rugby league was — I think I saw something — was second most profitable sport behind AFL.

Yeah. So, you know, huge.

AFL being Aussie rules.

AFL being Aussie — yeah. And I remember being in Australia and being absolutely shocked by that.

NRL 的扩张策略：拉斯维加斯实验

But what they have started to do, and they seem to be better at doing, is furthering the ambitions of that league. And so, being expansive, being creative —

Who are you talking about here?

Just the AFL.

Sorry. The NRL.

Yeah. What they've been doing in Las Vegas. I don't know if you've seen this. They've basically taken a game out to the US, and last year the Super League did the same.

Yeah.

So they actually played a game in Vegas.

Yeah.

Now, trying to take this game into a US audience is an obvious tactic, but they actually went put something on the ground in Vegas. They then saw a 600% year-on-year increase. The average audience for that first season opened the lot — the next year was 371,000 on Fox. It was actually on Fox. It was Wigan versus Warrington that played in Vegas in 2025.

So moving things out into new markets is a key part of it. We don't seem to be even moving it into parts of the country here.

Well, I think that they are trying. I honestly think that the governing — you know, the authorities of rugby league in this country are really trying. Hence their openness to a discussion with the NRL.

拟议的"收购"：含义与代价

Now, I think it should be quite clear we should be quite clear about what the implications of that might be. We said earlier on that since the mid-to-late 1990s, rugby league in this country has been a summer sport. Now, in Australia it's an Australian winter sport, which is our summer.

So part of the proposed deal with the NRL would probably involve rugby league in this country becoming a winter sport again. So that it wouldn't clash with the NRL's season.

So those are the levels of compensation that English — British rugby league rather. And of course there is also a team from the Basque Country. So it's not just an English league, but nonetheless it is broadly speaking English league. They are willing to pump their teams out to Vegas. They are willing to enter discussions with the NRL.

I think that it's quite clear to me that they are very, very aware that they have a looming — or already have a problem and potentially been bigger problem.

Two or three years ago they did a partnership with IMG, which is the global sports management sort of giant, you know, um has dominated sports like golf and tennis in the past and is one of the biggest agencies in the world. They actually came up with a bit of classic corporate speak:

"The point of the partnership: strategically repositioning the sport to maximize its commercial potential for long-term growth, build deeper relationships with fans and attract new audiences."

Um, I mean, you know, so it's not exactly Belgian football federation talk. Um you know, it is quite sort of corporate speak. But it looks to me that that was about two or three years ago when that partnership was founded. It looks to me like basically… having tried everything, they've ended up going, "Do you know what? We might be better off just being taken over by the Australians."

Yeah.

Um and there's been a bit of resistance to the words "takeover." But then when you read some of the things that are being considered as part of this takeover, you look at it and go, "Yeah, it's basically a takeover."

周末的挑战杯决赛

Um so it's going to be really interesting to see where that goes. Look, the Challenge Cup final on this weekend is one of British sports big showpiece events at Wembley.

60-plus thousand people I think were there the last time.

Normally about 65,000–70,000 people. Um this one is between Wigan and Hull Kingston Rovers. And I think as of earlier on today they'd already sold 61,000 tickets for that. So you can probably confidently expect that's going to be 65–70,000 who'll be attending that match on the day.

After — and this I hope for the players' sake that the weather's not the same. Cuz playing rugby league in that kind of heat would be brutal.

I'm feeling it just sitting here in my chair right now.

I'm sweating even imagining what that what that would be like.

国家队竞争力：被忽略的关键变量

There's one thing which I think has really played against them in the last 20 years. And in as much as they could do anything, my goodness me they need to focus on this.

When I was young and rugby league was a more nationally positioned sport, it was on the BBC quite a lot etc. The British rugby league team — and indeed the English Rugby League team — was genuinely competitive with the Australian national Rugby League team, and indeed could and did sometimes win series against them.

And there were some figures who during that period did become national figures, national sporting figures. People like Ellery Hanley and Martin Offiah of that period were national heroes. You know, Sports Personality of the Year — I can't remember if they won it, but they were absolutely in the conversation. They were very much part of that whole thing.

And probably for the general sports-watching public these days, some of them might struggle to identify a player. And that's probably because the national team has simply not been competitive against Australia for quite a long time. And that's when you start to drag in the broader audience.

Isn't that kind of another problem, though?

Yeah.

Like, you know, that — Yeah. — You need to have rivalries. You need to have competition to make sports attractive.

Yeah. Um, but Australia is the big one. And if they could lift their levels, become consistently competitive with the Australian national team, then I think we might might might start to look at a different situation.

结尾：邀请观众参与

Okay. Look, as I would genuinely pleased — we really want this. Engage with this on this. Would love to really understand.

On how many things we've got wrong in that conversation?

Probably tons. But — what's the spread?

What of?

Spread of mistakes. Eight to nine?

Eight to nine.

Is that what you think?

Yeah. I'm just — I'm just I'm just a guess.

It's hard — it's hard to — I hopefully none of the big things. Maybe a few figures or something in there because reporting's always a bit dodgy. I won't blame that on Kirsty.

Blame it on Kirsty.

Blame it on Kirsty.

But no, there will be people here that live and breathe Rugby League wanting you to bring that passion to let us know about this, because I'm genuinely intrigued.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个被忽略的「时间地理学」陷阱
表面上看，Super League 的困境是钱的问题——Sky 把转播费从 4000 万砍到 2150 万、Wigan 的营收只有 800 万英镑、和 Grimsby Town（一个英乙俱乐部）相当。但真正的结构性问题藏在一个看似无害的决定里：**1996 年把赛季从冬天搬到夏天，是一次成功的战术胜利，却是一次灾难性的战略自杀**。
为什么？因为这一搬，Super League 同时做了两件互相矛盾的事：在英国本土避开了足球（聪明），却在全球时区上**和 NRL 完全同步**了（致命）。当英国夏天 = 澳洲冬天 = NRL 旺季，意味着英澳两地的球员市场、转播窗口、赞助商注意力**全部在同一个池子里竞争**——而池子里有一头 4.5 亿英镑营收的鲨鱼。换句话说，Super League 用「躲避国内强者」换来了「直接撞上国际更强者」。现在传闻中的"被收购"方案要求他们改回冬季赛，本质上是 NRL 在说：**请重新回到我们的阴影之外，但代价是承认我们是太阳**。
## 跨领域连接：橄榄球联盟和「文化产品的伦敦溢价」
这里有一个出人意料的连接：

item.title: [Business of Sport] Was the First Enhanced Games a Success?
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：这不是一场"体育赛事"，而是一场伪装成体育的"制药业IPO路演"

整段讨论中最容易被忽略的细节是——Enhanced Games 是一家上市公司，市值 12 亿美元，首日还涨了 20%。但更关键的是其 SEC 文件中的自述："未经证实的商业模式、有限的运营历史、几乎为零的收入"。

这意味着：观众以为自己在看一场体育比赛，但实际上这场比赛本身才是产品的"演示版"，真正的产品是台下投资人正在押注的"未来制药消费市场"。一场只破了一个世界纪录、半数选手默默无闻的"失败赛事"，从资本市场角度看反而可能是成功的——因为它完成了一次"概念验证"（POC）：证明顶级运动员愿意为药物背书、证明媒体会报道、证明监管机构会愤怒（而愤怒就是免费曝光）。

Aaron 那句"每个有腰痛的中年男人都会问：他在用什么？我去哪买？"——这不是开玩笑，这是招股说明书里的核心叙事。

二、跨领域连接：Enhanced Games ≠ LIV Golf，而 = 加密货币 ICO + 大麻合法化运动

主持人把它类比成 LIV 高尔夫，但这其实是个误导。更精准的类比组合是：

  • 像 ICO（首次代币发行）：项目方先发行"故事"（破纪录、颠覆传统），用早期资本买通"名人站台"（运动员=KOL），再通过媒体反应抬升估值，真正的"产品"永远在路线图里。
  • 像大麻合法化产业链：先创造一个"合法的灰色地带场景"（赌城拉斯维加斯、单日赛事），让公众反复目睹"使用 + 不出事"，逐步消解污名（destigmatization）——这正是 Charlie 最后说的"创造一个新的童话"。

更隐秘的连接是与硅谷的"长寿研究"运动：Peter Thiel 是 Enhanced Games 投资人，也是 Bryan Johnson 式抗衰老叙事的金主之一。Enhanced Games 实际上是 Thiel "用技术超越生物极限"这一意识形态的体育表达层——它和延寿、脑机接口、AI 共享同一个哲学根源：人类不该接受"自然给定"的限制。从这个角度看，这不是一场体育商业事件，而是超人类主义（transhumanism）的市场测试。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：IOC 的"道德垄断"正在被自己的吝啬反噬

主持人触及了一个真问题，但没说透：Enhanced Games 不是"体育的对立面"，而是IOC 财务结构必然孕育的对偶产物（dual）。

让我把这个系统拆开来看：

维度 传统奥运体系 Enhanced Games
收入分配 IOC 拿走 80-90 亿/周期，运动员几乎零分成 单项奖金 25 万 + 破纪录 100 万
道德资本 极高（童话叙事） 极低（公开"作弊"）
时间结构 4 年一次，稀缺性垄断 必须高频（否则商业模型崩溃）
风险承担方 运动员（身体 + 收入） 运动员（身体）+ 投资人（资本）

意外的发现：Enhanced Games 在做的事，本质上是把"运动员身体的折旧"这项原本被 IOC 免费消耗的资产，重新进行了金融定价。James Magnussen 一句"必须有 100 万摆桌上我才考虑"——这不是贪婪，这是一名顶级劳动者第一次为自己的"职业风险"开出了对价。

这就引出一个让传统体育治理者很难接受的结论：Enhanced Games 越被道德谴责，越证明 IOC 的分配体系存在结构性盗窃。 因为如果运动员能从 IOC 拿到合理分成，这种"魔鬼交易"在经济学上根本不成立。Wada、IOC、World Aquatics 越是激烈封杀，越像是垄断者面对竞争威胁时的反应——而不是健康守护者的反应。这里有一个反身性悖论：他们抗议得越用力，越证明对方击中了系统弱点。

一个尾声式的预言

如果三年后 Enhanced Games 还活着，真正的转折点不会是某个世界纪录，而是第一家上市制药公司公开成为其官方"营养与表现合作伙伴"那一刻。那一刻，体育、医药、消费、金融的边界会正式坍缩成一个新的产业范畴——可能叫 "Human Performance Economy"。届时回头看 2026 年这场"令人失望的拉斯维加斯单日赛"，会像今天回看 2009 年比特币创世区块一样：当时所有人都觉得无聊，事后才发现那是分水岭。

📝 转录文本

开场：一个"有直接关联"的主持人

The enhanced games. I don't know about you, maybe it's because I've had some direct association with it for a few years, but I can't believe… When you said you have some direct interview. Am I — Oh, no. The founder, the founder. Am I — am I regularly sitting across somebody who a chemically enhanced co-host? Yes. Can you not tell? I am, I am, I'm performance-enhancing driving all over the place. Yeah. Anyway, the enhanced games, I mean — wow. Wow. What a story this has. And sorry, my point was: it's been a story in and around athletics, broader sport, for quite a long time.

什么是 Enhanced Games？

Can we just go back to what it is? Yeah. Well, let's give you a really simple understanding. If you don't know what the enhanced games is, it's basically the brainchild of a guy called Aaron Duza, who's a lawyer, an Australian lawyer. He had this idea: what if you could create a competition in athletics that celebrated the use of performance-enhancing drugs and technology that is legalized. Right? So — Legalized but currently against the rules of the governing bodies — saying legal — Legal, right. And this is the key — this is going to be a key point — because there is what the governing bodies allow and what the law allows.

So really it's a: hey, if we got the top athletes and they were able to take the testosterone pills and all of that stuff, would they be breaking a lot more world records? We think they would. Do people want to see humans perform at the highest level leveraging the best of science and technology? I think they do. Let's create an environment and a tournament that allows them to do that. But we have to do it in a way that is safe.

And I say "safe" like this — because, you know, I think there's a huge amount of unknowns with a lot of this — but also, most importantly, that also creates a system that pays athletes, that recognizes that they are extraordinary and that they should be compensated better than they traditionally are in athletics today.

从构想到落地：拉斯维加斯一日赛

Now this has been a conversation for a good few years. I did an interview with Aaron over two years ago, and back then we were talking about the games launching. And it is only just this weekend gone by — launched in a one-day event in Las Vegas. So not a kind of week-long, two-week-long Olympics.

The original — I think it's fair to say that the original ambition for this inaugural event was bigger than it ended up being. Is that fair, Charlie? Yeah. So I don't think that's being overly critical, because sometimes you have to just understand that maybe it's a little bit harder to get the whole thing to the scale that you want it to straight away. So it's just a recognition of reality.

It ended up being, I would say, a somewhat underwhelming event — and I'm just saying this as a sports spectator, sports business spectator and sports executive. Somewhat underwhelming in terms of the venue: it took place in Vegas, looked pretty smallscale, pretty unambitious, just to the naked eye. The level of athlete that they were able to attract across a number of different sports — athletics, swimming, weightlifting, these types of Olympic sports — there were three or four or five, six or seven recognizable names who you would know from watching the Olympics. But probably the majority of the competitors were not even household names.

底层假设的"愤世嫉俗性"

But the fundamental underlying assumption of it, I think — and I just want to see what your view is — I think it's cynical, right? The presumption basically is that a lot of these sports are enhanced anyway. Which is something I hear an awful lot from the general public: "Oh come on, you know, they're all doping, they're all doing it anyway." And that this effectively is a way of getting it all out into the open and saying: well, given that we all know that quite a few of the athletes are juicing, then we might as well just say everyone do it. So at least then you get a level playing field.

Can I just read out a quote from Aaron said on our show two years ago — literally to that point. So this is from Aaron on Business of Sport. He said: according to some scientific research, 44% of athletes competing at Olympics level have used banned performance enhancers at some point in their career, and yet 1% have been caught.

I'm going to leave aside whether those numbers are correct, and also the extent of the usage — because sometimes it can be something as small as an asthma spray. Because the depth of what's now deemed to be a performance-enhancing drug goes so far into the detail of it that it's not always somebody pumping themselves full of testosterone.

You're right. That's the narrative.

"诚实的比赛" vs. 两层愤世嫉俗

So the original narrative fundamentally was cynical. It starts off from: come on, you all know you can't believe what you're really watching. Why don't you at least watch an honest contest, in that everyone's being honest that they are juicing?

And the numbers — in the enhanced games, 91% of athletes admitted to having used testosterone, and 79% admitted using human growth hormone as well. Okay. So effectively, the vast majority of athletes taking part in this event over this last weekend were indeed "enhanced," or, if you were being less kind, doping. But the organizers would say: yes, and it's all out on the table.

So that was the first bit of cynicism. The next bit of cynicism, I think, was basically: well, we're going to show that actually this is genuinely performance-enhancing — because loads of our athletes who are going to perform at these games are going to break world records, which will demonstrate that traditional sport is not only dishonest about it but is also holding back human performance. You're not even getting to see what the human body can achieve, because they're trying to just block all sorts of things — sometimes successfully, sometimes not. Whereas our athletes, with all of that sort of nonsense off the table, will be able to perform to the limit of what the human body can do.

百万美元的奖金结构与唯一的纪录

And in terms of the financial structuring of the event, there were huge bonuses being paid for anyone who did break a world record.

$1 million. $1 million. And across all of the events, there was one world record broken. There was — Which I think was in the 50 meters. Christian Colom, who's the Greek swimmer — interesting, he never made a podium at four Olympics. He swam the 50 freestyle in 20.81 seconds, beating the official world record of 20.88. Yeah. So he won $250,000 for winning the event, plus a $1 million bonus for breaking the world record.

Ben Proud, the British swimmer — very controversial in going across, because he's an Olympic silver medalist for Britain.

He came 0.05 off the 50 butterfly world record. So that's 0.05 away from $1 million. I do have a bit of empathy for Ben Proud in that situation.

Icarus 的启示：药物不是魔法

But Charlie, you're right — this is all about, and has been all about, taking humans to achieve the extraordinary. And what we've really seen here is what we'd expect to see at a normal athletics meet: good athletes get together, and you're likely going to have a record broken at some stage.

I said to you before, it reminded me of something. I don't know if you saw the documentary Icarus — if you haven't seen it, it's an absolutely fascinating documentary on Netflix. I highly recommend you go and watch it. It's about the whole doping scandal that surrounded Russia. And basically there's a guy that documents himself taking performance-enhancing drugs to try and win a top cycling race. And what you realize — and this guy is a very high level competitive cyclist — he could only get to eighth, I think it was, in this competition despite pumping himself full of various different things.

And that tells you that these drugs may take you so far. You can't just assume you're going to take this and break records. If you're going to have a product here, you need to get the top athletes in it.

"为了钱"的清白选手

And the reality is that most notably, three of the headline event winners — Curley in the men's 100 meters, Eivelyn in the women's 100 meters, and Hunter Armstrong in the pool — they all competed clean. You don't have to compete enhanced. So they basically did it and put themselves in competition knowing that they want to still compete in the Olympic Games, but they're here, let's be very clear, for the money.

Now, that side of this discussion is where it does become interesting. Because I think it's very, very easy for governing bodies to sit here and go, "This is ridiculous. This is a joke. This is stupid. It's a gimmick." Blah blah blah. But some athletes are still going. Fred Curley quite entertainingly — he did go and run, but he's actually on a two-year ban for doping, and won bronze in Paris.

治理机构的回应

Anyway — WADA said of the enhanced games: "The enhanced games are a dangerous and irresponsible concept. Clearly, this event would jeopardize athlete health, as it seeks to promote the use of powerful substances for the purpose of entertainment and marketing. Some athletes have died."

IOC: "If you want to destroy any concept of fair play and fair competition in sport, this would be a very good way to do it."

World Aquatics actually are the first ones — I think they've said that anyone competing in enhanced games, whether enhanced or not, will be banned from competing.

Because don't forget that when an athlete says that they're competing clean in the enhanced games, let's be quite clear — that is purely what they are saying. Yes. Right. Yes.

So there is not testing for these substances, or at least not publicly. Interesting — there was testing by the US anti-doping body over the weekend of athletes claiming to be clean. There was testing on site, I think, at the enhanced games to ensure —

That's interesting. I wonder if that was true for the British Olympic Association as well. Well, as far as I know — Ben Proud, and I think we had a female swimmer in it as well. They're swimmers. And the World Aquatics have said: if you're swimming in the enhanced games, you can't swim anywhere else. So I think that may be slightly different. But in athletics, you could do both. Curley once he's not banned next year.

田径的钱去哪了？回到分配问题

But Charlie, we talked a few weeks ago about athletics —

The challenge of athletics: not a good distribution of cash to the athletes. You know, decades of extracting enormous value while paying them a fraction of what's generated — from the IOC, from World Athletics.

Well, I just want to go back, because it's really important to get the detail of this right. We did discuss it a couple of months ago. The problem you've got is that — and we're just talking about athletics here, before we go on to swimming — athletics is the main thing here, both in the Olympics but also in the enhanced games.

So the IOC, which owns the Olympics, makes vast sums of money. Billions. How much, Charlie? 8 to 9 billion, I think. 8 to 9 billion a cycle.

So you're looking at $2 billion a year that they are making, mostly — if we're being honest with you — out of athletics. Because if you think about where the eyeballs go during an Olympic Games, it is heavily towards the athletics.

So we were discussing a couple of weeks ago a bunch of sport brats who play tennis for a living saying that they want to see 25% of all revenue produced by the Grand Slams going to them. If the athletes were to receive 25% of even half of the $2 billion a year that they are producing for the IOC, then that would be producing a vast amount of prize money to go to athletes — which would then mean that these types of temptations simply were much, much less relevant.

Whereas, I think what you're about to say is: are we surprised that athletes in Olympic sports — where the IOC does not distribute any of its money to those athletes, despite the fact that it is being made off their backs — are we surprised then that some of them say: "$250,000 for winning an event, a million if I happen to do a really good time… do you know what, there is literally no way in which I could earn that money doing it the traditional way." Is that what you're saying?

Completely.

James Magnussen 的"摆桌上的一百万"

And James Magnussen — the first big-name Australian swimmer, successful Australian swimmer, first big name to sign up for this a couple of years ago — he was on the radio literally for two minutes with Aaron having a conversation. He then a few days later messaged, said he would be interested in doing enhanced games, but he wanted that 1 million on the table for a world record.

So let's be very clear: the athletes here are not saying, "Oh we really want to go and compete enhanced," they're not saying desperate to take some of this stuff necessarily. They're desperate because, as you said, there's massive financial opportunity here.

So while it's easy to chastise and target this competition as being ludicrous and lunacy and against sporting competition and all of that — it's the product of an environment that we've already discussed, where there is a shortage of proper financial distribution to the areas that matter most in athletics and wider Olympic sport, should we say. That's what allows things like this to come up.

Yep, I agree with that.

另一面：大药厂的隐形之手

There's another side to it. Two other sides. Two other sides to it. I think — I don't know what you think.

Look, my view on this — and it was a while ago, and I think it's become more apparent — I just see this is big pharma play. This is normalizing the use of performance enhancers to the general public through the eye of beautiful sporting competition. It's a marketing tool for a lot of those drugs, or again, products, whatever we want to call them.

And Aaron actually said at the launch in 2025, he said: "Every middle-aged guy who once played competitive sport and is now suffering from back pain is going to say, 'What is he on? And how do I get it?'"

So — yep — if we're looking at this, is it the beauty of paying athletes more and creating an enhanced and technologically advanced population in a sporting environment? Maybe a bit. But let's be very clear: this is a huge financial play. And you can see by who it's backed: Peter Thiel — founder of PayPal, Palantir, first investor in Facebook — Donald Trump Jr. You've got some big money in here.

I'd be interested to see the linkage between the underlying financial structures of the enhanced games and any big pharma companies in terms of sponsorships, partnerships, all of that kind of stuff. I haven't seen any filings on that. Now, the reason I say "filings," Chaz, is because the enhanced games is a quoted company.

上市公司？市值 15 亿

Mad, isn't it? Right? Which — now, you might have, dear listener, dear viewer, you might have received the impression from Charlie and my discussion of the enhanced games over the last 20 minutes that this is a dead duck. Shows how much Charlie and Charlie Junior know.

What is the current market capitalization of the enhanced games on the stock market?

It was 1.2 billion on the stock market. EV, it jumped 20% on debut. 20% jump. So 1.5 billion.

The company's own SEC filings, alongside this, by the way, admits — this is their words — "unproven business model, limited operating history, and minimal revenue to date." So a far from secure investment on flotation.

But it comes back to the purpose of this games and the belief of the people that have injected serious money into it. Big money — that this becomes a huge driver of value for one of the world's biggest industries, the pharmaceutical industry. Make no mistake about it: if you could tie that into sport, and have a shot — your shop window being this type of event — it would be huge.

这场比赛真的"发生"了吗？

And Charlie, it's always been a weird relationship talking about this. Because firstly, it's been kind of a non-starter — I must admit, two years after having the chat with Aaron and still nothing to show for it, I was kind of in the stage of: will this ever get off the ground? Now it has. I'm like: well, that was underwhelming. Did you actually know what was happening until I told you?

Yeah, I did. Yeah. And actually a couple of people had commented on last week's show. But it didn't stand out as the first thing that we should talk about this week, which shows kind of again how much it registers.

I was surprised in the structure when I saw it, because it was very different to how I thought it would be played out, and certainly around the vision. If you're talking like this — the big challenger to the Olympic Games — you expect more than one day in Vegas with a few thousand people there, and some maybe partly well-known athletes, but no massive headlines.

The interesting part is now they've just got an event. Sometimes you say you just got to get started, right? They've proved that they can get started. They've got a world record — whether it's only one — they've got a world record in there.

类比 LIV Golf：第一批"过界者"

Athletes will have seen that money. Colom said that this is transformational money to him. Of course it is. How does that translate to the rest of the industry from an athletes' perspective? I would imagine you've got a few that have been sitting on the fence for a long time, and you'll have a few that would never go near it.

This is almost like — I mean I hesitate to say, because of course it's very, very different in many ways, similar in some ways — a bit like the LIV golf thing. So you've got some first movers, albeit they didn't own any of the really big ones. You've got a very well-funded organization with links to entities, as you say, the pharma industry, which have different reasons to make it work.

And then you've got athletes — and you've got other athletes sat out there earning very, very little money thinking: "Okay, he didn't get a world record because he's not good enough. But if I was taking that substance, I'm already very close to a world record, and I can go and make a million bucks from doing something which I'm pretty sure I can do."

That's Ben Proud. That's Ben Proud. Yeah. That is literally Ben Proud. Entirely plausible if they keep on holding these events that he will do.

频率问题：四年一次行不通

One question I asked you earlier on, which I'm not sure that anyone has the answer to yet, is: clearly, to make this work from a financial perspective for the athletes, they can't have one every four years. Because they don't have an intervening world championships and Diamond League and all these types of things. They can't expect athletes to say, "Oh that was great, sorry I just missed out on the world record, but that 250 grand was great, I'll see you in four years' time." Financially, that wouldn't be attractive. So are they looking at having these events on a regular basis?

Yeah, and that was something that I saw again — I thought I'd put it down somewhere — but Aaron said something along the lines of: "We need to make the athletes' careers more viable outside of the once every four year cycle that it currently sits in," i.e. relying on the Olympic Games to build it.

So the frequency of these games now, I think, is a really good question. We don't have the answer to it. But this is the only competition that would allow enhanced athletes to compete in. So to your point, if athletes are taking performance enhancers, this will be the only event they can compete in, and therefore they will need a cadence of competition.

Yeah.

Well, to make it worth their while, but also to stay at the top of your game. The big critique of LIV golf was not necessarily just money — it was: will the golfers playing it be on the top of their game when they come back into how golf has traditionally been played, competing in four-day events? You'd throw this exact same piece here. To break a world record, you need to be competing relatively regularly to hit that mark. So that will be the next challenge.

But I think us talking about it now, having actually had an event, is a really key element.

道德判断与系统反思

And while again the craziness of it — you read it firsthand, you go: "That's just mad." I do think the more you look behind it — while I still don't think it's right, it's hard to say it's dangerous — but I generally think it's getting into the dodgy lines of what you should and shouldn't be putting out and encouraging people to do.

There's a reason this is here. And I do think there needs to be a point of reflection on the governing bodies, on the powers that be in major athletes, as to why something like this comes about. And ask yourself: do you do a PGA type thing? And do you need to find a better way to keep your top talent supported, so that they can achieve everything that they need to in the competitions that you've laid out?

Because the IOC has never had a competitor previously.

It's never had a competitor. So if you start having athletes taken away from it — what happens?

美国 vs. 欧洲的舆论温差

Look, it'll be interesting. And I have no doubt: if they do get a few more of these out, there will be a lot more talking points on it. It sort of feels — this time, I can't speak for how it felt in the US, which is where it took place and where most of the big backers are. So it may be that there was more of a buzz around it in the US. I can say that over here in Europe, there was not much buzz around it.

So I think what we saw with LIV was: however much you might have other reasons for doing it, however much you might have deep pockets, or however much your opposition — i.e. the traditional legacy version of what you're doing — might be quite complacent, ultimately, in the end, if you can't attract enough eyeballs to what you're doing and enough general interest, then commercially it will fail ultimately. Because these sums that they're paying to athletes have to be paid by somebody.

收尾：童话的死亡

So I just want to sort of finish on one thought, which is that the biggest challenge with LIV — and an even bigger challenge with this — is in a way the death of the fairy tale.

This beautiful thing that is put in front of the general public: that young people desperately want to play a sport, they do it mostly for the love of it, but because we all love watching it, they end up making money from it, and good on them. Right? That's the beautiful fairy tale that we all want to believe, and that we're brought up to believe. And it's why sometimes football fans get very upset with professional footballers who play for their club, not playing for the badge or whatever it might be — because the fairy tale is that you've got 11 people out on the pitch there who love the club, who love the sport, and yeah, they get paid very well because a lot of us pay money to watch them, and that's quite right. But the whole thing is a beautiful thing.

And we spoke about how the US Masters is this beautiful thing, this beautiful concept where they leave money on the table. LIV — I was about to say — is an ugly thing. It's basically saying: we could basically, like in nice terms, bribe a whole load of players to come over and play in our tournaments rather than those tournaments, and we will make the whole thing brash and modern and this kind of stuff. There's no beauty in that. It was a sort of brute financial economic calculation.

Shame product. Um, yeah, but I think that was probably second or third. The main thing was the brute economic reality of: if we paid him X, he could come here. Oh yeah, definitely.

Right. And that was very, very clear, and that became publicly clear. So the enhanced games is another situation where the fairy tale is being broken.

一个新童话的诞生？

Now, there are a lot of people out there who have been very cynical about traditional athletics and traditional swimming and traditional cycling, etc. So the view should be, or must be: could we capture a big chunk of that audience who are already cynical about these main sports? But once they've lost the fairy tale, does that then mean that they actually then become really passionate about the brute, nasty, modern thing — which accepts all of that cynicism and just says, "Hey, but you know, you should get really excited in this"?

I've just got a funny feeling that the people who have lost the fairy tale, and often are very angry about having lost the fairy tale, aren't then particularly likely to really sort of become passionate advocates of the thing that reflects their own cynicism. I'm not convinced by that.

So we shall see. But as you say, there's a lot of deep pockets behind it. And if you're right that big pharma is actually the main driver behind all this kind of stuff, then actually these types of sums of money that have been put out are absolutely tiny.

Because what is definitely true is that these products — things like testosterone supplementation and human growth hormone — are very high margin products, which currently in the general population carry a significant stigma about them. So if, over 5, 10, 15, 20 years, those products can gradually become destigmatized — as people see that there are healthy young adults who use them totally openly, and who go on to perform really well and have big smiles and do really well — the growth of those markets could be very, very considerable.

But creates a new fairy tale.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：这不是一场"体育赛事"，而是一场伪装成体育的"制药业IPO路演"
整段讨论中最容易被忽略的细节是——**Enhanced Games 是一家上市公司**，市值 12 亿美元，首日还涨了 20%。但更关键的是其 SEC 文件中的自述："未经证实的商业模式、有限的运营历史、几乎为零的收入"。
这意味着：观众以为自己在看一场体育比赛，但实际上**这场比赛本身才是产品的"演示版"，真正的产品是台下投资人正在押注的"未来制药消费市场"**。一场只破了一个世界纪录、半数选手默默无闻的"失败赛事"，从资本市场角度看反而可能是成功的——因为它完成了一次"概念验证"（POC）：证明顶级运动员愿意为药物背书、证明媒体会报道、证明监管机构会愤怒（而愤怒就是免费曝光）。
Aaron 那句"每个有腰痛的中年男人都会问：他在用什么？我去哪买？"——这不是开玩笑，这是**招股说明书里的核心叙事**。
## 二、跨领域连接：Enhanced Games ≠ LIV Golf，而 = 加密货币 ICO + 大麻合法化运动
主持人把它类比成 LIV 高尔夫，但这其实是个误导。更精准的类

item.title: [Lex Clips] Is dark matter real? - Why can't we find it? - physicist explains | Don Lincoln and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个让人意外的视角：暗能量危机其实是"测量学的傲慢"

物理学最大的尴尬——10^120 倍的预测偏差——表面上是个计算问题，但深处藏着一个被忽略的元问题：我们把"积分上限"当成了"现实的边界"。Don Lincoln 不经意间说出的话很关键："如果在 LHC 能量尺度之下出现新物理，误差就只剩 10^60 倍。"换句话说，那个臭名昭著的数字根本不是宇宙的属性，而是我们对自己无知的厚度的精确测量。每次我们把积分推到普朗克尺度，我们不是在计算真空能，而是在计算"我们假装自己懂物理一直到那个尺度"的代价。这和金融里的"模型风险"惊人地同构——华尔街的衍生品定价模型在 2008 年崩盘，不是因为模型错了，而是因为把模型外推到了它从未被验证过的区域。

跨领域连接：暗能量与生物形态发生的"密度守恒"之谜

视频里最容易被读者错过的瞬间，是他强调"暗能量是密度，而不是总量"——空间扩张时，新的暗能量在凭空"出现"。如果空间是量子化的，那么每个新出现的空间量子都自带一份能量。这个图景与发育生物学里的"细胞增殖中的形态原守恒"惊人相似：胚胎中某些信号分子的浓度在细胞分裂时奇迹般保持恒定，因为分子是随着细胞一起"被复制"的，而不是被稀释的。如果暗能量真的以这种"伴生"方式存在，那么宇宙的扩张就不是一张被拉伸的橡皮膜，而是一个正在自我复制的、有内禀代谢的有机体。这意味着宇宙学和发育生物学可能共享同一类数学结构——生成式守恒（generative conservation）而非传统的守恒律。

隐藏的系统性真相：暗物质研究是一面照妖镜，映出了科学共同体的"赌徒谬误"

Don 的告白藏着一个绝大多数人没听出来的潜台词：他说自己 25 年前坚信是引力理论错了，现在却倾向于暗物质真实存在——让他改变立场的不是新理论，而是两个"反例"（子弹星系团和蜻蜓星系）。这其实揭示了一个反常的认识论结构：一个"没有暗物质的星系"比一千个"有暗物质的星系"更能证明暗物质存在。这与医学里证明某种激素存在的方式同构——你不是给病人加激素，而是切除腺体看会发生什么。但更耐人寻味的是后半段：他承认实验灵敏度在过去三十年提高了一百万倍却仍一无所获，于是物理学界正在分裂成"信徒派"和"叛教派"。这个分裂的形状，和宗教改革时期天主教内部的分裂几乎一模一样——当一个核心信念无法被证伪也无法被证实时，共同体会自发地按"忍耐阈值"分层。暗物质实验其实是物理学家在测试自己对失败的容忍极限，而不仅仅是在测试宇宙。

一个出人意料的预测：暗能量的"减弱"信号，可能是宇宙的"中年危机"

最近的测量暗示暗能量可能在减弱。Don 提醒大家"别信"，但如果它是真的，意味着宇宙的命运不是单调的热寂或大撕裂，而是一个有节奏的振荡过程。这与一个让人毛骨悚然的跨领域类比相呼应：生态系统中"顶级捕食者数量的长期波动"。当一个变量长期被假设为常数（猞猁数量、暗能量密度）但后来被发现其实在缓慢振荡，背后往往隐藏着一个慢变量驱动的耦合系统。如果暗能量在变化，最深刻的启示不是"宇宙的终局变了"，而是"宇宙可能根本就不是在朝任何终局走"——它可能像生态系统一样，是一个没有最终状态的、永恒重组的过程。

一、物理学最糟糕的预测：真空能的危机

But there is the what you call the worst prediction in physics — which is...

oh yeah, that's another one — a nice little insight about the complicated nature of dark energy.

So the observations as you described say that empty space has a tiny energy density that accelerates expansion of the universe. But quantum field theory's prediction for what vacuum energy should be when coupled with gravity is much larger.

Mhm.

Uh, so this is what makes for the — quote — you have a video on this, "worst prediction in physics."

Can you explain this crisis?

Well, there's a measurement, and you can measure how fast the universe is expanding, and from that you get a measurement of dark energy. However, if you then say, well, suppose the dark energy is due to fields in space — so that's quantum field theory. Hey, I know a lot about quantum field theory.

And so we can take the quantum field theory and we can calculate what the density of energy is due to quantum field theory. And basically what you do is you take, within a volume, all of the wavelengths — the longer wavelength, the shorter wavelengths, shorter and shorter. And you can add them all up. And each wavelength adds a certain amount of energy. And if you add that all up, then you get a number.

And that number is the rather embarrassing 10 to the 120 power times — that's a one with 120 zeros after it — bigger than the measurement of dark energy.

Yeah.

So you go: yuck, that is not fun at all. And that is because the equation comes to the highest energy, or the smallest wavelength particle that you can imagine, to the fourth power. Since anything to the fourth power is a big deal, that's where you get that awful number.

如果新物理在更低能标出现会怎样？

Now, if it turns out that there is some new physics that's just about at the energy scale we can measure using our biggest particle accelerators — remember I told you that that was a factor: the maximum energy scale, Planck scale, is 10 to the 15 times bigger than what we can measure now. So let's say that we don't have to calculate up to the Planck scale, because something happens, something changes at the energy that we know right now.

Well then that means we don't have to integrate to Planck scale; we integrate to 10 to the 15 less of the Planck scale, and this thing is to the 4th power. So 10 to the 15 to the 4th power is 60.

So now, even if we say — you know, Don, he's brilliant, he's going to find something at the LHC tomorrow, going to solve all this problem — now we've solved it. It's much better. It's only different by 10 to the 60 power, which is still pretty bleeding big.

So the short answer is: there is very clearly something going on, something wrong, very badly wrong, in the quantum field theory. You know, maybe there's another field that balances out the energy, that cancels it down. And even that, you know, that's not so outrageous. Like we have matter and antimatter — they balance pretty well. Okay, maybe there's something going on. You could cancel that out. That'd be perfect.

完美抵消 vs. 不完美抵消

Cancelling something to zero is easy, 'cuz, you know, plus one and minus one, zero — plus two minus two, zero. But we still have dark energy. Dark energy is a little bit. So if it cancels, it doesn't cancel exactly, because it left over that little bit of dark energy. So that is its own curiosity.

Perfect cancellation, pretty easy — theorists do that, you know, eight times before breakfast. Imperfect cancellation, much harder.

二、"解决"暗能量意味着什么？

Just to elaborate that a little bit, what do you think solving — in quotes — solving dark energy would look like?

Well, what you would do is you would hypothesize that there existed some other field that had the reverse effect of existing quantum fields —

but not to zero.

— but not to zero. But if you had it to go to zero, you know, sure, maybe there's a field that exists at really high energies that we haven't seen yet. I don't know, but it cancels things out and we're cool.

How would we then demonstrate the existence of that field?

Uh, well, that would depend on the prediction.

How do you even come up with a new field?

Like all theorists do — well, let's add something to my equation and see what happens. I mean, and that's okay. I'm being glib about that, but that is precisely what you do. You say: what change — we have this thing that works quite beautifully except it fails here. What is the addition that we need to make that changes very little in the realm that we measured and yet fixes this hard thing?

And so you literally just go: okay, what do I need — plus six or something — and as long as it makes no changes where it would hurt our measurements and fixes the big thing, then that is at least a candidate theory. Now, that doesn't mean it's right, but it at least gives you an understanding of what the right answer should look like.

And so that's the first step: what should the real answer look like, or what is a possible real answer. And then once you kind of know that, then other people can look and say, well, let me think about a theory that kind of has the required properties to do what we need it to do. So it's a multi-step process, but the first step is: how do we tame this problem without coming up with really terrible predictions that we've already ruled out.

And that is literally a sensible, viable theoretical thing, you know, 'cuz you have to explore cool ideas.

三、暗能量与宇宙的深远未来

I mean, one of the reasons dark energy is super interesting is it kind of gives us a mechanism by which we can talk about the deep future of the universe. I mean, we have observations about the expansion of the universe, but it's also giving us the mechanism of that, right? So we can talk about — any good model we have that captures some of the weirdness of dark energy might give us insights about how this thing ends, how the universe —

about the deep future of the universe. Right.

Absolutely. As it stands right now: if dark energy is real — and who knows, you know, if it's real exactly as we've measured it — then as the universe gets bigger and bigger, dark energy becomes a bigger and bigger component of the energy balance of the universe, and it takes over, and it drives the continued accelerated expansion of the universe.

And if dark energy gets lower, you know, for some reason that we don't understand — maybe it changes over time, gets smaller — that could change things. If it gets bigger, it could change things.

That is one of the big open questions: whether it's constant over time or not, right?

And there has been a recent measurement that suggests that dark energy is getting smaller. Um, however, that is a new measurement, not confirmed, blah blah blah blah blah. Nobody should believe it, but it's a hint that maybe it's changing, which is kind of cool in itself, because the current bias until recently is that dark energy is constant.

"恒定"的真正含义：恒定的是密度

Now, I want to be super careful, because it's misleading. People say dark energy is constant. Dark energy is a density.

Now think about that. You have a certain density — let's start with that. Then the universe expands. So energy is volume times density. If the universe gets bigger and the density is constant, that means dark energy is increasing. It's not just increasing as a fraction and overwhelming ordinary matter — but ordinary matter, as the universe expands, its density decreases, because it's constant and the volume gets bigger. The density drops.

Dark energy, until recently, is thought to be constant density. So that's what's implied when you say constant. You say "constant density," which means it's actually increasing because space is increasing. The size of space is increasing.

Interesting.

四、空间是否被量子化？

And so that's a weirdness. And that then ties into the nature of space.

Why does that tie into the nature of space?

Well, because if dark energy is a field in space, if you increase the volume, you would think the energy density would drop.

Mhm.

But if space is increasing and space is quantized — and I don't know if it is — then maybe what's happening is space isn't stretching, but like little space particles are appearing as the space, you know, there's like bubbles of space appearing. And each bubble contains a certain amount of dark energy. And so therefore, that would give you a sense that dark energy is a property of space rather than a field in space.

But that's all very handwavy, guessworky stuff.

So if you had bet all your money, is dark energy like a real physical — what does that even mean — thing that exists, versus is this just a renaming of the cosmological constant?

Unfortunately, I think it's both. I mean — well, it is — it's describing a reality, but it's also maybe telling us something about space.

Literally a property of space.

Yeah, it's — I mean, that's kind of what it looks like, given that it seems to be constant density. That seems to me — now, this is not something anybody should believe, please, nobody believe this — but it seems to me that this is leaning towards the idea that:

  • A) it's a property of space,

  • C) as space is expanding, little quanta of space are appearing, and
