and that would kind of explain the constant density.

Now, please, nobody accepts that. This is just nonsense.

沙丘的比喻

But a lot of the stuff that you just said is experimentally — probably experimentally — testable. You can probably construct experiments — the bubbles of —

Well, finding out the bubbles of space — but those quanta conceivably are Planck-sized bubbles.

Yeah, the quanta.

Well, they'd be quantum of space. The idea is, you know, you look at a sand dune and it looks smooth and continuous, but you can see individual grains of sand, right? And so what this is saying is: as this dune expands, new grains of sand are appearing, and each one of them is a quantum of space.

五、用量子引力实验探测时空

So what kind of experiments can we do in the coming decades or centuries to understand dark energy better?

I mean, people have been talking about quantum entanglement of gravity. In standard quantum mechanics, a particle can be in two places at the same time. All right? So now you have two particles. So this particle can be in two places at the same time, this particle can be in two places at the same time. You put them near one another.

Well, if they're close to each other, there's a certain gravitational force. If they're far apart, there's a certain. And if one is close and one is far, you have another one. You can calculate the effects of gravity having to do with quantum entangled particles being in two places.

And people are talking about doing this and trying to see if, in doing such a measurement, they might be able to definitively determine whether gravity is a quantum phenomena or a continuous phenomena. And that is potentially a measurement that could be done soon-ish, because the technologies inherent in all of this recent work on quantum mechanics is allowing people to be able to make instrumentation that might be precise enough to do this measurement.

Now, this will not tell us what quantum gravity is. It will not tell us anything. But it will tell us that gravity is quantized. And just knowing that — well, for one thing, it shuts out a whole realm of continuous gravity. And the theoretical community will then turn its attention — forget this stuff — and think over here. Now, that doesn't tell you that space is quantized, but it tells you that gravity is quantized if it bears out. So, and if gravity is quantized, then people will start thinking more about space being quantized.

六、暗物质：比暗能量更神秘

I have to ask, because you mentioned dark matter is perhaps even more mysterious than dark energy.

Okay.

Can you build up the intuition why it's more mysterious. What is dark matter?

Oh gosh, what is dark matter? A: I don't know. B: it's terribly fascinating.

Yeah.

我们为什么相信暗物质存在？

All right. So first thing, and the most important thing, 'cuz I'm an experimentalist by God — the first thing is: why do we believe there's dark matter? And the reason is that astronomical measurements do not agree with predictions by Newtonian or relativity theory.

  • Galaxies spin too fast.
  • Clusters of galaxies move too quickly.
  • And the distortion of very distant galaxies due to the gravitational field of nearer galaxies disagrees with the prediction from what we see from the observed matter.

So there are three very distinct reasons why we are predicting that something is wrong in our understanding of either the laws of physics or the matter budget of the universe.

旋转星系的简单方程

The easiest one to talk about is the spinning galaxies. Now, what I'm saying is not unique to spinning galaxies, just easiest to talk about. So, galaxies are observed to spin more quickly than they should if we add up the gravity we see. By all rights, galaxies spinning that fast should blow themselves apart, and they don't.

So, what can be the answer? Well, you have the force required for a star to orbit, to move in a circle, and you have the force due to gravity, and they're connected by an equal sign, and the prediction is wrong.

So either the force due to gravity is wrong, the force needed to move in a circle is wrong, or the equal sign is wrong. I mean, this is really simple. One of those things is wrong.

So one possibility is simply that Newton's law of gravity — mass times the mass over r-squared times a constant — that's just wrong. Another possibility is Newton's F = ma, that we are taught in introductory physics, is wrong. Both of those are eminently possible. Maybe we don't understand gravity, or maybe there's more mass than we can see.

So these — I mean, it's nice that you can look at this really simply and come up with a list, you know, cookbook things we can test. And so we've done that. We've gone and said: what are the possibilities?

Well, the most obvious possibility is that there is more mass than we can see. There's black holes. There's hydrogen gas that we can't see. Whatever. There's something out there.

So that was the first thing. So you go and you look — and there's no hydrogen gas, because we can see that through radio waves. That's not it. In the '90s, we went looking for black holes, rogue planets, things like that. Those exist, but not enough of them. That's not it.

And so now we're left with: there's some sort of matter that we can't see, or we don't understand gravity, or we don't understand inertia.

一位物理学家立场的转变

Now, I personally, if you asked me this — oh, I don't know, 25 years ago — I would have said the most likely answer is that we don't understand inertia or gravity. You know, 20 years ago, 25 years ago, that's what I would have said. No problem.

However, there have been a couple of observations that have caused me to change my thinking, and I think that dark matter is more likely.

证据一：子弹星系团（Bullet Cluster）

One of them is called the bullet cluster. So the bullet cluster: there are two large clusters of galaxies. In these large clusters of galaxies, well, any galaxy consists of a couple of components. There are the galaxies themselves. There is the hydrogen gas that surrounds the galaxies. And maybe there is dark matter.

And if dark matter is real or dark matter is not real, you will get different answers.

If those two galaxies pass through one another, the galaxies themselves should pass through one another, basically not interacting. But the big thing is the gas clouds. So if there's big clouds of gas, as the galaxies pass through one another, the clouds should interact and the gas cloud should stop in the middle and be really, really hot.

So then you would see — if there were no dark matter — you would see a cluster of galaxies, cluster of galaxies, a big gas cloud in the middle. And because the big gas cloud in the middle is much more massive than the galaxies themselves, you would expect to see distortions that we call dark matter distortions in the middle.

If, however, dark matter is real, the galaxies pass through one another. The cloud stops. Dark matter doesn't interact with the clouds, so it passes through. In that case, you would expect to see the distortions where the galaxies are — and that's what we see.

So that is a strong evidence, in my mind. The bullet cluster is strong evidence that dark matter is a real thing.

证据二：蜻蜓星系（Dragonfly Galaxies）

And there is another example which is much more recent. Dark — bullet cluster was a while ago — called the dragonfly galaxies. There's Dragonfly 2 and Dragonfly 4. These are galaxies that rotate exactly according to Newton's laws. And so the fact that they rotate exactly according to Newton's laws says that whatever is causing galaxies to rotate too fast is not a property of matter.

But if you had a galaxy where there was no dark matter — for whatever reason, it got stripped off or something — this is one of those lovely ironies that the existence of a galaxy with no dark matter is very strong evidence that dark matter is real, because you can take the dark matter out.

So the DF2 and DF4 also suggests to me that dark matter is real.

So now, while it remains possible that we need to modify the laws of inertia or we need to modify the laws of gravity — those are possible still, in my opinion, and now this is Don's opinion, but it's probably the opinion of most of the scientific community — dark matter is likely a real thing.

七、暗物质是什么？我们排除了什么

Now that's great. I've taken you all the way to dark matter. So now you're going to ask me — you're going to say, "Don, what is dark matter?" I'm going to: I don't know. But I know what it isn't.

Okay? I know that it is not black holes. I know that it is not rogue planets. I know that we've done the measurements. We've looked across nearly every mass range for compact objects and ruled them out.

So if dark matter is real, it can't be made of those. So then you're left with the idea that dark matter is a particle. And that's what we've thought about. The name for the dark matter particle that we've called for a long time is a WIMP — for Weakly Interacting Massive Particle. And we have spent the last, god, 30 years looking for them in various ways.

寻找暗物质的三种方法

There are three ways that we might see dark matter.

方法一：直接探测。 The direct way, which says that dark matter exists literally everywhere in this room, in our laboratory, and the dark matter is passing through the earth like a wind. And we put up detectors trying to see those. We have done that and we've seen nothing.

So we should say we have done that for neutrinos.

We've done that for many different types of dark matter. We just simply put detectors in labs deep underground, and we can see neutrinos in them. It's true. But dark matter would have — especially heavy dark matter — these WIMPs, they have a different signature, and we've seen no evidence of dark matter interaction in these detectors.

So neutrinos are also weakly interacting and also have mass —

They are.

— but not enough mass. So WIMPs are heavy on the —

Right. Neutrinos are indeed WIMPs of a sort. Now, we have to be careful what we mean by WIMPs. They are weakly interacting massive particles. But we can calculate, and there's just not enough mass in them. It's not it.

Got it.

So we need another form. And we have seen zero evidence of this wind of dark matter through the earth.

方法二：间接探测。 Another possibility is you look where you think dark matter might be concentrated, at the center of galaxies. And if dark matter exists and there's antimatter dark matter, maybe they annihilate and make photons. And so we look for gamma rays and various other signatures of annihilating dark matter. And there are always constantly announcements of "oh, we saw it," "oh, we didn't" — the problem is that way of looking for dark matter is hard because there are other ways of making, for instance, gamma rays, like neutron stars and stuff, and you really need to understand the details of galaxies really, really well to believe that.

方法三：对撞机制造。 And then the final option is what I do, where we smash particles together at high energy. We try to make dark matter particles. If you make dark matter particles — because they don't interact except via gravity — they escape with your detector. So what you hope to see is an event where you collide particles, a dark matter particle escapes and you don't see it, but the recoil you see on the other side, because momentum is conserved. So you see a blob of energy on this side, nothing on the other side. Maybe that's dark matter.

And that also happens with neutrinos. So you need to understand everything about neutrinos and calculate how many of those you see, and then hope you see more, and then that might be dark matter.

Again, that hasn't worked.

巨大的质量范围

So, we've ruled out some dark matter particles. But the problem is the range of space of possible mass. If dark matter is of a particulate form, the range of viable dark matter ranges from something like the mass of an asteroid to far lighter than an electron — and everywhere in between. And we have looked, we've ruled out some little spots in that phase space, but that's a big range.

Is it really possible to miss a particle the size of an asteroid?

The astronomical searches were not sensitive to that level of dark matter. But, you know, then you would expect that there would be some of those in the solar system. And if they're what we think — like asteroids or something — then we'd heat them up and we'd eventually see them. But if they're really like truly dark matter — doesn't interact with matter, which means they wouldn't absorb energy from the sun — so they'd be really dark. I don't know, maybe they're out there.

But the only way we have searched for them was a thing called microlensing. So if a massive object — you have a distant star and a massive object pass between that star and your eye — that star will momentarily brighten.

Mhm.

And so you just look for these, what they call microlensing events, and you count them, and you see some — and we did see some. You know, black holes pass in front of stars and we've seen them, but we just haven't seen enough. And for very low-mass particles like asteroids, they just wouldn't make enough brightening effect to see. So there's like a minimum sensitivity of brightening, and that — about a third the mass of a moon, our moon — is about the sensitivity that we had.

So, you know, nobody I think really thought that these low-mass guys were likely. What they thought was more likely — they were just unseen black holes, which I think is completely reasonable. Then when that got ruled out, I thought, okay, modified gravity or inertia. Well, now that, you know, bullet cluster and dragonfly seems to have ruled that out. So I'm stuck in my head with: dark matter seems to be real, and we don't know what it is.

And it makes up a giant percentage of matter in the universe.

It is five times more prevalent than ordinary matter.

Wow, this is incredible. This is so fascinating.

八、对未来研究者的寄语，以及对"直接观测"的渴望

And that's why it's cool. So, if someone out there is, you know, a young person wants to get into this, understanding dark matter is a big deal. I mean, it's five times more prevalent.

The problem is, as I told you, if the mass is ranging from an asteroid to far lighter than an electron, if you get on an experiment that looks at one little range of mass, maybe you weren't the lucky guy that measured the right place, you know? And that's one of the reasons why, as fascinating as I think it is, I'm not doing dark matter experiments — because if you make an experiment that searches one mass range, it'll be blind to another mass range.

So what you need is many groups doing all sorts of radically different experiments, exploring all sorts of parameter space. And with all that said, until you see it, there still is the possibility that maybe we don't understand gravity or inertia, right? You know, you can't rule that out.

If there is dark matter out there, you're hoping it's actually somehow detectable.

I mean, I don't know what it is. I think it's cool. It's very, very fascinating. That is one thing I really do hope in my lifetime is understood, because I'd like to know the answer to that. And that's the thing that you could legitimately see a discovery of.

You got to get lucky though. I mean, you got to look in the right place, whatever it is.

Just imagine — or you have to come up with that really cool theoretical idea that everybody's overlooked, which is another possibility.

And there are people who are really, really religiously hating dark matter, largely because we've looked so hard for so many years, and the experiments in today's world are a million times more sensitive than when I was a starting student, and they still haven't seen anything. And that's why people really hate dark matter. I mean, some of them, because they think we should have seen it by now. But, you know, I don't know.

对"直接观测"的渴望

I mean, I'm a sucker for direct observation. Not — indirect is obviously also really great, but direct. Just imagine pointing your telescope in a certain direction and, because of some artifact of cosmology, being able to directly detect a giant amount of a thing that you could say is dark matter.

Yeah.

You would see it — orbit things, orbit it, or it would eclipse things in front of it, or —

Yeah.

— like in an obvious way. 'Cuz some of the stuff you mentioned with DF2 and DF4, those are like brilliant indirect deductions that there should be something like dark matter. But some obvious — yeah, blocking, occluding, this kind of thing.

We did that in the '90s — experiments called MACHO, OGLE, and some others. They looked for a black hole that you just can't see. You know, a black hole — you can't see it. It's a perfect candidate for dark matter. And if there's enough of them out there — remember, there's five times the number of stars, which means there's a whole lot of freaking black holes out there. We should have seen them. And we didn't.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个让人意外的视角：暗能量危机其实是"测量学的傲慢"
物理学最大的尴尬——10^120 倍的预测偏差——表面上是个计算问题，但深处藏着一个被忽略的元问题：**我们把"积分上限"当成了"现实的边界"**。Don Lincoln 不经意间说出的话很关键："如果在 LHC 能量尺度之下出现新物理，误差就只剩 10^60 倍。"换句话说，那个臭名昭著的数字根本不是宇宙的属性，而是**我们对自己无知的厚度的精确测量**。每次我们把积分推到普朗克尺度，我们不是在计算真空能，而是在计算"我们假装自己懂物理一直到那个尺度"的代价。这和金融里的"模型风险"惊人地同构——华尔街的衍生品定价模型在 2008 年崩盘，不是因为模型错了，而是因为把模型外推到了它从未被验证过的区域。
## 跨领域连接：暗能量与生物形态发生的"密度守恒"之谜
视频里最容易被读者错过的瞬间，是他强调"暗能量是密度，而不是总量"——空间扩张时，新的暗能量在凭空"出现"。如果空间是量子化的，那么每个新出现的空间量子都自带一份能量。**这个图景与发育生物学里的"细胞增殖中的形态原守恒"惊人相似**：胚胎中某些信号分子的浓度在细

item.title: [Lex Clips] The most shocking discovery in physics: Speed of Light from Electricity and Magnetism | Don Lincoln
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被忽略的认知颠覆

麦克斯韦方程组真正震撼的地方，并不是"推导出了光速"——而是它第一次让人类用纯粹的纸笔预言了一个本不该被预言的物理常数。在此之前，光速是测出来的；之后，光速是"算出来的"。这意味着宇宙中存在某种自指结构：电与磁这两个看似与"光"毫无关系的现象，在数学上被迫生出一个等于光速的常量。这是物理学第一次出现"数学先于实验"的反向因果，是后来爱因斯坦、狄拉克整个理论物理传统的祖先事件。换句话说，1860年代真正诞生的不是电磁学，而是"理论可以领先于现实"这种世界观。

跨领域的隐藏连接：电磁学与货币体系

Don Lincoln 强调"摆弄火花和磁铁"如何最终塑造了技术社会，但还有一个更隐蔽的联系值得指出：现代金融体系本质上也是一个电磁现象。SWIFT、央行清算、加密货币、HFT，全部依赖电磁波在光纤与铜缆中的传播极限。也就是说，麦克斯韦方程组里那个 $c$，不仅是宇宙的速度上限，也是全球资本流动速度的硬性上限——纽约到伦敦的套利窗口，本质上是被光速决定的。一个 19 世纪苏格兰人写下的偏微分方程，今天在限制着对冲基金能赚多少钱。这是"基础研究的下游应用"最被低估的一种形式：它不只塑造了技术，它塑造了价格。

出人意料的观察：他的"肥皂箱演讲"暴露了一个更深的悖论

Lincoln 用"过去的基础研究造就今天的技术"为今天的基础研究辩护——但这套论证其实是幸存者偏差。我们记住了麦克斯韦、记住了卢瑟福，是因为他们的"无用研究"恰好爆发了。被遗忘的是 19 世纪那些研究以太涡旋、磁单极、地球电流的科学家，他们同样"在挖掘自然深处"，却没有任何下游。所以真正的洞见不是"基础研究终将有用"，而是：基础研究的价值在于构造一个高方差的赌局，社会需要为这种方差买单，而不是为单个赌注的命中率买单。这与风险投资、生物进化、甚至神经网络训练中的"探索-利用权衡"是同一个数学结构——人类文明在认知层面上运行的是一种蒙特卡洛搜索，而麦克斯韦只是被采样到的一个高回报节点。

一个埋藏的隐喻

注意 Lincoln 在结尾的微妙跳跃：从"电磁学造就技术社会"直接跳到"核物理或许造就人类未来的能源"。他没说出口的潜台词是——人类每一次能量层级的跃迁，都对应着对物质更深一层的理解。化学（电子层）→ 蒸汽与电力；原子核（核子层）→ 核能；夸克层 → ？这是一个未完成的递归。如果模式延续，那么今天看似"无用"的强相互作用与希格斯研究，可能正在为某个 22 世纪的能源范式埋下数学种子。基础物理学的真正节奏，是每一层"无用"恰好是下一层文明的电池。

📝 转录文本

麦克斯韦方程与光速的浮现

So electromagnetism — 1860 or 70 — uh, the wonderful thing about that is: if you take Maxwell's equations and you apply a little bit of calculus, it's very easy to see that the laws of electricity and the laws of magnetism combined together make what's called a wave equation, which shows that these electric and magnetic fields oscillate. They vary. And if you have something that's varied, that's a wave. And the wave then moves.

And if you do the math, you find out that the speed at which these waves move is the speed of light. And so people said, "Wow, the speed of light comes out of those equations." And that had to be, I think, very persuasive.

电磁学与物质的根基

And of course, electromagnetism also plays a really significant role in chemistry, because after all, atoms are held together by electromagnetic forces. There's more to how atoms work — there is all the quantum mechanics stuff. But if you did not have electromagnetism, or if electromagnetism was very different, then atoms would be very different. So it plays a very big role in holding us together.

So it's a staggering advance in science to have a good behavior on that. And of course, being able to tame electromagnetism is why people can hear you when you do your podcast — through the miracles of the internet, or just electricity running the computers.

基础研究的辩护——一段肥皂箱演讲

I mean, this is a case, if I can get on a small soap box, where people back then said, "Well, why are you messing around with magnets and sparks and who cares?" Well, that very fundamental digging into the laws of nature has spin-offs. And it has spin-offs.

One of the big spin-offs is our entire technological society. Without being able to govern electricity, we'd still be farmers and shoemakers in cities, but we certainly would not have everything that we do.

So, off my soap box — but it's really a lovely thing to show how this digging into deep, fundamental, not understood, mysterious things can, 100 or 200 years later, transform the world.

今天的"无用"研究——夸克与原子核

And the type of science I do now, people often ask, "Well, what good is knowing about how the inside of atoms work, how the inside of quarks work?" And I don't know the answer to that.

Um, but just being a little more pragmatic: if I go back, say, a hundred years, where people were trying to understand how the protons and neutrons inside atoms held together, how they split, how you could combine them and so forth — this has led to nuclear power.

核能与人类的能源选择

Now, whatever you think about nuclear power — and some people like it and some people don't — but it is powerful. It will generate energy for humanity, and it may be that is the path that we take as we move away from digging fossil fuels out of the ground.

Humanity is going to need power no matter what. Nobody is going to go back to the way things were in the 1700s. And one enormous source of energy that is there for us to take, if we so choose, is the modification of the nucleus of atoms — which seem to have absolutely nothing to do with anything. And yet it provides humanity with an opportunity, which of course requires that we think carefully of how we do that, and if we want to. But it gives us something that we didn't have before.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Lex Clips] Why anything exists at all: The great antimatter mystery of our universe | Don Lincoln
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最颠覆的洞见：我们整个宇宙是一场"四舍五入误差"的残渣

请注意那个数字的荒谬性——十亿零一 对 十亿。这不是"略多一点"的差异，而是物理学家所能想象的最薄的不对称。换句话说：宇宙在大爆炸后的瞬间进行了一次大规模"互相湮灭"，99.9999999% 的物质都消失了，剩下那 0.0000001% 的"误差残渣"——构成了星系、地球、你的身体，以及此刻在思考这段话的神经元。

这意味着一个令人毛骨悚然的事实："存在"本身在统计学上是一个 bug，不是 feature。如果当时的对称性是完美的，宇宙将只是一片光子的海洋——纯粹的辐射，没有任何"东西"。我们之所以能问"为什么有东西存在"这个问题，恰恰是因为某个未知机制犯了一个极其微小的错误。

二、跨领域连接：中微子振荡 = 量子层面的"性别认同流动性"

林肯描述的中微子身份变化（猫→美洲豹→老虎→又变回猫）其实揭示了一个远超物理学的范式：

  • 在生物学中，这对应于表观遗传学——同样的 DNA 在不同环境下"振荡"出不同的表达；
  • 在金融市场中，这对应于资产类别之间的相变——同一笔资本时而表现为"避险"，时而表现为"风险"；
  • 在语言学中，这对应于一个词在不同语境中的语义振荡（如英语"literally"在过去 30 年内的意义流转）。

物理学家正在赌的是：当一个事物在"身份之间"振荡时，它的"正"版本和"反"版本可能以略微不同的节奏振荡。这是一个深刻的隐喻——任何一个具有"对偶身份"的系统，其对偶的两端在动态过程中可能并不严格镜像。这或许能解释为什么市场里的"多头情绪"和"空头情绪"在转换速度上从来不对称（恐慌总是快于贪婪）。

三、出人意料的元观察：科学的"反向勇气"

注意林肯说的那句话——"如果你不困惑，你就没在做你的工作。" 这句话颠覆了我们对专业能力的常规理解。

在几乎所有其他领域（医生、律师、工程师、咨询师），"确定性"是专业能力的标志——客户付钱买的就是你的笃定。但在前沿物理学中，专业能力等价于持续地、深刻地、有结构地困惑。

这里藏着一个更深的悖论：林肯赌中微子会以"相同速率"振荡，但他正在投入数十亿美元和数十年时间去做这个他认为大概率失败的实验。这是一种极其反常的认知姿态——"我赌它不存在，所以我必须去找它"。

这种姿态在哲学上对应于神学中的"否定之路"（via negativa）——通过排除"不是什么"来逼近"是什么"。在投资界，这对应于塔勒布的"反脆弱"逻辑——下注于自己最不确信的事。物质宇宙的起源之谜，或许只能用一种反直觉的认识论才能解开：最重要的真相，藏在我们最不愿相信但又必须去验证的地方。

📝 转录文本

一、谜题的提出：缺失的反物质

One of the big mysteries with antimatter is the bigger why. Where is the antimatter that should kind of be there? If the whole idea is that anytime you generate matter, you generate the same amount of antimatter. And yet when we look out into the observable universe, it seems like there's not antimatter for the most part there.

So what do we understand about this mystery? What are the possible explanations as to why?

二、爱因斯坦的两个论断与"重子生成"

So there's this thing called um biogenesis, and—as you say, so reiterating a little bit what you just said—um these are both Einstein things.

Einstein says that when you take energy, you make matter and antimatter in equal quantities. And Einstein says after the big bang there was a lot of energy in the universe, which should have made matter and antimatter. We only see matter. Where'd the antimatter go?

And the answer is: we don't know. However, there are some ideas, and there's a lot of thinking on it. And um, in fact, for me, it's doing an experiment right now with neutrinos, trying to better understand what it was that made the matter and antimatter not be the same.

三、那个惊人的"十亿零一比十亿"

Now, we do have a measurement of how much different it should be. And it's kind of neat. We can do this by counting the number of protons in the universe—just looking at galaxies and so forth. And then we can look at the cosmic microwave background, which is sort of the aftermath of the big bang. And we can count the number of photons from the cosmic microwave background.

And with a little bit of math, what we can do is we can then say that somehow in the early universe, something made a very, very tiny asymmetry. So that for every billion billion—with a B—antimatter particles that existed in the universe, there were a billion and one matter particles.

Mhm.

The billions canceled, annihilated, destroyed each other, and that extra one that's left over is us.

Mhm.

四、可能的解释：从原初不对称到重子生成

And so what physics mechanism made that ever so slight asymmetry is not understood. There are some thoughts.

One thought is, uh, that—well, it's just how it was when the universe was formed. There was an asymmetry. It was not made by matter for an antimatter.

Another possibility is, um, there are various numbers of theories all under the word "baryogenesis"—and "baryo" coming from the word "baryon," which basically means protons, and "genesis" meaning the creation of. And we'd say that simply because the protons are the heaviest particles, and so baryogenesis is just the creation of matter.

And there are just a number of theories in quantum mechanics that say that matter and antimatter can oscillate back and forth into one another. And there is a slight asymmetry in how that happens. And we know that this is true to a degree. Um, we've measured it in the 1960s with a different form of matter—I mean, you know, not protons, but a type of ephemeral matter that only exists in particle accelerators. And so we know that there is a slight difference between matter and antimatter, but it's not enough. If it doesn't explain that, we're not sure.

五、Fermilab 的新思路：轻子生成（Leptogenesis）

So at Fermilab, we have this idea which kind of turns things on its head, and it's not baryogenesis, it's leptogenesis. So leptons are the electrons, and because Fermilab is currently the world's most powerful neutrino accelerator, and neutrinos are leptons.

There is this idea—now, leptogenesis is incredibly complicated, but the idea is that it is possible. We know that neutrinos actually change their identity. There are three different types of neutrinos, like uh, I don't know, cats and jaguars and tigers. And if you have a beam of just cats, if you go along a little while, you find there's cats and jaguars and then tigers, and then they'll be back to all cats again.

And so this oscillation thing is called neutrino oscillation. And we've known it's been true since 1998.

六、正在进行的实验与一场国际竞赛

And what we are studying is: we're going to make a beam of neutrinos and another beam of antimatter neutrinos. And we're going to study the oscillation behavior of the two of them. And it is possible—it is unlikely, but it is possible—that the two of them will oscillate at slightly different rates.

Mhm.

And if the neutrinos oscillate at slightly different rates, then that, along with several other highly improbable things, can tie together and might explain why there is more matter in the universe.

So if I was going to bet the farm, I'll bet that they oscillate at the same rate. But I don't know, and you don't know till you do the measurements. So that's what we're doing.

There are some other uh experiments trying to measure it right now. So there's a big race between the Fermilab group and another group in Japan to see who gets there first and make this measurement, and we will find out.

If it turns out though that there is a difference in this oscillation rate between matter and antimatter, it will be a huge clue in this very, very difficult puzzle.

七、科学家的诚实：困惑是工作的一部分

I wish I could tell you I knew what the answer is, but literally nobody knows. I mean, and that's the thing of being a research scientist like me: if you're not confused, you're not doing your job.

So, there is this desperate—or not desperate—exciting search for this tiny asymmetry.

Yes.

It's so, so crazy to think that everything we see around us is a result of this tiny asymmetry, that there was this gigantic annihilation of matter and antimatter in the early universe.

And this is just some little accident.

Yeah. Yeah. That's crazy.

It's a happy accident. That is just—I mean, it's totally crazy.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [All-In Podcast] Pope vs AI, Anthropic's Digital God, AI Job Loss Narrative Flips, Open Source Crackdown Coming?
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、教皇与硅谷投资人的"反常共鸣"：当神学预警与博弈论得出同一结论

最让人意外的不是教皇 Leo XIV 写了一本 235 页的 AI 通谕，而是 Bill Gurley 这位最世俗的风投老兵，通过纯粹的博弈论推演，得出了和教皇几乎一模一样的结论——只不过一个用拉丁文，一个用 GTO（Game Theory Optimal）。

教皇说"AI 不是中性的，它会染上建造者、出资者和控制者的特征"；Gurley 说"想要造不可被剥削的超级 AI，最好的策略就是把三四个对手关进同一个房间，然后用技术不对称碾压他们"。这是两套语言、一个观察：当少数人掌握认知工具时，权力就会以你无法察觉的方式重新分配。

更微妙的是 Sacks 引用的那句拉丁文 quis custodiat ipsos custodes（谁来守卫守卫者）——这恰好是教皇的母语在反过来质问硅谷：你们说要"AI Safety"，那 Safety 这个词的定义权归谁？这是个 2400 年前苏格拉底就提出、但直到今天才有商业紧迫性的问题。

二、隐藏的递归陷阱：Anthropic 正在变成它声称要防止的东西

这是这期节目里最未被点破的悖论：Anthropic 通过强调"安全"获得知识分子精英的光环 → 光环转化为监管影响力 → 监管又转化为竞争壁垒 → 最终形成寡头垄断 → 而寡头垄断正是教皇通谕里点名的最大风险。

换句话说：它用"防止集中"的语言完成集中。这套打法你在哪里见过？

ESG 投资。烟草公司的"减害"叙事。1990 年代微软的"用户自由"营销。道德信号作为护城河——这是一种被反复使用却很少被命名的商业模式。

而 Dario 那首被引用的诗——"machines of loving grace"——用田园牧歌的修辞掩盖了一个具体的权力主张：由 AI 决定人类的资源分配。这不是科幻，这是 Skinner 行为主义在硅谷的转世。教皇看穿这一点用了一本书；Chamath 看穿用了一句话："他们不是在写软件，他们是在接生一个神。"

三、跨领域连接：从"安静辞职"到 AI 悲观主义的同构性

Gurley 在前半段提到 Gallup 调查——59% 的人对自己的工作"无感"，他称之为"quiet quitters"。这个数据看似与 AI 无关，但这才是真正的解释变量。

注意这个隐藏的逻辑链：对自己工作不投入的人，把这种内在的无力感投射成对 AI 的恐惧。他们不是在害怕 AI 抢走工作，他们是在害怕自己本来就没有真正占有过那份工作。AI 只是让这个真相提前显形。

这就解释了为什么 Sacks 和 J Cal 永远吵不出结论——他们在用同一个词谈两件事。Sacks 在谈有 agency 的劳动者（他们用 AI 放大自己），J Cal 在谈被动的劳动者（他们被 AI 替代）。这不是数据之争，是人类学之争。

四、最惊人的预测：Apple 才是这场战争的暗马

Jason 提出的"intelligence sovereignty"（智能主权）概念被快速带过，但这可能是整集最被低估的洞见。

逻辑是这样的：隐私（privacy）= 你看不到我的数据；智能主权 = 你不能用你的 AI 决定我如何理解世界。这是一个从"数据"上升到"诠释权"的范式跃迁。

而 Apple 是唯一一家硬件 + 操作系统 + 原则性隐私立场三位一体的公司。当 M5 芯片、128GB 统一内存、本地运行的开源权重模型这三者交汇时，Apple 会突然发现自己手握了对抗中心化 AI 的唯一可行架构——而它甚至不需要赢得模型竞赛。

这与一个完全不相关的领域形成镜像：1970 年代美国的"光盘加密"之争最终不是被法庭裁决的，而是被消费级硬件的廉价化裁决的。技术主权问题，从来都是被基础设施而不是法律解决的。

五、那个 5 亿美元 Claude 账单的真正含义

节目结尾抛出的"某公司一个月花了 5 亿美元在 Claude tokens 上"——所有人都笑了，但没人指出这背后的商业模型：

这套定价策略（$20/月无限用 → 触发上限 → 用信用卡续费）与三个看似无关的行业完全同构： - 赌场的"comped drinks"（先免费让你上瘾） - 早期自来水公司的"前 10,000 加仑免费"（基础设施成瘾化） - 19 世纪鸦片贸易的初始让利（建立路径依赖）

Frontier labs 不是在卖软件，他们在贩售对智能的渴求，而这种渴求一旦在组织内部形成路径依赖，价格弹性就消失了。这才是为什么 Sacks 说"token efficiency 会成为未来一年的主题"——这不是技术议题，是戒断议题。

And what they came up with is we have to have the guardians guard against each other. And so they came up with the idea of separation of powers. We'd have separation of federal and state. We'd have the three branches of the government, even within the legislative branch, it was a bicameral legislature. This is the power of compounding, right? Is just do the math on it. 10 times 10 is 100. 3 times 3 is 9. Also, there may not be enough compute to support that kind of growth. So go check that out if you want to reskill. Generation tool belt.

Yeah. I think it's better than you know, having the government fix things. And then, you know, as we started and talked about, I've got a new grant program myself to help people kind of tilt their career in a different direction. Go do something you love and apply, and maybe I can help fund you moving in that direction.

And as to your vibe shift, I think it's because, candidly, people's houses have been Molotov cocktailed because they're doomerism, and people specifically are citing that when they shoot at their houses, and throw mullets of cocktails at them twice in the same week. And if you're IPO-ing and you're coming out saying, hey, jobs are going away, jobs are going away, that's just a really bad look.

And or it's because we called it out and they got caught. And so now they're telling the truth.

Closing: Shoutout to Tulsi Gabbard's Husband

All right, everybody. This has been another amazing episode of the All In Podcast. Thanks for coming, Bill Gurley.

No, no, no. Sorry, I need to do one thing just doing at the end of this. Oh, yes. She's a friend of ours. I just want to just give a huge shout out to Tulsi Gabbard and specifically her husband, Abraham. Oh, he is tragic. Going through some really tough stuff with cancer. He is going to kick its ass. I just wanted to say we love you. Yeah. Tulsi's great. She's amazing.

That's episode 275 in the can. We'll see you next time. Bye-bye. Love you, boys.

We'll let your winners ride. Rain Man David Sackett. and they've just gone crazy with it. Love you, Westies. I'm going all in. What your winners lie? What your winners lie? What your winners lie? Besties are gone. That's my dog taking an audition to drive what he said. Oh, man. My habbitasher will meet me at Blake's house. We should all just get a room and just have one big huge orgy because they're all just useless. It's like this sexual tension that they just need to release somehow. The jerky's are back.

item.description:
## 一、教皇与硅谷投资人的"反常共鸣"：当神学预警与博弈论得出同一结论
最让人意外的不是教皇 Leo XIV 写了一本 235 页的 AI 通谕，而是 **Bill Gurley 这位最世俗的风投老兵，通过纯粹的博弈论推演，得出了和教皇几乎一模一样的结论**——只不过一个用拉丁文，一个用 GTO（Game Theory Optimal）。
教皇说"AI 不是中性的，它会染上建造者、出资者和控制者的特征"；Gurley 说"想要造不可被剥削的超级 AI，最好的策略就是把三四个对手关进同一个房间，然后用技术不对称碾压他们"。这是**两套语言、一个观察**：当少数人掌握认知工具时，权力就会以你无法察觉的方式重新分配。
更微妙的是 Sacks 引用的那句拉丁文 *quis custodiat ipsos custodes*（谁来守卫守卫者）——这恰好是教皇的母语在反过来质问硅谷：你们说要"AI Safety"，那 Safety 这个词的定义权归谁？这是个 **2400 年前苏格拉底就提出、但直到今天才有商业紧迫性**的问题。
## 二、隐藏的递归陷阱：Anthropic 正在变成它声

item.title: [Alan Chen加州生活号] NVDA尾盘下杀估值比标普白菜还便宜？SNOW财报爆涨40%引发板块大轮动！月末调仓真相 | NVDA GOOGL AVGO INTEL CRWD PANW IREN NBIS ANET
item.dc:creator: Alan Chen加州生活号
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：这次轮动的"错位逻辑"

表面上市场在说"资金从硬件流向软件"，但仔细看Alan提到的一个细节藏着真相——NVDA的估值现在已经低于SPY（标普500的整体估值）。这意味着真正被砍的不是"贵的"，而是"被认为还能涨的"。市场在做一件极其反常的事：用估值最便宜的龙头去补贴估值更贵的二线芯片股的rebalancing。

这像极了一个心理学现象——"锚定效应的报复"：当一只股票长期被视为"领跑者"，它的下跌反而被解读成"理性回归"，而真正应该回归的高估值同业反而因为"还没涨够"被资金追捧。这是月末rebalancing机制里一个少有人注意的暗面：机械的权重调整正在惩罚最优秀的资产。

二、跨领域连接：软件业的"商业模式相变"与生物学中的寒武纪大爆发

Alan提到一句话特别值得放大——"现在大家都在烧token，一两个月把一年的budget烧完了"。这不是简单的成本问题，而是整个SaaS行业正在经历计价单位的相变：从seat-based（按席位）→ consumption-based（按用量）→ 最终很可能走向value-based（按价值）。

这个演化路径与生物学上的寒武纪大爆发惊人相似：当环境（AI算力供给）突变时，旧的商业形态（按人头收钱）就像浅海软体动物——突然失去了生态位。Snowflake的"40%涨"和Oracle的"10%涨"不是简单的资金轮动，而是市场在给那些已经长出"硬壳"（消费计价 + 数据壁垒）的物种打高分。

而真正会让人意外的推论是：未来软件业的赢家可能不是技术最好的，而是最早建立"AI价值度量体系"的公司——谁能向客户证明"我为你创造了X美元的价值"，谁就能跳过烧token的死亡螺旋。这是一场会计学革命，不是技术革命。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：SpaceX IPO是一场"流动性虫洞"

Alan提到邻居那帮"鬼精的基金经理"在2.5T估值上排队买SpaceX——这个细节比SpaceX本身更值得玩味。如果一笔IPO能吸走整个航天概念板块的资金（除Rocket Lab外），那它实际上充当了资本市场的虫洞：把分散在小盘股的"叙事溢价"全部吸进一个超级单点。

这里有个让人后背发凉的连接：RKLB、IRON、NBIS、CRWV这些股票的命运，其实由一个尚未上市的私募估值决定。这违背了"价格发现"的基本逻辑——一级市场的隐含估值正在反向定价二级市场。

更深的系统性观察是：当散户在追"轮动"、机构在埋伏"IPO虫洞"、量子和核电板块在玩"政治叙事"时，三条资金时间线已经错位。Alan担心的"夏季大跌"可能不会以传统形态出现，而是以"叙事用尽症"——当SpaceX IPO一旦落地，整个市场会突然发现自己没有下一个故事可讲。这才是lockout rally的真正风险：不是估值崩盘，而是想象力崩盘。

📝 转录文本

开场 & 近况

Hello everyone, we had some things to do in the past two weeks, so we didn't have time to record a video together. Today, I will continue to talk about US stocks with you.

NVDA 尾盘下杀 & 估值观察

Let's pay attention to today's Yen. Yen has been doing well during the day. Yesterday was also a breakthrough of a downward trend line, and today it has come all the way to 217. We have been paying attention to Yen since the financial report, and we think its valuation is very attractive.

We are still on Twitter, constantly browsing the news. We can't find any news on Twitter. All the memes are like "What the fuck, what the fuck?" 现在都是猜测，有可能是它的一个新的 GPU 那些，但是那个消息三个多小时前出来了。It's a bit late to see the reaction. It may also be the rebalancing of the end of the month.

But to be honest, we've seen the valuation of Nvidia in this quarter. It's lower than SPY. SPY was around 22.

月末调仓与芯片板块的反常轮动

Broadcom AVGO is a forward-facing program. So I think it should be a kind of chip rebalancing. Today is the end of the month. Maybe the market is doing some, I don't know, just balance again. Maybe there's not too much over-discipline, but it's still quite interesting to observe.

It's important, but I find it a bit strange that other chips are more expensive than Nvidia's. And when you rebalance it, you have to rebalance the Nvidia chip. It's quite counter-standard. It's a bit strange. It's like someone made a mistake. Yeah, it's amazing.

Intel is also a small market leader after the rise of the market. It also fell today.

资金从硬件流向软件：SNOW 财报点燃板块

We can see that there is a clear phenomenon this week. The money from the chip hardware has gone to software. This week, a huge bubble came from Snow's financial report. This day, the number of players rose 40%, and today it rose 67%.

Then we saw Oracle and Microsoft. In early May, it was constantly reminded in the social media. Today, Oracle has also increased by 10%. These are all software.

I don't know if it's a flow of funds, right? In fact, Oracle is another track. It's not entirely software. I personally don't particularly like its basic face. The main thing is that it borrows too much money. Yes, it's too much open-ended.

比如说现在软件也只是一部分软件便宜而已，如果你说像网络安全这种，你看 IGP 是刚刚站上去，所以它也只是一部分的那些软件便宜。而且有些网络安全已经是 all time high，那些是不便宜。所以资金重新回来这些 beat down software stock，再看一下有没有什么机会来找点便宜，这个也是有可能。

毕竟现在它进了出来的财报，It doesn't show that the software has been killed by AI. Now they are making more active changes to adapt to the age of AI.

AI 时代的软件商业模式重构

My personal idea is that if you want to survive in the age of AI, like these companies, you have to change your business model. 改成为适合 AI 时代的生意模式，就不要再用什么加上流量啊这种做法。

但是现在也没有很明确的什么样的生意模式才最合适 AI 的，就是流量的似乎也不大行，因为现在大家都在烧 token，发现一两个月把一年的 token budget 都给它烧掉了，这个好像也不大行。

所以 I feel like it's still a bit too early, but we can see that most software companies are actively seeking for changes from the previous seed base to the common market flow base. I think it is very likely that there will eventually be a value base, that is, how much value it will create for you, and then it will collect some of it. This is quite possible.

但我们也可以看到 from semiconductor, it's already very expensive. How can you squeeze money? Semiconductors are very expensive. So this is also quite normal.

If you are now rushing to, especially the semiconductor outside of NVIDIA, and then you are suddenly smashed, the bamboo shoots are cut off, and then you are thinking, why is it me? Why is my market unfair? But think about it, in a semiconductor system, it is already a very crowded track, and you are still squeezing in. It's also quite normal to be washed down.

健康的板块轮动 & 关键技术位

Yes, I think this is a relatively healthy round of the board. Like we are in May, this is Discord. We just broke through 198 in May. It has already been announced, including Microsoft. It was still around 403, 407. It was on May 14th.

And then you can see that they have successfully taken many forms. Including Microsoft has reached 450 this year. Oracle also came from the 190th to the 225th today. These are all representative, including Snow and Now. They are all software-based. It was driven by AI for almost half a year. Now there is finally a bit of a comeback.

The entire software stock, you can see that this is some of the important intelligence and resistance drawn before. After breaking through the black resistance, it is now up to the 200-day military line above. This is also a theme that is resounding.

We have been emphasizing this software stock since April and May. This money goes back to it here. We can take a look at this. It is such a ratio of iSMH to iGV. iGV to iSMH. This is where it is. We can see that the money is slowly starting to form a trend at the bottom. From the software stock to the chip stock, today it is also moving upward.

It's not that the chip stock is completely finished, but in the short term, there may be a trend change. After the change, if the value of the chip stock slowly returns to a reasonable range, I guess money will continue to chase.

CrowdStrike 仓位回顾 & 财报前的抉择

In addition, our big market share, right? We remember the last time we added more than 390,000. Now it's almost three times.

Let's take a look at our cross-strike. The average price is 399 yuan. The current profit is already 710 yuan. Personally, do you think there is still some room for us to talk about the basics? Why can we continue this? This is a month of basically no return on the market. Is there any basic change?

In CrowdStrike, we released a report on the community last week. We carefully analyzed the stock. It combined the entire software version and the network security version of the industry report. It basically looks at the 7-story building in the AI era. We talked about the 7-story building in the previous software eye report. If you are not familiar with it, you can check it out in our community.

Basically, it is full of 5-story buildings. So in the era of AI, it is not an attacker, but a very big beneficiary.

When you see our video, now I think, can I still buy it now? Can I still buy it now? Can I still rush in now? This question, I personally think it is too expensive now. It will be reported soon. And next week, Palo Alto Network.

If it is removed, it will definitely bring down the cross-strike. So today we reminded the community that next Monday is the power to network to report. Now there is more than 70% profit, so you have to consider how to deal with it. Because if the financial report is not good, it will definitely bring it down.

Of course, if we say that, it is also a great risk. After all, the financial report is all gambling. If the financial report is really 5% more than perfect, then the company will pull it up to 800 yuan, then we will be called a curse. So this kind of decision, you have to do it yourself. Because no one knows what the financial report will be like.

Yes, it's hard to say whether it will go after it or not. But the 399 buy is the real-time trading signal that our community members have seen. Not only...

Look at its current valuation. It should be nearly 160 times the valuation. I'll quickly check it out. CrowdStrike is now at 151. Very expensive stock. It's basically... I think it's close to the stock before it was listed. It was at a very high growth rate. It's the highest stock in the last few years since 2022. So it needs to have a very high growth rate to be worth the stock. Otherwise, the market will blow it down.

And then Palo Alto Network OKTA? OK. We haven't had time to do OKTA, but we still got OKTA.

牛市的板块迁徙 & FOMO 情绪

I can feel the momentum of the bull market. From one block to another block. DD OG is doing well today. 10%. It seems that... The FOMO mood is strong.

Yes. Because everyone thinks that the first wave didn't catch the chip. Where should we go next? Go to software.

And then, including the previous period, I found that Trump was shouting something, looking for something, including Dell computers. Although we didn't directly eat the food, we did the HPE in the community report. We also ate with him. And HPE will do the financial report next week. Before the financial report, it has increased by more than 10% with DAER.

We talked about this ticket at the community meeting at the beginning of the 30th. We have shares in the M1 strong account. In the data center's Pi, we have shares.

量子计算 & 题材股盛宴

Yes, but you can see that the hardware and software are all developed now. Including the quantum computing that Trump mentioned. We bought INFQ today, and then there are other R, These names are all very cheesy. RTGI, and also QUBT. I can't remember. Anyway, there are many hot topics in quantum.

Including the day before yesterday, the day before Jones, right? ONDS. This was also on Tuesday, when we were doing the video conference. There were a lot of money that was flowing very excitedly. And then, the whole share price is also good. The share price is also good.

I can feel that the market is still more active in looking for these next possible market moves. I didn't see many people think that the market is selling in May. But it's June next week. In June, many people said that the world cup was taken, including SpaceX IPO. There are all kinds of sayings.

SpaceX IPO：吸血还是引爆？

SpaceX will be able to... Do you think it will take a lot of money? Because I have a neighbor who bought SpaceX. Some people are in 1.5 trillion, some people are in 2 trillion. Now if you look at the gambling market, SpaceX is now 2.5 trillion.

In this situation, will the money still go in or will it wait? I personally think that the institutions have some amount to pay. I don't really understand. I personally think that if it goes up to 2.5T, it will be difficult to buy.

Yes, everyone thinks it will suck blood. I saw it now. At least last week, when I was having BBQ with my neighbors, those very cunning fund managers, they all thought that was a very rich investment. But when they went in, they invested 1.5 trillion. It sounds like a lot of money. They invested a lot of money, plus a little bit of data center, 2.5 trillion. That's ridiculous.

Will there really be people who will rush in like this? Don't become it will become a whole market down.

散户陷阱 & Lockout Rally 的隐忧

But you just talked about this kind of rapid circulation. Many ordinary retail investors are particularly easy to be caught. He looked at which stocks rose very high one day, then rush in. Then the next day his funds circulated to other small ones. He was caught in the small dollar. Then cut meat. Continuously to do this, continuously cut.

So now it looks like the market is very healthy. As long as you have circulation, not funds. The market is very healthy for the market to escape. But mainly some retail investors who don't have time to look at the market. It's easy to be caught up. Then the market rises and I lose money.

Then there is another one to say: Why do I think there will be a big sale in the summer? 整个上涨的过程都是一个 lockout rally。什么意思呢？你看它的交易量一直大盘上来，它的交易量 SPA 也好，QQQ 也好，交易量并不是特别大，所以参与的人并不是特别多。

在这种过程中，Yes, very interesting. Let me think. Yes, this is a temporary expectation.

SpaceX 概念股的"假繁荣"

Back to the topic of SpaceX, I personally think that many people say I think it's more likely that these other flight versions will be lost because of SpaceX. Either it's the value of the product or the news. Because they don't have any real business, including technology. They're just noisy because SpaceX is going to be lost. They don't have a more reasonable title to be noisy. So these are things to be careful about.

SpaceX is the flight version. Now, except for Rocket Lab, other Iron, Iris Energy.

IREN / NBIS / CRWV：数据中心三剑客

We sent a report after the financial report. This and IRON and MBIS. And Corwin. Yes, these three are the comparisons. Our favorite is MBIS and IRON. But since MBIS is already in the long-term market, and it's quite expensive now, we only have IRON.

For Coreweave, I personally don't like it. I don't touch it much because it's too much money. It's not the same as other ones. The market is different. We've talked about this in a video.

We said that we have more than 50 new stores and 40 new stores, right? Yes. Today, we have a lot of new stores. Let's see if we forgot to check. Let's check today.

基本面驱动的投资理念

So it will give you a few seasons like this. If you are particularly interested in this kind of basic-based driven investment or operation, you are welcome to help us. The community will take a look. Although we talked a lot about the technical side of the video before, in fact, many of our transactions are from the bottom-up. This is an analysis of the basic side, and then we will find a chance to enter the market through the technical side.

Arista Networks (ANET) 看多逻辑

Arista Network. We sent a message to A-Net right after the news came out. Arista Networks, Bullish. They gave us a bullish call and then built a warehouse.

I think it's a good idea to go back and make up for the gap. Then go to the high end. It's still a big hope. But this stock market has a limitation. I have to see it in the next two seasons. Must overdeliver. Because it has a sendback. That is, the guidance given by the previous financial report is a bit soft. Because the chip is a bit weak.

And this stock has a very subtle relationship with the competition between Invida and AMD in the CPU. It has a certain impact on it. But this relationship is a bit more complicated. It's hard to explain this in one go. But this stock is also a bullish one.

DigitalOcean & Agentic AI 受益股

And then there's DigitalOcean. Yes, it's a server. Yes, we think this stock is also bullish. You see, it has always been like this. Technical form, let's not talk about the basics first. After it was reported, the gap couldn't be touched, and the low point was getting higher and higher.

But now it's just a little bit, push it up, can't break through. That 160 should be about 160 or something, that place can't rush over.

But overall, we have passed through its past few quarter reports. We think this is a stock that has appeared to be a turning point, and in the next few years, the barrier is still quite deep, and not many people can threaten it. It has a very big benefit from the agentic AI agent.

We have actually built it once before in the 50s. At that time, it was the OpenCore. We said everyone raised lobsters. There are two beneficiaries of open lobster. One is Mac Mini, Apple's. This year is also very magical.

光模块 & 电力主题预告

And also, those who are interested in light modules, like Corning, we are very happy to see that there are some feedbacks these days. Yes, we have also done a deep talk about this. We have one minute left in this room, so we will leave this topic for the next time. We will discuss it in depth.

and Coral Heron, these are all very good labels. Let's see if we have a chance to upload them in the community.

We still have 30 seconds left. Someone in the community has suggested a version called "Electricity". We are also doing a deeper study on it. It will be released in the next video. We will also do a research on it.

收尾

Okay, that's it for today. If you are interested in this content, please give us a thumbs up and comment. And support us to continue to update every week. See you next time. Thank you everyone. Bye bye.

表面上市场在说"资金从硬件流向软件"，但仔细看Alan提到的一个细节藏着真相——**NVDA的估值现在已经低于SPY**（标普500的整体估值）。这意味着真正被砍的不是"贵的"，而是"被认为还能涨的"。市场在做一件极其反常的事：**用估值最便宜的龙头去补贴估值更贵的二线芯片股的rebalancing**。
这像极了一个心理学现象——**"锚定效应的报复"**：当一只股票长期被视为"领跑者"，它的下跌反而被解读成"理性回归"，而真正应该回归的高估值同业反而因为"还没涨够"被资金追捧。这是月末rebalancing机制里一个少有人注意的暗面：**机械的权重调整正在惩罚最优秀的资产**。
