I think one of the deepest adversarial thinking benches in the world is the Israeli ecosystem: 8200, Wiz, Armis, Island, Dennis O Group. What do you think the researchers, engineers, business people in the tech ecosystem outside of security — and then in the labs in particular — are missing about what needs to happen in security, and alignment, which is what you're talking about here?

What is really important when you're building security products in general — and I think what people in Israel have really good know-how on — is just understanding how security teams work. Because at the end of the day, no matter what is the technical problem you're solving, you're building a tool for people, for an organization. That organization has a certain structure. There are certain teams. There are certain flows of responsibilities, of information.

And creating a product for this audience that doesn't just solve the technical problem but they actually love — is really hard. You need to really care about the day-to-day of these different functions, and you need to have people in your ecosystem that have built products for them in the past, that know them like they know their best friend — like they know what they do when they step into the office in the morning, they drink their coffee, what are the systems they're opening, what is their boss wanting from them, what are their colleagues wanting from them, what are they going to get praised for, what are they going to get mad for. Then you need to take that and make it as your product.

And I think that's one of the really hard things that people in Israel learned, because they've had so much contact with these buyers and end users. And yeah, I would just encourage people to be much more curious about the day-to-day of security people. It's a cliché to say it, but these people are actually saving us daily from attackers stealing our money, taking our data, and they're kind of keeping our way of life as it is in this digital world.

So yeah, I think more love to security teams around the world.

十七、悖论：AGI-pilled 信徒为何还要服务"人类安全团队"？

I'm going to ask you to just square that with something else you've told me, Maxim, which is — you're the most AGI-pilled person I'm going to meet in Israel. Embedded in what you said is a belief that we will continue to have defensive security teams for some number of years. So you do believe that?

I do think that security teams are also going to become completely high-powered, and I do think that they're going to be run by AI agents — like everything else in the knowledge work space, I would say, in the near future.

But I do think that it's important to be grounded, and today when I sell a product, I sell it to a human audience with a few agents. And by the way, we also invest in making our systems very convenient for agents to use. And it's important that I focus on delivering an amazing experience today for people who buy the product today. And as that audience becomes more agents than humans, it will be important for us to evolve and to make it work really well for agents doing the work.

So I think the core principle is the same: we need to really be minded of who is the end user, what is their experience. For a human, it might be not overwhelming with too much information that is irrelevant. For an agent, it might be not wasting too many tokens in their context when we talk to them. Maybe it's the same thing, really. So I think it's important that we always remind ourselves who's using the system and what will be the best experience for them.

结束语

Awesome. Thanks so much for doing this, Maxim.

Appreciate it. Thank you very much.

Find us on Twitter at @NoPriorsPod. Subscribe to our YouTube channel if you want to see our faces. Follow the show on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, or wherever you listen — that way, you get a new episode every week. And sign up for emails or find transcripts for every episode at no-priors.com.

item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的悖论：安全的核心矛盾从"限权"变成了"放权"
传统企业安全的第一性原理是**最小权限原则**——不管系统多聪明，都只给它"刚好够用"的权限。但 Maxim 在访谈中无意间揭示了一个范式断裂：**人们购买 Claude Code 的根本动机，恰恰是希望它拥有"我自己的全部权限"，这样我们才能"交代它做事、去吃个午饭、回来看到事情已经完成"。**
这意味着 AI Agent 是历史上第一类**用户主动放弃权限边界**的软件。Identity Security、Endpoint Security、API Gateway 这套统治了企业安全二十年的体系，在这个时刻全部失效——不是因为它们技术落后，而是因为它们的**前提假设**（系统的权限应该被限制）被产品的核心价值主张（系统应该拥有人的全部权限）反转了。
Onyx 的真正赌注不是"做一个更好的代理监控工具"，而是**赌定整个企业安全栈的范式必须从"权限即边界"切换到"意图即边界"**——这是一次和当年从外网防火墙转向零信任同等量级的范式断裂，但极少有人意识到我们正处于这场断裂的第一年。
## 二、跨领域连接：闪电棋

item.title: [Bits + Bips] Steve Sosnick: The Macro Read — Bonds, the Fed, Equities, and Where Crypto Fits
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

三个让人意外的视角

1. 这场访谈最反常的细节：股市对"和平消息"已经审美疲劳，但债券和原油却仍在诚实地反应

Sosnick 提到的"棘轮效应"（ratchet effect）表面上是说股市单向上行，但真正的反常之处在于：同一条 Axios 新闻里，原油和债券收益率会"回吐"，而股票不回吐。这暴露了一个被忽视的事实——三个市场对"真相"的诚实度排序大致是：商品 > 国债 > 股票。Sosnick 自己也说"股票交易员排在队伍最后面"。

这其实是个跨市场的"认知带宽分层"现象：商品交易员只看供需，债券交易员看通胀预期，而股票交易员看的是叙事。叙事容易被"用尽"，但供需不会。这意味着，当你想判断一个地缘事件的真假，别看 SPX，去看 WTI 的尾盘动作。

2. 跨领域连接：Coinbase ETF 与"加密游客化"，本质是同一种"流动性民主化诅咒"

这里有一个被反复忽略的悖论：ETF 让 BTC 触达"normies"是 2024 年的最大利好，但同一机制现在正在变成最大利空。这和互联网泡沫时期的"宽带过剩"完全同构——Sosnick 自己也举了 Global Crossing 的例子，但他没把这条线串到加密上。

我想把它串起来： - 1999 年：铺光纤的人不是最后赢家，用光纤的人（Google/Meta）才是。 - 2024 年：买 IBIT 的人不是最后赢家，因为他们是"业绩追逐者"——上行时涌入，下行时夺路而逃。 - 真正的赢家很可能还没出现，就像 Google 是泡沫之后才诞生的一样。

也就是说，当前 BTC 的"低波动率"不是成熟的标志，而是叙事真空期的标志。九个月低点的 IV 在历史上往往不是顶，而是新故事尚未浮现的"叙事前夜"。

3. 一个出人意料的观察：Warsh 想换通胀基准这件事，比任何降息预期都重要

主流报道都聚焦"Warsh 鹰派还是鸽派"，但 Sosnick 提到了一个真正的暗线：Warsh 想把基准从 core PCE 换成 trimmed mean inflation。这件事被一笔带过，但它的杠杆效应是巨大的——

这不是货币政策调整，这是"测量学"的政变。换一把尺子，等于把"通胀目标 2%"的语义重写。中本聪当年决定 BTC 通胀率递减的逻辑，Warsh 现在在用相反的方向做：通过修改度量定义来改变政策空间。

这里有个跨领域的洞见：所有长期的政策博弈，最终都会从"参数博弈"退化为"度量博弈"。GDP 是怎么算的、失业率是 U3 还是 U6、通胀用 PCE 还是 CPI 还是 trimmed——谁掌握度量定义，谁就掌握了政策的合法性。这与加密世界里 stablecoin 用什么"挂钩参考"、L2 用什么"TPS 定义"、AI 公司用什么"benchmark"，是完全同构的权力斗争。

一个让你不舒服的预言

Sosnick 说"中期选举年的市场往往不好"（2018、2022 都跌了）。但他可能低估了一个更深的模式：新美联储主席 + 中期选举年 + 高 margin debt 占比 + IPO 巨潮——这四个变量同时叠加在历史上只出现过一次类似组合（1987 年前后）。

你不需要相信周期重演，但你要意识到：这个组合的"未知度"远高于市场定价。当前 VIX 与 BTC IV 同步压在低位，说明市场默认"四个变量互相独立"，但它们其实高度耦合——一个变量出问题，会通过流动性结构把其他三个一起拉下水。

这不是看空，这是在说：真正的风险不在任何单一变量，而在它们之间"未被定价的相关性"。

📝 转录文本

开场：去中心化 ≠ 无组织

we can be decentralized and organized at the same time.

We can have a system that is support to decentralized set of like — Nir is actually, whenever people join Nir, they were like, "holy shit, I didn't realize there's so many things." Like we have dozens of companies working together at all times. Like you go to our Slack, there's like 20 Slack connects that you effectively join by default. Because we actually have lots of different companies working together in close coordination.

When we did have — we had a hack as well on Nier with Ria. We had five different companies effectively working together. They tracked down exactly who did it. They had all the forensic evidence and effectively returned all the money. And it was done because all those teams can come together whenever there's a need and figure out how to work together.

And that is like, I see the function of leadership. And I mean, not perfect, obviously, there's a lot to improve, but that is a function of leadership of both, you know, the kind of spokesperson and the vision center of the ecosystem, right — which is Vitalik in Ethereum. And like, this is not going to change. And the foundation as a supporting function, like it doesn't need to go and do the actual day to day, even sales function, but they need to organize and set the framework for that.

与以太坊基金会的历史

So we have a long history with Ethereum because we actually pitched in 2018 to build these two, to Ethereum foundation. Because I actually, like, if we could just build this to make it work, we would go back to AI in like 2020. And because that was like the whole thing where like we needed a blockchain for our near AI stuff. And we're like, looked around, we didn't find anything that would actually match our needs, right? Scalable, easy to use, kind of abstracted.

And so we were like, oh, well, Ethereum is, you know, was talking about sharding back then, was talking about WebAssembly, was talking about faster slot time, was talking about better user experience. We're like, cool. WebAssembly. You don't hear that very much in Ethereum land these days. And so we're like, cool, we can just build all that. We have a team, we are VC funded. We just need an upside. That was the thing.

It's like, hey, we need a way... Whoa, whoa, whoa. Sorry, I'm going to stop you right there. I don't know if you're familiar with the concept of communism, but we're not paying you shit. You can build us these too, but we will not pay you a red cent, my friend. And so that was kind of where that conversation went.

Yeah. And to be clear, I've pitched this idea multiple times. So before launching Mainnet, I've pitched it again because like, hey, look, we've built it now. We had Baynet, we had sharding, we had WebAssembly. We had account abstraction. Let's figure out what this could look like. We do have VCs. We need to pay them money. I have a team that wants upside. They work 24-7 effectively.

Why would you have VCs? What were you thinking? I know. I feel like we've heard this before and they tried it out with Tomas and shit the bet. Now they're just like, let's just go back to what we know, which is like infinite gardening and funding longevity research and like doing a bunch of retarded stuff that nobody in the world gives a shit about other than Vitalik and his little cabal.

对 EF 的批评

I'm just going to say it — like the EF is completely out of touch. So, I mean, now that I've dunked on them a little bit, like I can also say that at least they're acknowledging that other organizations are going to have to step in, but then it becomes a question of whether or not they're actually going to like seed those reins to any other organization that is more competent and more in touch than they are.

Because they just starve. They're like funding hippos in Asia and all that. And like, you know, it's just stupid. Nobody cares about that. Like, it doesn't matter. Like, Ethereum is no longer a startup. It's now a mature and robust ecosystem. And like, you know, you can't really take the same liberties that we did six years ago, 10 years ago. Like, it's just completely different. There's like billions, trillions of dollars on the line here. And like people's livelihoods are dependent on that. There are people with families — or like my entire career has been on Ethereum. If it goes to shit, I mean, what am I going to do?

回到主题：去中心化 ≠ 无组织（重复段落）

And so I think like — so there's like fundamentally to me, there's few things. One is I don't think decentralization needs to mean disorganization. And so this is something I've told my team over and over again. We can be decentralized and organized at the same time, right?

We can have a system that is support a decentralized set of — like, Nir is actually, whenever people join Nir, they were like, "holy shit, I didn't realize there's so many things." Like we have dozens of companies working together at all times. Like you go to our Slack, there's like 20 Slack connects that you definitely join by default, right? Because we actually have lots of different companies working together in close coordination.

When we did have, we had a hack as well on Nir with Rhea. We had five different companies effectively working together. They tracked down exactly who did it. They had all the forensic evidence and effectively returned all the money. And it was done because all those teams can come together whenever there's a need and figure out how to work together.

And that is like, I see the function of leadership — and I mean, not perfect, obviously there's a lot to improve, but that is a function of leadership of both, you know, the kind of spokesperson and the vision setter of the ecosystem, right? Which is metallic.

节目开场：Steve Sosnick 登场

Hi, everyone. Welcome to another episode of Bits and Bips, the interview. My name is Steve Ehrlich. I am the head of research at Sharplink and also your host for today. We've got another terrific episode.

But before we begin, just a quick disclaimer — nothing that you hear on the show should be construed as investment or financial advice. For full disclosures, please see unchainedcrypto.com/bitsandbips. And then finally, before we get started, let's take a brief moment to hear from some of the sponsors who make the show possible.

All right. Welcome back. So today I have Steve Sosnick, the chief strategist at Interactive Brokers and a repeat guest on the show. Actually, Steve, I think you were my first guest on this show. So, yeah, so really thrilled to have you back. You're one of the best people I know when it comes to reading the tape and understanding the dynamics between TradFi, crypto, how macro forces are colliding with geopolitical uncertainty, et cetera.

And we've got a lot to talk about today. As we're sitting down, there's renewed tension in Iran, Strait of Hormuz, skirmishes going on between US and Iran. The Fed's preferred inflation indicator is the highest in years. Gold is dropping, equities are up, yields are teetering. So a lot to unpack. Thrilled to have you with us.

It's a pleasure to be here once again, Steve. I always enjoy our discussions and looking forward to this one.

股市为什么仍接近历史高点？——"棘轮效应"

Appreciate that. So let's dive right in. I kind of set the stage here, but equities are still like near all time highs. I mean, how do you make sense of all this?

Well, there's a few things going on and they're working in concert. I will say to some extent, we can thank good earnings. And that's always in my mind the best reason for a stock market to rally — the fact that in this past earnings season, we've seen a decent number, I would even say a significant number, of EPS beats and more importantly, positive guidance. To the case in some situations, let's say semiconductor memory stocks, the guidance has been extreme. We can argue whether some of that guidance has been extrapolated maybe a bit more, maybe a bit further into the future than it ought to be. But I will stipulate that there is a solid base behind this rally.

But the magnitude of it we can question. And to a certain extent, what we've noted over the past few weeks, let's say since the end of March, is what I've been calling the ratchet effect about news from the Persian Gulf. And by that I mean we seem to rally on each positive story about some sort of resolution to the situation, something that might reopen the strait.

And so far, as we're taping this — yeah, there was another positive story this morning coming out of Axios. But for the most part, basically we're all for however many there have been. I'm not sure. I lose count of how many there have been. And while oil and bonds tend to give back their declines — well, yields give back the declines and bond and oil futures give back the declines — when the stories don't come to pass, stocks haven't really given back anything. So that's why I call it like a ratchet. You know, you move in one direction.

And so that's been a big factor. And what I'm wrestling with right now as we're speaking is — stocks are reacting positively. I literally stepped off the desk to go to the bathroom and boom, we went from down to up very sharp. I'm like, what happened? Had to be a good story. We had a good story, but they're not ripping ahead, even though this is one of the more detailed and more substantial stories that we've gotten.

So I do have to wonder how much of this either (A) is priced into the market, or (B) is basically traders' exhaustion at saying, you know, another one of these stories. They're not mutually exclusive, but how the market interprets them can lead to very different outcomes — because if it's largely priced in, you have to wonder if it's a sell-the-news type of event. If the market's not reacting just because they're simply tired of reacting to these, that does leave room for further upside. So that's a huge unknown right now.

AI 与半导体：信仰还在驱动？

Yeah, it's hard to tell because people like to think of good things happening. And at this point in time, I get the sense from people I talk to that the worst case scenario is that things stay ossified where they are right now. I don't see much appetite from the US side to really renew hostilities in any way, any major form, despite what President Trump says from time to time. And as much as he claims to not care about the electoral calendar, that is something that I'm sure is on top of mind for him.

So maybe we're at worst case scenario and this is where it's at. But I've read — and I'm sure you have too — we may not have felt the full pang of oil prices if the strait doesn't reopen. I mean, $5 a gallon gas plus, et cetera, could have a damaging effect. We have to kind of see how that's going to fit.

But I think within everything you're saying — and this is not a secret — AI stocks, semiconductor stocks, they're doing the heavy lifting here. They are producing solid earnings, as you said, which is a big departure from maybe like the dotcom boom of 20 plus years ago when a lot of it was just based on hype and hope. But these companies aren't profitable. They're extremely in debt. And there's still a lot of just faith — a lot of this upswing is based on faith that they're going to continue to deliver.

And I found it really telling a couple weeks ago when Nvidia had another good quarter — I think they beat earnings expectations for like the 14th or 15th quarter in a row — and the stock, I think it went down a little bit despite a terrific quarter. So there's a lot of hope. How do you weigh that?

Well, when it comes to earnings, let's say in something like Nvidia, the bar has been raised so high, right? So think of like the pole vaulter, who's got the bar raised up already — it's very hard for the pole vaulter to clear it. And I think that's what happens to some extent when you have an Nvidia situation where everybody expects them to be better than expected.

And to a certain extent with Nvidia, you do also have to ask the question: where might fresh money be coming into Nvidia? Is there anyone who's not familiar with the story already? Is there anyone who's left to invest in it in a major way other than sort of general inflows of the market? So I think in that situation, it's more of a victim of its own success than it is anything else.

两大隐患 + 互联网泡沫的平行教训

And so to some extent you take it case by case. But you do raise the point — there's two huge vulnerabilities to this rally, neither of which has raised its head yet.

Vulnerability 1: All you really need to create a terrible situation is for someone like Alphabet or Microsoft or someone else to say, "You know what? We've spent a lot of money here. We're not gonna continually throw more money at this situation. We're gonna try to sit tight with what we have and see if those investments pay off." That happens, you'll just hear like a big screeching of the brakes and that could be problematic.

Vulnerability 2 — the dot-com parallel: I'm gonna throw some names at you and tell me if these sound familiar. Global Crossing, Lucent, Northern Telecom, Cisco (but Cisco's still around). These were the companies in those days — every race was to build bandwidth. We needed bandwidth to support the internet.

And I will stipulate that bandwidth proved necessary. I'll also stipulate that even the wildest expectations for how life-changing the internet was going to be came true. Yet many of the companies I've rattled off — and I haven't even finished rattling, there's WorldCom, there's MCI, there's a whole bunch of these companies — they fell by the wayside. Why? Because they spent and invested wildly putting in this bandwidth, but it wasn't necessary immediately, and it took a long time for it to pay off, and there was a lot of misallocated capital.

And I do feel that that is something that we have to be cognizant of, because no company wants to be left behind in this AI gold rush. And certainly the manufacturers of the picks and shovels have been the big beneficiaries of this — metaphorically speaking. But at some point they're not all going to be winners. It's just not going to happen. It's physically impossible. But yet you can't risk not taking your best shot, because if you don't take your shot, it's the Wayne Gretzky theory — you miss on all the shots you don't take.

So everybody's got to take the shot. Everybody's got to spend a lot of money. Some will spend it wisely. Some will not. And there will also be competitors that arise that we haven't really — that either we haven't thought of or honestly don't exist yet. Remember, neither Google nor Meta/Facebook existed during the internet bubble. They're post-internet bubble. So it's a much more fluid situation. And I think it's a little risky for the market to just sort of declare victory. Otherwise, if that were true, we'd be searching, we'd be connecting via AOL and searching on Yahoo. And things would be very different in our use of the internet. So these are the parallels. History doesn't repeat. But yeah, there's certain elements that are rhyming.

Although I do certainly miss my AOL instant messenger. That was a lot of fun back when I was in college.

等权重 S&P 持平 + 高短仓 vs 高保证金负债

I guess two more quick ones before we move on. Talked about how a lot of this — the AI companies are doing the heavy lifting when it comes to the S&P 500, NASDAQ 100, et cetera. I'm sure it's not lost on you that the equal weighted S&P is flat basically since February. And we're wondering if and when we're gonna see a broadening of these gains.

Goldman had an interesting report yesterday more or less saying that there's like historically elevated levels of short interest on some of the more cyclical stocks. And if they get drawn up, that could lead to just forced buying pressure that could broaden out these gains, at least for a little while. Do you have a view on that?

I haven't seen the Goldman report directly. I am familiar with it from your commentary and from other reporting. Yes, it's plausible. If you have a big short interest in stocks in specific sectors, it does leave them quite susceptible. Not knowing the specifics — are these stocks being shorted? I don't know. Is it a Walmart? By the way, Walmart and Nvidia traded at the same PE prior to their earnings, despite one having much higher growth. So let me just stipulate and say, yes, heavy short interest is a risk.

The flip side to that, of course, is margin debt as a percentage of the stock market capitalization is also at a record peak. On one side, you've got the dry powder of short covering. On the other hand, you've got sort of a record reliance on borrowed money to facilitate the market's gains. It's a very fluid situation. And yet we don't really see people displaying any real risk aversion. That's a bit tricky.

Yeah, nobody wants to be the one that missed out.

大型 IPO 是否预示市场顶部？

OK, one more question on equities and then we're going to move on to a few other sectors. Last week, I had another good friend of mine, Noel Atchison, who's a crypto macro analyst on the show. And she made an interesting point that historically big IPOs have coincided with sort of market tops. We have three big IPOs coming up focused on AI: SpaceX is likely going to be the biggest in history, and then OpenAI and Anthropic.

One example that will resonate very well with this audience is Coinbase when it went public in April 2021 (technically a direct listing). That was a local top during COVID and Bitcoin and the market traded down for months thereafter. So what are your thoughts on that?

Yeah, again, without getting into the specifics, let me just say broadly — given the reported size of some of these listings, that's a lot of money for the market to absorb. One of the positive factors as a big underlying scenario factor is that there's been generally a favorable supply demand relationship in equities — meaning that some combination of buybacks and private equity activity have tended to reduce the amount of stock outstanding. So less supply, more demand, or even equal demand, that provides a lift.

This wave of IPOs threatens to put that into retrograde, at least for the short term. If we're talking about somewhere between $75 and $100 billion in new stock coming onto the marketplace, that could certainly be a bit of a negative for stocks from a supply-demand basis, particularly where in some cases you have some of these stocks which are not necessarily particularly profitable.

That's putting things generously.

So that can be a big problem.

货币基金 + 回购利率 → 边际购买力？

Yeah. And it's funny too — this is coinciding at a point in time where there's a glut of money in the system. I want to make sure I get these notes right, but I think $120 billion went into money market funds this month. Repo rates actually fell below the bottom of the Fed's target range, which again speaks to just a lot of cash sloshing around. Do you think that's a little extra dry powder that might provide a cushion or is this just like uncorrelated data points that aren't that related to each other?

Yes and no. Obviously the more money sloshing around is a good thing. Whether or not why it's sloshing around in money market funds can be two different things. Is it because people are concerned about their finances and that's the money they need to keep in a place where they can find it? To certain — one of the economic statistics that came out this morning was that personal income growth was zero, meaning that people are losing purchasing power.

So consumers have not been acting all that well. Again, Walmart we saw go down. Today, Dollar Tree is zooming. What does that tell you about consumer mindset? If you believe in the K-shaped economy thing, if that money into money markets is coming from people who have disposable income to invest, that mitigates against the supply-demand discussion. But it's very tricky to parse out. I'm always a bit reticent to sort of say "oh, cash on the sidelines," because sometimes that cash is there and it's not leaving.

PCE、密歇根消费者信心 与 通胀错觉

All right, well, that's a good point. And since we talked about new data that came out, let's just briefly talk about inflation. So PCE headline was, I think, 3.8% in April. Core was 3.3%, the highest in years. It was a little bit below analyst expectations, but still historically high. What is your read on all of this and its associated impact on consumer behavior? I know spending went up, but I think it was just like a tenth of a percentage point.

We got a little bit of a surprise last week in that the Conference Board consumer sentiment survey was a positive surprise, although the Michigan numbers that have been coming out just are sequentially worse, worse, and worser, for the lack of, you know, to coin a phrase. When you dig into the Michigan numbers — because they break it down pretty effectively — yes, there's a partisan aspect to them. Democrats feel worse than Republicans, but independents are pretty feeling pretty crappy too.

And they've had some graphics in there that indicate that affordability is really a big, big issue for people. Even when inflation is moderating, it's telling you the prices are going up, just not as quickly. They're not coming down. It's a common misperception. Very common misperception. I think that's where a lot of people really feel — I think that's a big messaging problem. It was a big messaging problem for Biden when he was seeking reelection. It's gonna be a big messaging problem for the Republicans in the midterms because people hear inflation is coming down and they think, "I want to go to the store, nothing's cheaper. Everything's more expensive."

That's literally what they're telling you. They're just telling you it's not getting bad as quickly as it was.

It reminds me of a similar misperception in crypto where people say Bitcoin is deflationary. It's not. It's inflationary. It just — issuance rate decreases. The supply is still going up and it will go up for another 114 years or so.

Just more slowly. But that's the same misperception. And I think the problem you have from a political and socioeconomic point of view is the people who are least likely to understand that nuance are the ones in many cases feeling it the hardest. Because one of the other things in the consumer sentiment surveys is how miserable you are tends to correlate inversely with how educated you are. Not 100%, but there are statistics that show people with lower levels of education feel worse about affordability than people with higher levels of education, but not exclusively. And everybody, I think, feels miserable about it.

So we have to work through this. And the situation in the Persian Gulf is doing nothing to help affordability inflation expectations. So yes, the PCE number this morning — on a monthly basis going on the core PCE, which is the Fed's preferred target, going up 0.2 as opposed to 0.3 — was indeed a good data point. But we didn't really see much of a rally based on that. We saw stocks come off their lows a little bit. But before the Axios story, stocks still had a minus sign in front of them when I'm talking about broad indices.

We're also seeing inflation in other commodities like copper — that's a direct result of the data center build out. So a lot of moving parts, few of them moving in the right direction for affordability for consumers.

Axios 故事的细节

Gotcha. And just one quick note — this Axios story you're referencing basically says that negotiators on both sides reached an agreement on a 60-day MOU to extend the ceasefire, appears to leave a lot of the big nitty-gritty details for another day's negotiations. But I guess it is welcome news, especially since there have been fights going on the last couple of days. Iran launched, I believe, a ballistic missile at Kuwait, and nobody wants to see a return to all of that.

So I can understand why people are relieved. But as you said in the very beginning — we've seen this many times before, and nothing is done until it's done.

All right, we'll be right back.

Is my crypto working as hard as I am? ... [Same Coinbase One ad as above — 40% staking boost, 3.5% APY on USDC, up to 4% Bitcoin back. Last chance to join at Coinbase.com/Unchained before member month ends. Standard disclaimers apply.]*

Warsh 深度解读：从两次降息到可能加息的剧变

All right, we're back. Steve, the floor is yours to give your two cents on Kevin Warsh.

The Kevin Warsh situation, as with anything Fed-related, is not the easiest thing to parse, especially against the backdrop of price pressures emanating from the Gulf. Think about where we were just in February, before the missiles started flying. The markets were pricing in two full rate cuts by December with a 50% chance for a third — give or take, that extra rate cut chance fluctuated between 35 and 65%. So let's call it two and a half rate cuts priced in. That would be about 62 basis points.

We've now flipped. When I looked earlier this morning, we were talking about call it a 70% chance for a rate hike by December, with a rate cut fully priced in for March of 2026. So when you look toward the end of the year — 62.5 before, and call it another 18, there's roughly 80 basis points and change at the front end of the curve. That's monstrous. And yet stocks are up.

Stocks can rally when rate cut hopes fade, but only if they're rallying because the economy is so strong. I would argue these rate expectations have moved not because of a wildly stronger economic picture. In January, February, we were concerned about a slowdown in the labor economy. Remember the Fed's dual mandate — full employment at stable prices. There was a genuine debate: if inflation is coming down (maybe not to the level the Fed wants), but the labor market requires cuts, which side of the dual mandate will the Fed err towards? The consensus was that they would err toward cutting rates to help out the labor economy.

Well, that mentality has flipped pretty much 180 degrees. The last few labor reports have been solid enough to take a lot of that labor market worry off the table. That's not necessarily cold comfort for new graduates who are having trouble finding jobs or some people who feel they've been displaced by AI. I'm not saying this is a super robust labor market. But when you're talking about 4.3%, 4.4% unemployment, you're also saying that 95.6% of the people who want a job have a job. So you flipped on labor, and now you flipped on price pressures because clearly the situation in the Gulf has raised energy price pressures and is starting to leak back into the core.

FOMC 内部的"四票反对"

So now the question is: do we stay stable or do they have to raise? At the last meeting, we had four dissents. One was Dr. Myron, who dissented in favor of lower rates — I think he was 100% dissenting in favor of lower rates in his relatively short tenure as Fed Governor. That was his prerogative. His seat is because Chairman Powell didn't leave because of the legal issues. Kevin Warsh took Dr. Myron's seat.

The other three dissents came from various FOMC members who were advocating to remove the easing bias from the Fed's statement, which the market has sort of done for them. Last Thursday evening, Christopher Waller, who had been one of the more dovish members of the Fed — some might argue it was because he wanted the job that Kevin Warsh got, others might just argue that "hey, when the facts change, so do my opinions" — came out basically saying he wants the easing bias removed as well.

Warsh 是哪个 Warsh？

What Kevin Warsh is going to have to deal with here, and what markets are going to have to reckon with, is — when it comes to setting policy, he's one of 12. And it's not clear there is a consensus by any means to start cutting rates immediately.

The other question, which is sort of a broader question we're not going to get an answer to for a little bit, is that he's a known quantity in the fact that he's been on the FOMC before — he was a Fed governor. But he was one of the more hawkish members at the time. So which Kevin Warsh do we get? Is it the relatively hawkish member who voted against a lot of the monetary stimulus that occurred in the wake of the global financial crisis? Or is it someone who told the president what he wanted to hear and that "I'm going to advocate for lower rates"?

One of the ways he's going to try to do that, I think, is to change the benchmark. I think he wants to use a trimmed inflation measure rather than core PCE, because that would smooth out some of the higher price influences. But he's going to have to convince a room full of skeptics at this point.

And also remember — markets have an eerie way of throwing real-world tests at new Fed chairs. They don't happen very often, so it's not statistically significant, but if you look back, a few of them have gotten real-world tests early in their tenure. We will learn soon enough how he handles it. But it is — it's not smooth sailing.

Warsh 的更深定位：Fed 应远离财政化

Yeah, sort of trying to make sense of the two — the Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde, hawkish/dovish Kevin Warsh. One of the things that stood out to me during his confirmation hearings was pointing out how he wants the Fed to move away from something more akin to fiscal policy and just focus on monetary rates. That's sort of how he's trying to thread the needle.

Given all the QE during the Great Financial Crisis and the trillions of dollars added during COVID — the ratchet effect you mentioned, the Fed balance sheet has just been going up and up and up. And every time it seems they try to reduce it, something happens that forces them to either pause or have it go up. So we'll have to see.

加密板块：被遗忘的资产类别？

All right. Let's turn to crypto. We have about 10 minutes left. Bitcoin is back up above $73,000. ETH is right around $2,000. These are both up maybe 100 bps from lows earlier today before that Axios story came out. But enthusiasm for crypto is still muted from what I can tell. Implied volatility just hit a nine-month low for Bitcoin. I'm not seeing a lot of positioning for convex movements one way or another.

Crypto, to a certain extent, the simplest answer would be — crypto has been dull compared to tech stocks. If you had to draw a Venn diagram between crypto enthusiasts and tech investing enthusiasts/tech traders, there's probably a pretty big overlap between those two circles. And momentum-based trading or momentum-based investing has been a huge feature of recent markets.

So quite frankly, if you're looking at your portfolio saying "okay, my crypto, it's not doing much" — Bitcoin tried to rally let's call from $65 to $80 and that didn't really pan out, and meanwhile I bought Micron Technologies and that's however many zillion percent — where am I going to want to go with it?

IPO 巨潮 vs 加密资金分流

Looking down the road, if I were going to look for excess liquidity to invest in some of the IPOs we discussed earlier — many of the people who would be thinking about investing in those IPOs, at least from the individual side, would be people who might view their crypto positions as something they could sell to buy those IPOs. Not advocating for it, but I think these are pressures crypto is facing.

数字黄金 vs 实物黄金 vs 非生息资产

Also, the other feature is — let's go back to the digital gold argument (a whole other topic for debate). Real gold, the yellow stuff, isn't exactly ripping ahead either, because these are for the most part, unless you're doing some relatively sophisticated stuff with crypto, non-interest-bearing assets. And interest rates, as we just discussed, have gone up substantially. When you have a situation where nominal rates and money market rates have increased substantially, and you're holding a non-interest-bearing set of assets, that's not necessarily a favorable backdrop.

So that's kind of why we've seen crypto sort of get a little left behind. It's not the sexy flavor of the month right now — tech stocks are. And I think it's suffering as a result.

Kraken / Ledger 推迟 IPO + ETF 资金外流

Yeah, it's making me think — going back to some of the AI-related IPOs coming down the pike, and then you think about one of my former employers, Kraken, reportedly they've delayed their own IPO. I think Ledger is another one that's delayed it, citing market conditions. Granted there's different market conditions for SpaceX and OpenAI and Anthropic — you could make the argument that they're so big they're almost immune to any market conditions at this point.

And then, billions of dollars in outflows from crypto ETFs. I saw something — it might have been the FT today — I think it was one hyper-focused AI ETF, like Micron and only like two or three other stocks, beat one of Fidelity's crypto-related ETFs to like over $10 billion.

DRAM, which is the Roundhill Memory ETF, depending how you measure it, either became the most successful ETF launch in history out-surpassing IBIT or was neck and neck. It depends how you measure it.

"Normies on crypto" — ETF 的双刃剑

The bottom line is — crypto got a huge boom. You had sort of the perfect storm between easy accessibility via ETFs and an administration that essentially was outwardly touting the benefits of crypto. That was as good of a scenario as you can come up with.

The downside of that ETF adoption is — I think I termed it as "normies on crypto" now too. It hit me when I was invited to speak at a crypto conference here in Connecticut. I looked around the room and I was around the average age. I thought I was going to be like grandpa going in there. It was a lot of people who'd gotten into crypto via the ETFs.

The good thing at the time was that led to huge inflows and obviously price increases. Bad news now is — to a certain extent, they're crypto tourists. They can say "I bought, insert name of crypto ETF here. I bought this, it did well for a while, and now it's not. Or I bought it at the highs and I mistimed it completely. Let me move to something else." If it was bought by performance chasers in the first place, it's going to be sold by performance chasers as something else outperforms it.

That's not me taking a view one way or the other on crypto as a long-term investment. But I do think that is a very important consideration. You can't dismiss the importance of money flow. For better or worse, the huge success of crypto ETFs was such a boon on the way up. But now this is the flip side of that trade.

Bitcoin 矿企转型 AI 数据中心

To many investors now, it's one more sector among their speculative momentum-based investments. Also think of the popularity of some companies like IREN and NBIS, which have largely changed their business model from data centers to AI data centers / computing power. Not fully fungible, but it's migrating.

Yeah, and I didn't mean to interrupt you, but we've done entire shows on that transition. I had John Todaro from Needham — he walked me through the economics. You're buying an asset, producing an asset in Bitcoin that from their point of view isn't moving up exponentially anymore. The hash rate keeps going up. It's more competitive. It's more expensive to mine. Or you can go turn yourself into some sort of hyperscaler, get a 10-year contract from Google for an ungodly sum of money. What are you going to do?

加密的"清洗效应"与逆向投资精神

But it makes me think — this could have a good cleansing effect for Bitcoin / crypto in general. There's a lot more competition for that sort of highly risk-on money. There's prediction markets, there's AI stocks, there's lots of other places, not just altcoins. And in certain ways, I think you almost need to have a real sense of purpose when you invest in crypto today because it's not the sexy thing to do.

There's a common saying — you're not really a crypto person until you've seen your net worth drop by 90% and then have it go up 300% thereafter. The normies may not have the stomach for it, but it's happened to me multiple times and to plenty of long-time listeners. Once you get through it once, you're hardened. But it looks really scary on the way down.

It makes me think of Howard Marks or Warren Buffett in the sense that you have to really put your money in when everyone else is bearish. Howard Marks did in the distressed credit space and made a fortune. BlackRock — they launched their ETFs, they filed for them during a crypto winter, when Gary Gensler was still SEC chair. It was not a popular thing to do and they did it against the backdrop of a highly crypto-skeptical regulator.

So we'll have to see what happens. But certainly there's a lot more competition for the money that would just automatically go into crypto. And I think some of those ETF flows encapsulate it perfectly.

收尾：Sosnick 最关心的一件事

So we're just about at time, although I know we probably could go for another 45 minutes. We talked about a lot today — inflation, gold; we didn't even get to what's happening with the rising dollar and the flattening yield curve, the divergence between equities and geopolitical uncertainty. What is the one thing you're really going to be watching over the next few weeks?

Number one right now, as I alluded to at the top of the show, is how does the stock market react if we get a peace deal? If we don't, we just sort of muddle along again. At some point, traders get tired of it. But the number one question that really jumped out at me today is — and I wish I had an easier answer — are we in a "buy the rumor, sell the news" scenario whereas a peace deal is a negative? Or is the market not reacting better today because they're sort of skeptical of it, which means they can rally further? Those are two very divergent outcomes from the same potential piece of news. I'm of course rooting for something that brings that about.

三类交易员的"地缘智商"排序

Once we get past this — equity markets particularly are terrible at geopolitical events, commodity markets are best. Notably, at least when we started the show, oil was not down today on this news. Oil was off its highs, but not down. Commodity traders are best. Treasury traders are next best. Equity traders are somewhere deep in the back of the pile. The others are more focused strictly on supply/demand or inflation expectations as opposed to stories. They're less easily distracted.

收益率曲线扁平化 与 Waller 的影响

So we're going to have to see how that all plays out amidst the backdrop of a flattening yield curve. And that again is because Christopher Waller is the one to largely thank for that. We've seen the inflation expectations rising and pushing up the short end of the curve, but it was pushing up the curve parallel. The Waller talk flipped it a bit because if the Fed's going to be more sensitive about inflation expectations, that changes the back end of the curve.

中期选举年的隐忧

Ultimately, let's see what happens in the midterms. Don't forget that from an equity point of view, we've had two down years in the last 10 or 12 — 2022 and 2018. Those were both midterm election years. And you have a new Fed chair. So while we've had a very successful year in equities so far, I think it's not necessarily right to just assume that the path is ever upward. And I'm a little concerned by the lack of vigilance, by the lack of risk aversion that I'm seeing right now.

节目结束

Got it. Okay. All right. Well, that's a great place to end it. So thank you once again for joining us, Steve. Thank you everybody for watching and listening.

That's it from me and Steve, but don't go anywhere — because Laura's up next with EtherFi founder Mike Silagadze and Security Alliance's Isaac Patka, who are asking whether Manuel Araoz is right that all DeFi is unsafe. Stay with us.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 三个让人意外的视角
### 1. 这场访谈最反常的细节：股市对"和平消息"已经审美疲劳，但债券和原油却仍在诚实地反应
Sosnick 提到的"棘轮效应"（ratchet effect）表面上是说股市单向上行，但真正的反常之处在于：**同一条 Axios 新闻里，原油和债券收益率会"回吐"，而股票不回吐**。这暴露了一个被忽视的事实——三个市场对"真相"的诚实度排序大致是：**商品 > 国债 > 股票**。Sosnick 自己也说"股票交易员排在队伍最后面"。
这其实是个跨市场的"认知带宽分层"现象：商品交易员只看供需，债券交易员看通胀预期，而股票交易员看的是叙事。叙事容易被"用尽"，但供需不会。这意味着，当你想判断一个地缘事件的真假，**别看 SPX，去看 WTI 的尾盘动作**。
### 2. 跨领域连接：Coinbase ETF 与"加密游客化"，本质是同一种"流动性民主化诅咒"
这里有一个被反复忽略的悖论：ETF 让 BTC 触达"normies"是 2024 年的最大利好，但同一机制现在正在变成最大利空。这和**互联网泡沫时期的"宽带过剩"完全同构**——Sos

item.title: [Bits + Bips] Why Arjun Sees a Link Between Pope Leo and Bitcoin
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最意外的观察：教皇不是在"评论"加密货币，他是在重新激活一条1600年的反集中传统

大多数人读这段会停留在"哇，教皇提到了 Bitcoin"的层面，但真正的爆点是 Arjun 不经意揭示的那条暗线——奥古斯丁《上帝之城》→ 阿奎那自然法 → 天主教社会训导中的"辅助性原则"(subsidiarity) → 中本聪白皮书。

这不是巧合，也不是隐喻。辅助性原则的核心命题是："决策应在最低胜任层级做出，而非向上集中。"把这句话原封不动放进 Bitcoin 白皮书的摘要里，你几乎察觉不到违和。换句话说，加密最深的哲学根基不在赛博朋克 90 年代的密码朋克邮件列表，而在 4 世纪希波的奥古斯丁那里。Satoshi 其实是在用代码重写一份天主教文献——只是他自己未必知道。

这也解释了为什么"教皇提到加密"在情感层面让从业者激动得有点奇怪——不是因为分发渠道，而是某种潜意识的归乡感：主流终于用我们原本就在使用的语言回应了我们。

二、被节目嘉宾错过的更深矛盾：教皇的机构 vs. 教皇的话语

Austin 在尾声半开玩笑地戳到了痛点——"一个领导着史上最集中机构之一的人，在论证去中心化"——但他随即滑开了。这里其实藏着一个结构性的不对称值得深挖：

天主教会的组织形态是 hierarchical（层级制），但其神学输出却长期是 anti-concentration（反集中）的。这种"内圣外王的反向版本"——用集中的权威去布道分散的伦理——正是当今 AI/加密讨论里最被忽视的治理模型。

类比一下：以太坊基金会本身是个相对集中的组织，却不断为去中心化布道。Vitalik 不是教皇，但他在结构上扮演的角色非常类似。真正稳定的去中心化系统，可能恰恰需要一个集中的"传道节点"来不断重申反集中的价值观——否则去中心化本身会被市场的赢家通吃逻辑吞噬。这是一个反直觉但极其重要的治理悖论。

三、跨领域连接：从"巴别塔"到"Articles of Confederation"，再到 AI 时代的失败模式光谱

节目里 Rob 把 Sam Altman 的"天空中的魔法智能"类比为巴别塔，Austin 立刻反驳说"第一届美国政府因为过度去中心化而失败了"——这两句话碰撞出的东西比他们意识到的更重要。

把这两个失败案例并置，你会得到一个集中/去中心化失败的双极光谱：

  • 巴别塔失败模式：单点集中 → 通天 → 神变乱其语言 → 系统崩溃。对应 AGI 风险、央行单点货币、Big Tech 垄断。
  • 邦联条例失败模式：极端分散 → 各州拒绝协调 → 财政与防务瘫痪 → 必须重构。对应 ICO 时代的混乱、纯 P2P 协议在没有协调层时的扩展失败、Quakers 模式无法治理大规模社会。

真正的工程问题不是"选哪一极"，而是"在光谱上找到那个既不通天也不瘫痪的工作区间"。Pope Leo 的演讲、Federalist Papers、Bitcoin 白皮书——它们其实都是在为不同时代回答同一道题：如何在不变成巴别塔的前提下，不退化成邦联条例。

这给一个意外推论：未来 AI 与加密的监管讨论，不应再用"中心化 vs 去中心化"这种二元框架，而应该用"光谱定位 + 失败模式识别"。监管者真正的工作是判断当前系统更靠近哪一端的失败，然后把它推向中段——而不是教条地选边。

一句送给你的"啊哈"

教皇没在谈科技。他在用 2026 年的词汇，回应一个奥古斯丁在公元 426 年就已经写下答案的问题。加密行业以为自己在做未来主义，其实在做神学考古——而这恰恰是它最被低估的合法性来源。

📝 转录文本

一、教皇 Leo 首份通谕：AI 与人的尊严

The Pope recently had some things to say that we want to get to. Um, released the first encyclical of the Magnifica Humanitist titled On Safeguarding the Human Person in the Time of AI. Uh, this was presented along with one of the Anthropic co-founders, Chris Olah.

And Leo had a lot of things to say here — not just about AI. We'll get to something else he said as well, but he said:

Artificial intelligence needs to be disarmed, deliberately chosen, because this moment needs words capable of attracting attention.

He said AI is an instrument of domination, exclusion, and death if unrestrained, and that the pursuit of greater profits cannot justify choices that systematically sacrifice jobs — because the human person is an end, not a means.

二、教皇谈金融体系与加密货币

There were a couple of other things in here, but I also want to get to some of his greater comments on the financial ecosystem at large as it impacts this debate, which was:

In recent years, finance has increased in importance and has undergone significant innovation driven partly by the introduction of cryptocurrencies.

That's right — the Pope is talking about crypto.

The reflections and observations contained in the teaching of my predecessors, particularly in their encyclicals, have highlighted how the financial intermediation sector, when operating without the necessary anthropological and moral foundations, has not only produced manifest abuses and injustice, but also demonstrated a capacity to create systemic and worldwide economic risks.

On the other hand, the Pope also said:

Savings transformed into credit for the real economy, thereby creating both jobs and self-employed work, remains central for the development and the investments that must accompany ongoing transactions. The social function of credit remains irreplaceable.

So there's a lot to unpack, and I put those side by side because the Pope seems to be saying, "I'm not for or against either of these tools — it's a matter of how you use them."

三、Chris 的回应：教会与技术的长久关系

But Chris, I want to start with you. I know you had some views here. What did you make of Pope Leo's comments?

Well, when the big man talks — and I'll just say that Villanova is having a moment right now, between the Pope and the Knicks. Um, but I think it's absolutely fascinating that the Pope is talking about these technologies and even crypto.

And the church has had a long, storied relationship with technology going back to the Gutenberg press. Uh, in the beginning they thought it was awesome because it gave them the ability to spread their teachings — but then, you know, the Protestants got a hold of it and they were like, "Wait a second, we need to control this stuff."

And so it's just so fascinating to me that this is another iteration of the Catholic Church having a relationship with technology.

年轻人重回教会

Um, and by the way, I don't know if you guys have seen the stats — the Catholic Church is doing very, very well amongst young people. Uh, I was down at Georgetown recently and I don't think it's an anomaly. Like, I talked to the kids there and they're like, "Oh yeah, the church gives me meaning. I lost it in social media. Now I'm finding it again." Which is just fascinating.

So these are part of big macro trends that are happening.

教皇的"分发力"与 DeFi 的暗示

Um, I think at a very basic level, the fact that the Pope is mentioning crypto is a net very much positive for the space. Because talk about distribution — the Pope has massive distribution, particularly from a mindshare perspective and a mainstreaming perspective. If this was fringe technology, I don't think the Pope would be talking about it.

That goes for AI too. He's talking about AI and crypto together. Uh, how many times have we talked on this show? These are technologies that you cannot separate. They go hand in hand.

Excellent. I thought it was fascinating that he was critical of intermediaries. And my open question is — maybe the guy likes DeFi. Maybe Villanova's finest likes DeFi. And I think there's a reason for the Pope to like DeFi, frankly, because it does democratize access to financial services and credit — things that he thinks, I think, done right, are thoughtful.

But I mean, in my mind, this is absolutely fascinating.

四、Rob 的解读：康德、无人机与权力集中

Yeah. Rob, the Pope's comments — I thought they were very thoughtful.

You know, he was invoking Emmanuel Kant when he said, "Focus on people as ends, not as means." He identified AI as a technology of tremendous genius. He said it's a powerful tool. But what he also said is, he doesn't want to diminish the role of human dignity.

So you sort of think about things like automated lethality and the rise of drones. You have decision-making that's done by an AI. There's a risk of dehumanization — creating a distance between one human being and another. Um, I think that may have been what he's addressing. I thought all those comments made a lot of sense.

He also highlighted in an earlier speech a few months ago around the risks of creating concentrated wealth and power in the hands of few people through AI — which is a legitimate risk. You know, if you control AGI, you can spin up businesses, divisions on the fly. You can marshal execute resources. Citadel is well positioned to benefit from AI.

You know, if tokens become money, and tokens are claims and productive assets and they're going to trade — which they will in the future — there are legitimate concerns around this. So I think he identified the right issues. Uh, so I thought it was a very well-written speech.

五、Arjun 的核心论点：辅助性原则与 Bitcoin 白皮书的哲学血脉

You know, there's — I think the crypto philosophical lineage is kind of important to think about. You know, there's a — and I spend, I don't know why I do this, but I've been spending a lot of time thinking about this — um, you know, like Judeo-Christian philosophies.

I think there's a general thread connecting what the Pope said to the entire crypto philosophical project, right? All the way back to the Bitcoin white paper. And I don't think it's a stretch, actually.

Subsidiarity：从奥古斯丁到中本聪

So if you look at traditional Catholic social teachings, it has a principle called subsidiarity. Um, and that idea is that decisions should be made at the lowest competent level, and it's not centralized upward.

And so that principle tracks back to Augustine's City of God, where worldly power is always presumed to be corruptible — which is what we're talking about right now. And the only stable institutional design is one that resists concentration.

So when he's speaking about concentration — this is the same thing if you read the Bitcoin white paper. The lens that Satoshi is essentially arguing for is monetary subsidiarity. (Sorry, I always mess that up.) And so it pushes decisions down to the protocol layer rather than upward to a central authority.

And this is what we always talk about, right? Like, what is the point of why we started this in the first place? Why are we excited to get up?

AI、加密与同一句话的不同词汇

And so I think when the Pope is framing things in terms of AI, I think it's the same terms — which is: How do you resist concentration? How do you protect, in this specific case, human agency, regardless of whether you think it's right or wrong?

And so he's kind of drawing on an intellectual tradition that I think is also crypto-philosophical and what is inherent. So I'm not, you know, I wouldn't say it's a metaphor or anything — but it is the same philosophical comment expressed in different vocabulary, and we're all thinking about the same thing.

政治影响：1.5 亿天主教徒与全球监管

So the political implication, I think, is that a decentralized infrastructure argument now has more of a moral and theological backing — for, I don't know, the billion and a half people that follow Catholic teachings, or the global institution — which should show up in EU regulatory debates, it should show up here in the United States, it should show up in the Global South policy, etc., etc., and hopefully at the next round of how people think about AI and finance legislation, um, or in some cases non-legislation — we just continue the path.

And so I, my hope is that at least his essay, or his post, or his speeches gives a real shift in that type of political coalition and thinking. Um, and I think maybe the markets may not price that today, even if that is the direction that we go down.

六、Austin 的延伸：第一任美国教皇与联邦党人文集

Austin, sorry, Austin, you have to quote Aquinas now. We got an Augustine. That was a thoughtful answer, man.

I am. And I'm going to go a different direction here. I'm actually going to pull out another philosophical thread, which — I found it interesting to read this with Leo being the first American Pope. Because part of the exact same framing that he's putting there also shows up in some of the political theory around the founding of the American system at large, right?

Which is this idea that concentrated power is inevitably corrupting in whatever form it is given, and that one of the ways to fight that is, of course, decentralizing it.

邦联条例的失败与权力制衡

I will remind everybody, the first American government actually had to be redone because it was too decentralized to function — with the Articles of Confederation. But two, if you read the Federalist Papers, it's the idea of having all of the different nodes fighting against each other and working at cross purposes. Because if we believe in inevitable corruption among these things, and that they're fallible sort of creations of man, you pit the states against the federal government. You pit the federal government against itself with the executive and the legislative and the judicial separation.

非匿名的去中心化：奇异的盟友

It's fascinating to me to see related principles expressed in theological terms — because I'm wondering if what we're moving towards here, especially in the world of AI and like fears about AGI and jobs, is a greater appreciation of not what I will call anonymous decentralization, but call it non-anonymous decentralization, right?

Like, actually allowing individual people to get their hands on the decision-making apparatus and be respected in that framework.

So I'm wondering if the Pope is perhaps going to create an alliance of strange bedfellows here as we start seeing these disparate forms of philosophical thought run together — because a lot of what I just said, market economists would be all over as well from a competition perspective.

七、Arjun 回应：自然法、反垄断与互操作性

Yeah. And you know, I think — forgetting the actual quote — but there was a quote around the separation of powers, federalism, Bill of Rights, etc. You can make the argument that it's the same structural separation argument applied to political power. And it traces back, again — this is, you know, Catholic natural law philosophy.

And so when you think about all the folks that wrote this, and then eventually you had the American founders — sure, I mean, you might have this perspective that the American Pope is referring back to this.

I think it's more important — which is, what's the forward implication of when people speak about this? And does anything happen? I don't know if anything will happen.

But when I think about what we're doing in crypto, it's: we think about antitrust, we think about mandated interoperability, we think about neutral utility regulation, we think about ring-fencing. And so my perspective — and I think actually Trump started this in the United States, so credit should be given to who started it, um, you know, after the Biden era — is just to get the moral cover from more globally acceptable and credible voices on ethics and decentralization.

Um, and in the moment where we think about American antitrust, I think — maybe there's like a political system that's being rebuilt around that, and then AI being the second piece of that. And so then we can use the same logic around financial infrastructure, payments networks, exchanges, stablecoin issuers, custody, etc., etc. And they all run on winner-take-all dynamics in the past. The question ends up being: What does it look like in the future? Um, that's open and neutral.

八、轻松一刻：教皇支持 CRPS？

So, you guys — are you saying that the Pope is into CRPS? I hope so. I hope so. Sorry, I couldn't help it. Austin — I'll throw in another — Let me explain what CRPS is. Like, that's the Ethereum Foundation's doubling down on its philosophy on censorship resistance, open source, and privacy. So — I don't know. It just sounded like a CRPS thing. You should just say "C, O, S."

九、Rob 的圣经隐喻：通天塔与"天空中的魔法智能"

Well, I'll throw another biblical metaphor. There's this comment Sam Altman made referring to AI as "magic intelligence in the sky." When I first heard that, I said, "Gee, this sounds an awful lot like the Tower of Babel."

The Tower of Babel, of course, was the first human centralized platform. It literally was about translation — which is a core use case of AI. It was built towards the heavens, and it was about ego over humility.

Um, and so I think there's a good message for the Pope. It's like, hey, you know, this is not a substitute to reify synthetic intelligence above human judgment and intuition.

Did you just make an argument for building a single giant AI data center into the sky? Basically. The Tower of Babel is like a stack of GPU chips in this metaphor, if we're — Hold on a second. For those checking the potential markets — InfiniBand is, you know, the connectivity. Go ahead. Rob did his, you know, every-show bashing of Sam Altman. So he got it in, guys. Not a nothing-burger. Got it in.

十、Austin 收尾：到底需要多少去中心化？

So, all right. Let me push back slightly here and say — I find it questionable that the Pope is arguing for complete decentralization, right? Given the nature and structural design of the Catholic Church. Just hypothetically speaking.

But I think the greater point — and this is one that actually probably hits the pivot point of the CRPS argument — is: how much decentralization do you need?

Do you need everybody to be their own sort of source of truth or decentralized, you know, version — aka the Quakers? Or do you need a system, if we're going to go in the other direction, where it's okay for there to be some top-down direction, so long as it is not absolute in a sense that denies human dignity — which is something that looks more like, call it, I don't know, a semi-permissioned chain?

That, I think, is an open question.

教皇不喜欢 FedNow，但喜欢 Canton？

But the interesting thing about the comments to me is less about what specifically it's defining. I think it's very clear from Pope Leo's comments that he's not okay with a system where it's just one or two large companies telling everybody how to think. Aka —

Pope Leo is not a fan of FedNow. But he's a fan of Canton. It sounds like you're saying — Canon guy. Sorry. Canton's not a blockchain. It doesn't belong in Russian. We'll just throw a stone there.

蝴蝶效应与最终希望

No, but, all facetiousness aside — Arjun, you made a good point about what is like the butterfly effect when somebody speaks like this, and I think that was very insightful — is: if there's a downstream argument here that moves towards an acceptance of the value of decentralizing decision-making power in more traditional tech and finance, that's probably a net positive for people in the world over time, given some of the abuses we've seen in the centralized system.

So I hope that's the outcome.

And for the record, I have absolutely no dog in the fight for the Knicks thing. I'm a longtime Spurs fan, so we'll wait to see who's in the finals there.

十一、节目预告与订阅信息

Chris, if you liked this segment, please like, subscribe, and tune in every Monday at 4:30 p.m. Eastern Time. I'm Austin Campbell, the host of Bits and Bips, along with my friends Ram Ahluwalia and Chris Perkins and our slate of exceptional guests. Every week, we're going to discuss macro, crypto, and the collision of worlds — covering topics that move markets and shape the financial landscape.

If you've been loving Bits and Bips, don't forget that the show is transitioning to its own feeds on X, YouTube, and your favorite podcast player. If you're not already subscribed to Bits and Bips on its own channels, go there now and hit that subscribe button so you can keep up with our twice-weekly livestreams and macro-crypto breakdowns.

Bits and Bips will only be on the Unchained feed for a few more weeks, so subscribe today to be ready for launch. You can get all the links at unchainedcrypto.com/bitsandbips.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Zhang Xiaojun Podcast] 143. 对何小鹏的第二次访谈：更大赌注、人形机器人Iron诞生、那场意外、技术剧变下CEO、GX和缝合怪
item.dc:creator: Zhang Xiaojun Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"AI 缝合怪"的自我否定：当成功本身成为最大的认知牢笼

何小鹏在这次访谈中最反常的动作不是"下了更大的赌注"，而是主动停掉了一个花了几年、几十亿建成的成熟系统。他把它命名为"AI 缝合怪"——一个用软件方法论 + AI 工具箱组合而成的"更强力的软件"。

意外之处在于：这套被废弃的系统在行业内仍然是"先进"的。它的"上限 1000 分、下限 900 分"的稳定性，正是大多数车厂梦寐以求的工程指标。何小鹏放弃它，不是因为它失败，而是因为它成功得太合理——合理到看不见 L5 的天花板。

这里隐藏着一个反直觉的系统性真相：对于物理 AI 这种长周期赌博，工程上的"局部最优"恰恰是战略上的最大陷阱。这与生物进化中的"局部适应陷阱"完全同构——一个物种在某个生态位适应得越完美，跨越生态位的成本就越高。何小鹏在 2022 年给自己立的两条座右铭"永不放弃 / 想赌就赌"，本质上是在对抗"成功路径依赖"这种最隐蔽的认知重力。

二、Token 的"两个宇宙"：物理 AI 与数字 AI 的不可通约性

整段访谈里有一个被他轻描淡写、但极具颠覆性的观察：一辆第七代 VLA 自动驾驶车一天只跑 3-4 小时，消耗的 token 数量远超数字 AI 给一个工程师配的全月额度。也就是说，物理世界中"机器服务机器"的 token 消耗，正在悄悄形成一个数量级完全不同的宇宙。

这意味着所谓"token 经济"将出现一次底层分叉：数字世界的 token 衡量的是"人和企业的价值"，物理世界的 token 衡量的是"机器为机器/人创造的价值"。它们的贝尔金指数（单位 token 的价值产出）完全不可比。

跨领域连接来了——这非常像 19 世纪铁路出现后，"时间"被迫分裂成两种：本地太阳时和铁路标准时。所有人都在说"时间"，但说的早已不是同一个东西。今天所有人在说"AI"，但"数字 AI 的 token"和"物理 AI 的 token"已经是两个不能换算的本体论范畴。绝大多数试图"把数字 AI 经验复制到物理 AI"的公司，都会死在这个不可通约性上——这也正是何小鹏看 Waymo 的视角：技术好，但"难以全球化"。

三、Iron 砍腿那一晚：CEO 在新技术范式下，本质是"组织级催眠师"

最值得玩味的细节是去年机器人 Iron 被怀疑"里面藏了人"那一晚。何小鹏的团队建议他等 24 小时让子弹飞，他拒绝了，第二天直接当场剪开机器人的腿。

表面看是危机公关。但深层是一个出人意料的角色转变：在剧烈的技术范式跃迁期，CEO 最稀缺的能力不是判断，而是"反熵的相信"——在 99.99% 的人都还不能"亲身体验" AI 能力的时候，CEO 必须做一件极反人性的事：把自己已经知道的真相，戏剧化地、可被低维世界感知地展示出来，否则内部团队就会因为"无法对外解释"而集体熵增、士气塌方。

这里有一个跨领域的奇妙连接：这个动作和宗教改革时期路德把《九十五条论纲》钉在城堡教堂门上是同构的。两者都不是为了说服已经懂的人，而是为了给沉默的相信者一个公共仪式，让分散的内部信念结成可见的共同体。何小鹏在访谈中反复说"我不能告诉你"、"我连内部都不会分享计划"——这正暴露出，物理 AI CEO 的真实工作不是"决策与传达"，而是在不能解释的时候，依然能让组织相信。

这也解释了那个他没明说的小秘密——为什么他坚持"小刀不要慢慢割，要砍就砍到根上"。在范式跃迁期，温和的渐进调整反而是最大的不仁慈，因为它让组织永远活在"旧逻辑还能再用一会儿"的幻觉里。

五、数字 AI 与物理 AI 的根本差异

七、永不放弃 vs 想赌就赌

Secondly, all the things done in digital AI, 很多都無法複製到物理AI, 這是一個不一樣的世界。 很多人提物理AI, 我上次都在笑, 我說首先, 你有沒有在物理世界做過業務? 如果在物理世界都沒有把業務做成, 僅僅是從一個數字世界 去分析物理世界的定義規律 去做一個物理世界的AI, 我覺得它還是一個 窄翼的物理世界。 這個物理世界還有很多 是跟人牽掛的事情。 the environment and laws related to the physical world, and finally, the business.

It is formed by which can be the first step and which is the second step. Of course, you can leave it alone from the scientific point of view. But if you look at it from the engineering point of view, or even from the product point of view, or from the business point of view, or from the scale point of view, you have to think more broadly and deeply, rather than just two or three long-term ideas.

So why is this? I can't answer you. I think you can go to any digital AI CEO before. They won't tell you their thinking logic for transformation in AI. Because they don't know if they can succeed. Second, they won't share it with everyone.

八、企业转型的真假必要性

Q：每个企业都必须转型吗？

But this is a common problem that everyone faces today, right? That is, we must transform.

I don't fully agree with this sentence. Today, digital AI can get a good transformation in some aspects. In some aspects, it doesn't need to. For a large company, if 10,000 or 50,000 people are transferred in three days, the probability of success may be increased by 15%. For a micro-company, if it uses the same method and organization, it will be able to read without any changes. Because it's small. And today's AI can help us.

There are three kinds of AI. The first one is a simple analyst. I don't need an analyst to analyze many things. AI will help me analyze. Second, a simple programmer. Third, a simple designer. In fact, he is mainly a supporter. Increase efficiency and increase efficiency. He didn't replace. He doesn't have a large-scale co-efficient. He hasn't reached it yet.

So in another way, for the small micro-companies in these three directions, I think it's more suitable. The speed should be faster. For those who are not related to these three directions, 可能再晚一点。 而对于物理AI, 今天大家根本都没有想清楚 物理AI世界是什么岗位。 刚才我讲的是三个岗位 是什么岗位最容易跟最优先在什么地方落地? 绝大部分我看到的 讲颠覆这个世界用AI来去提高效率 根本没有概念。

所以我自己认为是, From a certain point of view, it is correct. From another point of view, it is completely illogical. We often think that the physical world has a lot of changes in the sense of sense of sense. Far beyond the digital world. Because in this world, if there are 366 different senses in the 360 degrees, we think that the sense of the digital world is relatively limited.

Many people will not judge like this. Many people say that the white spirit has more sense of sense than the blue spirit. There are many in the white-collar sector, but they are also in the position of interlocking with the digital and the physical world.

From a certain point of view, if we look at the physical world today, let's assume that Google's auto-pilot system was launched in 2009, and now it's 2017. I think that Wemo is a good solution, but it's also a bad solution. Why? I think it has good technical ability. I think it's hard to globalize. In the past, the technology of level 4 was naturally a more advanced fusion. It's hard to achieve extremely high-level evolution in AI. I don't think it's a very smart evolution. Of course, Google has done a lot in the new big model. I think they may change in the future, but I haven't seen it yet.

So, in other words, I just want to give an example. Even if it makes it easier, safer, and faster for drivers to drive, we are all drivers. We have been doing this for 17 years. No company can do it better. In other words, many small companies have several guns and more than ten people. I think they have underestimated AI coding.

物理世界改變的多樣性跟複合性, 就像我們就開始從 移動互動往衝的汽車行業 都覺得我把一個事情做的極致 再極致一點就很好。 Because you think about the dimension. It's a physical dimension. You're a digital dimension. You do a digital dimension, its value point may be higher.

十、AI 的四个主方向

I've heard that many of your programs are basically talking about digital AI. Yes. Basically, there are no people in a million people. I want to ask you, what should physical AI do? How should it be promoted in a formative organization?

I think I can't say it because I've been exploring. From my point of view, today AI has four main directions:

1）数字 AI：We need to explore how digital AI changes. I think a lot of people are thinking and acting on this.

2）物理 AI：I think the second is physical AI. It may be 100 times harder than digital AI. But I think from 2017 and 2018, we will see the effects of physical AI. Similar to such as GPT, SOBIC, and other digital AI. In other words, what position does it have a huge help? Even in the long term, there is a certain replacement.

3）人体 AI：I think the third one is that today no one can really, I think except Google, put two very good O-hers on the AI of the human body. Because the medical industry is a very complex industry, including the elderly. I think the human body is not only the internal cycle, but also the external cycle.

4）企业 AI：I think the fourth one is very difficult, which is how companies and AI are intertwined. In fact, the department and the center of the AI are quite simple, while the enterprise is more difficult. But I see that there are many small enterprises I think they will start to explore more within the next 1000 years. I think this is a very good point. We are also observing and learning. We are doing the same at the right time.

So I think the AI of the enterprise is a chance for small, medium and large companies today.

Today, many people say that the old age in the old age has been done. I say that which is called the old age? Walk slowly forward with the same monotonous pace. I don't think that's the old age. I think in the past, in the era of mobile remote control robots, will gradually go down with the emergence of high-tech robots. I think it will enter a stage of division. In fact, everyone hopes that the core representative robot in the middle of physical AI can help humans achieve what kind of value. Workable. This value in the past was emotional value. Emotional value is valuable. But more importantly,

So I think next year is the beginning of the beginning.

十三、解散 300 人团队，重建机器人逻辑

Q：2023 年发生了什么？

What happened in 2023 that made you believe in it instead of believing it?

In 2023, I think if we used a team that was very familiar with robots, we wouldn't have been able to make a good, new generation of robots. So we only left less than 60 people in the 300-person team. We disbanded it.

I heard that they had 10 startup teams. Most of them have received money. Like Zhongqing, they have received a lot of money. I think we need to rebuild the logic of the entire system. We can't use the old pure machine skills, nor the old pure machine skills. But we can't know nothing. What kind of people should we use? I don't know. So I chose another new team. He knows both AI and cars, and engineering and robots. He tried to create a new robot logic.

Why did you choose him?

I think it's fate. You can't say he's not a robot or a smart driver, so you chose him, right?

He's not. I thought his direction was more consistent with my thinking. So he is one of the few people I think about when I'm thinking about the line. First of all, you choose him, he also chooses you. Then his choice of the opposite, I think, I think it can be tried.

So I often see a lot of demo or demonstration or video of the outside machine. Or they are still in our third or fourth generation of technical stations to do testing. I myself think that maybe it will succeed, but I've tried it. So I think that in Xiao Peng's continuous innovation, we are very patient and courageous to make a long-term investment. So today, many financial companies are very quickly making a demo. It's nothing for me. Just like in 2017, there were a lot of data companies in China at that time. It's the same as today's financial. Of course, there are more financials. In fact, it is. It doesn't mean that these technologies can ultimately see the true value. So it's just a simple comparison. Because such a comparison is not good.

Because of what you said, I would think that Musk likes to use talented people. He doesn't like to use those expert-like people. I think maybe in Tesla's, I think it's very difficult to bring it to most countries, cities, or industries.

I think LC is very focused on the density of talent. I think it's the potential of talent. It basically uses the best people. But we are all willing. You need to have the ability to understand such a group of people. You need to be willing to support their long-term exploration. I think it's the potential of talent. He thinks it's the density of talent. I think 要用超級聰明的人去做超級困難的事情, 而不是說用非常清晰的方向或者流程或者工具, I think it's very right at some point.

At that time, you had to bet on a humanoid robot. What was your reason for choosing it in 2023? Why must it be a humanoid robot?

I think it's because every company has chosen it. I think I'm very happy that I'm the same as my team LC. LC is crazier than me. He kept telling me that he wanted to recruit people, not to recruit robots.

Most people think of robots as commercial products. But I don't think he thought about the robots in this society, in this universe. 参与感或者跟我们每个人的情绪价值的连接度 就像2014年很多人觉得智能汽车的智能是没有价值的。 我觉得站在今天也有很多人 第一不相信通用汽车会到来 第二也根本不愿意看到通用汽车也许在20年50年以后会跟人类的 各个领域强相关。

今天刚才我们所讨论的数字AI, 只是辅助人类的 可能以三个为主的 AI 更长寿 更开心。 第二个 我觉得在物理AI上 机器人对老人的影响度非常大。 老人很有可能把机器人作为他唯一的依赖。 我觉得至少有人会这样思考吧。 我觉得小朋友机器人的思考道路 跟很多公司是不一样的。

十五、那场意外：Iron 被怀疑藏人的那一晚

去年那波爆炸性的讨论 有出乎你的意料吗 你当时还挺委屈的。

对 我们是没有想到的 我们实际上想法非常的技术化。 因为去年那款机器人是我们前年 第一季度开始做的。 对我们来说,它只是我们的一个中间版本。

那这个中间版本,我们只是想认证, 如果一个机器人有能这么多的关节, 像我们的关节还是比能少很多, 能有200多个关节。 and there are muscles and skin. I can tell you that many of the muscles we see today, their external movements, if they have muscles and skin, their movements will change and shrink significantly. Because this is a completely different thing.

In another way, we just want to explore if in this situation, can enter our lives or our work more easily.

例子：金毛 vs 机器狗

Let me give you an example. For example, we used to ride a horse or a dog before. No matter what the horse or dog is, it can't enter your room. If you let a 7-foot-1 to 1.2-meter-long hole enter your room, your room will soon have a lot of problems. For example, it can't hang its head on the bedside table.

如果你家裡有一隻金毛, 牠在掉頭的時候, 牠的尾巴, 牠會往上動一下, 牠會抬起, 而且牠會刮到牆跟你的床。 你不會覺得牠會受傷, 你也不會覺得你的床會受傷。 但是一隻機器狗, 牠百分之百會讓你們兩個都覺得受傷。

那狗做小一點呢? 如果牠做得非常的小, 牠的能力級就非常的差。 It is light. Another problem is that it has a very short battery life. This is the first one.

The second one, I will say one more. If it is a twin-engine, with armor, and a size of 1.8 meters. Today, even if you are with it, you are its designer, you are not willing to be one centimeter away from it. It feels suppressed. You think it may be dangerous. You think it may be very hot. You may think he has electricity in his body. You may think he is dirty. Anyway, for some reason, you will keep a distance from him.

If even we adults do this, what will the elderly and children do? How should it solve the safety and legal problems in society? An industrial-based robot can make such a logic. Because it's not in the family or business itself. So many people go to industrial-based robots. He said I avoided the ABC problem. What I just said is the ABC problem. But industrial-based robots have triggered a new problem. Is that the ability to control is very strong. It will introduce a new problem.

I still look back. In the future, if robots enter the human society, they will have a lot of value in many areas, whether it's basic blue or white. They will definitely enter our lives. So we have chosen a very difficult path. So I myself think that many Chinese companies, whether in quality and safety, including recent sports conferences, we can see that just a little management will lead to such an interesting phenomenon. It's still in the mind of the boss to think logically. Why not do some mediation?

I think we are already in the middle stage. Our middle stage is that if you want to bring the body to the human world, we are already choosing the easiest middle stage. How tall should this person be? Look at our current generation of body, it's about 1.69 to 1.70 meters. It has to be comfortable for both men and women. Yes, and it can wear clothes, it can even have hair, but it can't have its own face. It has to be a certain distance from humans.

Q：为什么不能有面孔？

Why can't it have its own face?

There are some effects of the horror bone, and some other effects of law and social studies. So in fact, the robot that everyone is seeing now and the robot that the child is doing still have a big difference. I think we'll all wait until the second half of this year. I look forward to a very deep thinking, a very simple middle position, and then use the resources of the whole company, the resources of the whole small company to do a good job.

十六、剪开机器人的腿：那一晚的决断

At the press conference last year, you said you were very troubled. Do you want to prove that the truth is in it? Do you want to show everyone? Why are you troubled? What are you troubled about?

I'm not troubled. My team is very troubled. I think I should say it right away. My team said, the more you say it, the more people will think you have a problem. Because you will think that the pro is pro, the left is left. This is one kind of team view.
