Some are uncanny valley effects. There are also some other legal and sociological effects.

So actually — the Xpeng robot you see now, and the robot that Xiaopeng is making, there's still a big difference. I think we should wait until the second half of this year. I look forward to us thinking deeply about it. The simplest intermediate state made. Then use the power of the whole company. With all the power of Xiaopeng, we can do a good job. This is the current situation.

十九、为什么要剪开左腿？

Last year at the press conference, you said you were very confused. Do you want to prove whether there is a real person here? Do you want to cut it open and show it to everyone? Why are you struggling? What are you struggling with?

I don't actually care. My team is confused. I think we should say it right away. My team says the more you talk, most people will think there is something wrong with you. Because you will feel that those who are pure will become pure themselves. Those who are turbid will become turbid. This is a team perspective.

The second team view — can be further observed. Wait 24 hours. Let's take another look and see if I should say it.

I waited for hours. I can't stand it anymore. I said in China, this matter is being discussed in many places around the world. It's just that it has spread very quickly. I said when 24 hours later, maybe the bullet has flown to nowhere. Very simple. Because the people we develop and use next to — we all know it's definitely not a person, to be honest. The same goes for that robot. Its thermal management is problematic — it's hot. But it is just a milestone (stage) among us. Right. We actually know that later versions will be better.

So my dilemma at the time was — how do I make things work together internally? Because many people inside think that we don't have to say anything. Because we know it ourselves. But in fact, 99.99% of people in this world — there is no way to touch him.

I remember that night — it's me calling our team. I told you to come up with an idea for me tomorrow morning. I'm going to tell everyone this is a real robot. Instead of hiding inside alone. They were forced out by me. I added something temporarily in the morning. And then this happened. Indeed some people believe. There are also more people who don't believe it. And then we did it again. But the speed is very fast.

Why choose this method of cutting?

Because there are so many people online — say it in various ways. If you are A, you must be for this reason is a real person. If you are B, maybe that's the reason. We think the easiest thing is to remove its legs. And it's the left leg. Because everyone can see the left leg. It walks from left to right. The left leg is the easiest to see. This is a real robot. So we did something like this.

二十、爆款带来的产业信号

The explosive public opinion last year — what kind of signal does it bring to you? In the research and development of robots?

I think there are a lot of interesting results. We later looked at a lot of Chinese social media — their recognition of the value of the robot. Most young people want to join the family — help him with work.

I know — not posting on social networking sites, middle-aged men and women, just my age, even older than me — many people are considering: waiting until he gets old, what is the use of this robot? I think it's very different. It's just that I haven't experienced such a big point of view before. He does not have such a big data. I think — this is something to look at from different perspectives, right?

Have you changed the direction and process of your robot?

I think it actually accelerated. In fact, in the robotics industry, there are a lot of very good people joining. Xpeng robot is a — there is a very, very big difference from starting a car business. I can't share this with you yet. I think it will take three or four years. After many, many companies have stepped on the trap, everyone will see what is the difference.

I think it will be in another three or four years. You'll see robot competition — what kind of state will you fall into? Xiaopeng's robots have gone through many pitfalls. It's not something you can avoid just by understanding it. It is real after you step on it. Only then do you know its entire logic. So robot entrepreneurship — I even think it is far more difficult than starting a car business. In fact, it is already difficult to start a car business. I see it personally. Robot entrepreneurship is about 20–100 times more difficult than starting a car company.

So my own opinion is: even with Xiaopeng's ability, after the marketing of this robot appeared at the end of last year, I think this has increased our probability of success a little bit. I think it's very good, valuable.

So I still want to say the challenge of this matter for each company — it's very interesting, including ourselves. But it also has a very big impact. There are too many robotics companies now. I think it's much better than when you were making cars back then.

二十一、机器人公司远比当年造车公司多

Newly established car company — what do you think? Quantitatively, there were statistics when cars were built back then. At most, there were more than 300. But what? I feel like I'm at home with 100. There may be many families that were stillborn.

I doubt. I think robotics Inc. — it is said that there are more than 200 established ones now. In fact, it may be double (twice) than that of the car company back then.

But I still want to say it myself — robots are different from cars. Because the classification of the car may be passenger car — example: new energy vehicle, commercial vehicle or special vehicle or mini car. You see this is a classification, right? I think the classification of robots — far more than just seven or eight categories. There may be countless classifications of robots. For example, medical robots, right. In the robot system — a robot that specializes in freight transportation, or a cargo inspection robot. I think it's possible.

And in the logic of the robot, there is a very interesting feature. I think robots have a lot of robots — doesn't need to be humanoid. I agree with this too. It just means that we chose a humanoid robot that is the most human-like. We chose this path. So on this path, our Xiaopeng is walking. But there are many paths. I think we can also achieve differentiation and commercialization.

So change the angle. I think today's robotics companies — I personally think that if we adopt a universal humanoid robot, 99.99% will die. But there are various differences between robots. I think in this world there may be many solutions. That robot's winning rate is quite high. I think it's higher than a passenger car. This is my opinion.

Then why do you choose the most difficult path?

What you up to. What are you capable of doing. Do you think it should be done? Then you don't do anything — and how do you think you do it to have the best probability theory? You combine these five abilities. Isn't it your business or product strategy?

In your opinion, universal humanoid robot — who is your opponent?

I think universal humanoid robots have no rivals now. It's all about myself. Because every robot company — the most important thing is to make yourself the strongest. Your own ability is the organization from the bottom. Further up is infra. Then go up to your entire system — including system capabilities of technology, products and commodities. Then move on to your own project.

二十二、运动控制：机器人比汽车难得多

I think last year you robots — a core reason for being very out of the circle. It's because it's very anthropomorphic. What do you think is the most important thing behind this? What are some technical bets? And when it was made?

Yes, I can tell a small part. For example, give an example. I think most people underestimate it — motion control in robots.

In fact, in the automotive field, motion control is a — many people in the past hundred years, things that I think have been solved. So many passenger car companies make cars — they all buy from whom. Motor control abilities — go for portfolio integration. Robots in the past may have come from 2018 — some open source motion control NPCs. Wait for something like this. So why do they seem to have a lot to work with?

I think the final robot needs to be really ready — it should be completely better than the next generation of the car. Still need to be able to do better. Because the car actually consists of different control domains for single point control. It is not fully coordinated as a whole. It just means that it does an excellent job of bordering. So it can divide the labor well. You do A and I do B. The two of us have clear boundaries.

But actually, let's take an example. If a self-driving car — on the left tire is snow, when the right tire is on grass, how to make a 47.5 degree turn? This is difficult for people. Because there is snow on your left, on the right is the adhesion of the grass. Control of your entire four wheels, balance of the entire movement, overall delay management. If there is a car in front of you when you happen to turn, there's someone on the left — it is extremely easy to slide. Just rear-ended him.

So the motion control of cars today — what I want to say is that it is very good. But it is still far from being omnipotent enough. There is still a lot of room for improvement.

If we talk about robots — robots are still possible. I didn't even know it was a car from the 1990s. Maybe 1930. Cars from 1920 maybe — at that time, probably from the era of the Model T. Is the Model T available today? In robot — I think the Model T car must not be among the robots yet. But I think I'm just saying that from the perspective of chassis motion control, maybe it still belongs to that generation.

Back to your previous question — I think there needs to be a full pose personification. Motion control capable of full AI combination is extremely difficult. For example, give an example. All the robots you saw today — it is either going, otherwise it would be a fight. Its two motion controls may need to be adjusted. Then you said you want five motion controls — gone. Actually to a person, a person has more than 200 types of joints. His combination is an infinite loop. Like I'm two fists now, and he also has emoticons. Hands, eyes, feet, the synergy of your soul and emotions.

So I think it is — we are actually in the field of motion control. I really want to think like a human being. How can all movements be controlled with AI? Rather than using software control. And how can this control be balanced?

Just like human instinct — when a person learns to walk, learn to run, he has instinctive ability. When he saw a ground, there may be a snow field or grass — he will even try to walk, come and feel the friction and the whole body sensation. Maybe one with boiling water and a two-lot field with ice water. Both feet are walking at the same time.

So in the field of robotics — just to deal with various situations like this, all have to do thinking. Come and go thinking about motion control. So I actually think Xpeng's possible motion control — a bit like the next generation of cars. So this is also a certain angle why we think most robots — it was many years ago. Motion controlled car goes above. So naturally it can only be stitched using software. It is naturally difficult to create a good robot with comprehensive capabilities.

二十三、机器人对小鹏的意义：第二条增长曲线

What does robots mean to Xiaopeng? It is the distance of Xiaopeng?

We don't think so. We think Xiaopeng's new decade will have three curves.

  • 第一条曲线：汽车（Car） — Make the car a complete intelligence.
  • 第二条曲线：机器人（Robot） — I think the robot itself is an intelligent agent. In fact, the car still needs to prove. It will also take ten years. Proving software capabilities accounts for 50%. But I don't think robots need to prove it. If a robot has poor software capabilities, you basically don't want it. Of course, the hardware capabilities are very poor — you won't use it either.
  • 第三条曲线：全球化（Globalization） — In fact, there is a big difference between robots and cars. Most people ignore it. That is to say — once the robot has the ability to scale, its scale will be much faster than that of cars.

Cars in the past 100 years — it is gradually scaled up. Because it has roads to build. Secondly, there are traffic regulations to be implemented. The third — mass production is very difficult. For example, let's say you made a particularly good car today — 100,000 units sold in one day, sorry, you don't have the ability to build 100,000 units next month. Besides, you don't dare to go past three months. Really produce 100,000 units a month. Because you don't know if you can sell 100,000 units later. This is an inherent law of cars.

But I think robots are different. It's possible for robots to do extremely well at a certain point — remember it is a point, not the same face. I think it may have a very big explosive effect.

Like I think the biggest thing last year was in digital AI — it's Anthropic's coding. Very short time. That's probably less than 18 months — from the second half of the year before last to the end of last year. There will be huge changes. I myself think that robots are also possible. Although slower than it. Because it's still a digital world and a physical world. But it's still possible. Will it be slower than a car? I think if the robot has software capabilities, it can reach hardware speed — will far surpass the car.

Of course, change the angle. Because Xpeng is a car manufacturer — we make robots working with many companies to build robots. We develop 80% of our hardware in-house. Our hand chip joints — we all conduct self-research. It's just that we may cooperate with many companies, Tier 1 we may follow it, more upstream tier 2 cooperation. Only if this is the case — only then can you achieve good quality and scale.

Will you start mass production in the second half of this year? What do you think is the difficulty now?

There are many points, I think so. - Can the hardware be quite reliable and stable? - 第二，high-level — multiple large models, can it be a good fit? - 第三，another difficult issue is whether commercialization can be proven.

In fact, robots are full of pitfalls. So why is it said to be much more difficult than a car? You just need to build the car — assume there are no competing products, assume there is a user need. You must believe it is something that can be sold. But don't you dare say that to a robot.

Robots have not been clearly defined to this day. Yes, the market is also waiting. The market is waiting for you to have a similar — maybe a phone like iPhone 4 will appear.

But I believe that the first commercially produced robot — I don't think it's as good as iPhone 1. But the iPhone 1 is a tool after all, it can replace. Because at that time, there are many other mobile tools. There's Nokia, and there's Motorola, Sony Ericsson. But when robots may come out — many people have not done anything similar I think. So it's another thing that's not completely comparable.

What do you think your winning rate is? In such a long battle.

That depends on how big your goal is. Only then can you know what your winning rate is. I think so — we have about 20%.

20% — I think this is what I have seen in Chinese companies, I think it is the highest winning rate.

二十四、血海与蓝海：机器人会更快上岸

You were still telling me the year before last, building a car is like swimming in a sea of blood. What about making robots?

Not yet into the sea of blood. The sea of blood is still ahead.

But I think there is a big difference between robots — that's because the AI in the robot has very strong capabilities. And it turned out that the group of people swimming in the sea of blood — the person you swim with — in fact, most people don't know much about AI.

Today in the automotive industry, many people saying it's because I don't understand software. So I'm looking for a good tier 1. It works inside the car. It doesn't work inside the machine.

So the blood sea of robots — just like the Internet competition back then. Will soon go from blood sea to blue sea. Its progress will be extremely fast.

二十五、GX：把飞行汽车和机器人能力装进车

We were talking about robots just now. Then you will also release multiple new cars this year — including your GX. Do we want to talk about your product? For GX, Xiaopeng once again makes high-end products.

Yes, this is also the first time we have made a full-size model. A six-seater large SUV. That is our flagship — called flagship SUV.

Very interesting this year's Beijing Auto Show. There are about 30 of these new full-size models. I probably have a few models in my head. Xiaopeng actually does a lot in this — the difference with them.

第一个差异：把飞行汽车的冗余带进 GX。

The first time we were thinking — how do we put the car, many capabilities of flying cars and robots, in this new generation of smart cars, connected it. Let me give you an example. We put the inside of the flying car, redundancy of core flight components, brought it into this car. Of course I think this GX is the first in China. The OEM made a front-mounted Robotaxi. So it has eight (correction: six) full safety redundancies. In other words, you drive into the wild — if the power supply is broken, it can still be turned on. Even if the power fails, it can still be driven. The mouse chewed up the wiring harness and it was still open. It's like an airplane — it allows you to have redundancy.

第二个差异：线控底盘与 VLA 联动。

I think the second example is as an example. We put the car's wire-controlled chassis — follow our new EEA system — linked to our VLA's autonomous driving. It can make new chassis — can be performed during the execution of VLA — raise the lower limit. In other words, the security level is higher, shorter latency, control sensitivity — it can be improved by nearly dozens of percent actually. This is an example we gave.

第三个差异：把机器人的思考方式装进车。

Then the third one will be soon. We take the thinking inside the robot. The robot has to listen to a task. For example, Xiaojun asked you to help me get a bottle of water. That's a task. It first determines who you are — you are not my master, I don't listen to you. Then where is the water. What water do you want to drink. Where to get the water after you get it. Then how can I wait for you to do a series of things like this. Cars will be seen in the future, not too far away.

So we didn't talk too much about this to the outside world today. In fact, it itself is derived from robots. Of course it also comes from a lot of us — from the ability of the car. For example, take this car as an example. Its third row space can be completely bulldozed — become a two-row car. Or become a 2.5 row car. We call it Sizuo. We call it Sizuo. A car that can be used by anyone with five or six seats.

So actually you will see — it combines many of Xiaopeng's own abilities. Many of the capabilities of cars and robots. It's actually a very interesting fusion. Then we also added many of our partners. For example, we made privacy glass with Fuyao Glass. We made a new generation of car refrigerator with Midea. I think there are a lot of them — very interesting combination of abilities.

So actually we have always felt — make a big appearance, some parts are pretty, the interior is rough, then the details are not customized, then I put together a car with many capabilities. I think so. That's called the Sea of Blood — a certain angle. In fact, I saw a lot of cars like this at car shows. But how can you make it different? It requires a full dimension set. Every point — some people say my point is 60 points. That point is 80 points. Another point is 85. Maybe your worst point is 80. And then there are more 90 points and 95 points. Only then can you win this war.

二十六、GX 不会重蹈 G9 覆辙

How was this price determined? Think about it from the beginning, it's still final.

No car has a clear logic at the beginning. It must all be a plan. This plan depends on — what market do you expect to capture? What size do you expect this market to be? What kind of profit do you expect? In addition, in this market, what kind of market might there be for players?

I think it's about what time. And it's now a pre-sale. It should be May 21st. We will officially release. So at the press conference, you will see our clearer configuration and price.

Are you worried about repeating the same mistakes as G9?

I'm not worried. Because of the thinking and logic at that time — think about logic at this time. I think the difference is very big. Very many things. Judging from the past few years, if your ability reaches a higher dimension, you can probably analyze it all — where were the previous problems. And there is a high probability that — what is the problem now. And here we are today — it won't be the same as before. That is to say, the stronger your system capabilities are, the harder it is for you to fail easily at one or two points.

You personally said that you also like this car. And you want to build a car for a friend. What kind of people are these? Who is your target persona?

I think over 30 years old, relatively brave. Then many people do very well — some are entrepreneurs, there are some good managers, good professionals. They are willing to explore new technologies, experience the luxury of new technology. Logic that is similar to our taste. I think he was one of our first users. So our car — more expectations are for family-oriented technological luxury.

You drive out. Do you have any feedback, friends?

My friends actually like a lot of people. I will be in Beijing tomorrow, have a group of friends. They all made an appointment to take a look at our car. In fact, many people told me — he said: Xiaopeng, you should have made a car this big a long time ago. He said that although I and my family, I really want to use a big car. Then they may have been driven by someone in the past time. Has anyone ridden in other Xpeng cars? In fact, they are all looking forward to a bigger car — meet their needs.

二十七、和理想 / 蔚来：是友商也是盟友

How to face competition with ideals?

I feel like we are friends with everyone. They are also allies. Maybe a competitor. Today I wanted to go to his booth with Li — went to take a look. The new L9 is also a very good car, I believe. Yesterday I went to Li Bin's place to watch ES9. I think Wei Xiaoli is in the 9th series. This time, everyone will give their own different opinions and understandings.

What did you find out from the G9? And then use it in this generation model?

I think it's too much. Our organization also adjusts. Product planning adjustments. Our understanding of customers has also been adjusted. The business logic has also been adjusted. I think it's too much.

It's been three and a half years, right? From the end of 2022 to now. I think it's too much.

So confident?

I think this is a completely different ability level. A car is a very complex — a large integrated system of technology, humanity and management. So why is it said to be the pearl of manufacturing? So the deeper you study in this field, the deeper the summary. You will see many different points. In fact, in many small hardware, or consumer grade hardware — they are invisible. They handed over a lot of manufacturing. They have a lot of supply capacity. Rely on supply chain. The possibility of its combination is not high.

So in the automotive field — why I'm excited about the years from 2022 to 2026? I think it is a huge qualitative improvement.

If someone says that Xpeng's car is ugly — what do you think?

Every car says — when I meet other people, they say his car is ugly.

Aesthetics is a subjective logic. Do you think Xpeng's cars look good? I think it's on the way to becoming more and more beautiful. I don't have enough confidence in other things. I still have confidence in ugly beauty.

You said appearance comes first — inside. Appearance is a theory of relativity. It is only possible to be the first in multiple fields. Appearance is one of the areas.

Why not choose a strategy of making fewer but better models? Why are we making four cars this year?

I think every company has a path. You can't ask company A why it doesn't do less but better, company B is not big but comprehensive. There are different theories of logic.

You feel very confident overall this year? I think there is no one who is not confident in running a business, right?

二十八、CEO 时间花在哪里

Where do you spend the most time now? In the car, in the robot, in AI, or where?

Still organizing. Still purchasing. Participate in everything you say. But I don't think so. I think the most — I think it's still about strategy and planning. That's what the future is like.

Just like you asked a lot of questions just now — who is successful, etc. Maybe they will be more successful in the future. But today, from my point of view, it's not a success. Like inside a car — does scale count as success? Is one of the representatives of success. Not always successful. Does profit count as success? Some profits may be temporary — it's also painful.

So among entrepreneurs, the concept of success is very different. So change the angle. I may have spent more thinking — it's for the entire enterprise. Product and business strategy and planning issues. But these planning issues — it takes your technical questions, your organizational issues, your entire market problem, combination capabilities of other series. Only then can we finally do it. That's probably it.

This year for you, what is the most important thing?

No, are all important. Many are important. In fact, cars are a very complex problem. It is more than an individual or a clear enterprise. He said I just need to do this one thing well. Just because a car does something well doesn't mean it can do it well. This is a very painful question.

I just talked about longboard. Short boards have the same problem as wide boards. Many people will say that you should make a longboard. It's correct most of the time. But most of the time — this question is answered in the automotive field. It is a composite system. It's useless if you make one.

For example, let's say you do an excellent job in autonomous driving — is it possible to achieve level 4? First, generally speaking, I think who said it can be done this year? I think it's bragging. Secondly, I think even if he did it, nor does it have a huge value for it. Because its value must be reflected gradually.

So when do you think L4 will be implemented?

I most likely think 18–24 months for this — to you, or for the entire industry.

I think for us — what will it bring after implementation? Will it bring more sales of cars? Sales volume is 100% more. How much more sales? I need authentication. Even if there are more sales, it does not represent long-term value.

You see there are different issues here. For example — we last month, at the end of March, we released the first version of the second generation VLA. I recently shared the data with you at the auto show. China's car sales in April — the year-on-year decline was around 20%. We probably increased by 50%–70%. A considerable part of this is related to the second generation VLA. Is it good or not? I think it's good.

So if you do L4 — how many times can this ability be improved? Double? Five times, ten times? I think it's hard for me to judge. Is this good or bad? Secondly, what is it about society? What is the impact on the industry? Today we actually have a lot of — the views here are inconsistent.

What I still want to say is — the final success of the automobile company. It's not just an ability that comes from AI. Or a capability in the hardware. Or a capability in sales. I don't think so.

二十九、和余凯的分歧：自研还是用 Tier 1

Teacher Yu Kai (founder and CEO of Horizon) often says you are the most difficult user for him to defeat. He thinks car companies should eventually use third parties. What do you think of his point of view?

Classmate Yu Kai has a very good relationship with us. I thought it was interesting that he walked in a — very promising. But it may also be a challenging road. To see this world — in the end, will there be more or fewer car robot companies? The more I see, the broader Yu Kai's path will be. The less Yu Kai — the more painful the road will be.

Which one do you tend to think?

I might think it's getting more concentrated. If you look at something from the user's perspective — because we look at a thing from a business perspective, the user perspective may be just one of our dozens of perspectives. So from the user's perspective, everyone feels the need for more car companies. There are more products from friendly merchants to choose from. There are more different things.

But in this world — cars are too complicated. What if it was a simpler thing? I think it will be unified soon. What I'm talking about is Apple's mobile phone. I think — this doesn't sound very nice. Just in the last few generations, not much has changed on Apple phones. But it can still be very stable. Changes in the entire mobile phone market. Now I buy a mobile phone unless my phone is broken. Otherwise I have — not because of mobile phones which ability improves, I must buy a situation like this.

But cars are far from that point yet. Even I think — in the future, R&D expenses of 100 billion per year will be — continuously making great innovations. In such a field, I think in the past car companies, there are many companies that actually do not conduct in-house research. He said you go and visit these car companies. It does a lot of research and development. It cost a lot of money. There are many people in it. I said but it doesn't do self-research — it does integration. So you said it's wrong for them to integrate. You go and study on your own.

In fact, you will find that self-study is even more wrong. Because cars are too complicated. You have many, many things to develop. So you might as well say — there are some places where you don't have genes. You might as well find a partner. Looking for B to cooperate. That's right.

But change the angle — it depends on what your car's strategy is, or is it your tactic? If it is a tactic, you should not study on your own. If it is a strategy, you should do your own research.

Back to the ultimate question. What you asked at the beginning just now. For example, ten years later, our car software accounts for less than 50% of the value of the car. If you occupy one or two tier 1, can you help partners achieve 50%? Of course he (Yu Kai) hopes so. Right. I think — maybe maybe it can't be done.

We have something called cross-domain fusion in the car. It integrates many abilities together. There is hardware and there is software. Manufacturing, design, service, operation. These are completely different and related. Then you blend it together. Many people say — so how do you integrate this?

For example, give an example. Why do I hear so many words in the car — it's just putting different things together. Why is there multiple-in-one, n-in-one in software? It may combine dozens of capabilities into one. Taken together, the entire system is different. It is not something that any company can do.

For example, you can cooperate with three companies. It's easy to provide a total solution together. But sorry, you have to work with 300 companies. Provide a personalized solution. You will be in great pain.

Xiaopeng's path, both Yu Kai and Yu Kai's horizon road may be successful. I don't think I know today. I think Yu Kai has done a good job running Horizon Company. The product is also well made. First of all, I agree with this very much. But what I still want to say is — the strategic end of this path. In other words, what is the visible ending?

Do you think cars or robots — how many households will be concentrated in the end?

This is a multi-year cycle. I think different companies are very different. I won't talk about robots first. I keep saying 2030 — China may only have five large-scale automobile companies. It doesn't mean that other car companies will go bankrupt. But what I want to say — downturn in scale will make it more and more difficult entering this ultra-high-intensity competition.

三十、血海尾声：还没人上岸

Have you swam out of the sea of blood today?

I think they are all swimming.

Are you out yet?

No, I don't think anyone came out either. In my definition. First of all, don't ask me what my definition is. I don't think any of them came out.

The robot challenge will only be 10 times bigger, right?

I think robots would be slightly better. Because the possibility of homogeneous involution of robots will be low. First, its software value will be very great. There is no doubt about this. Secondly, I don't think there is a very good open source. Can help a robotics company make good software.

三十一、何小鹏的一天

What is a typical day like for you?

I think it might be the same as many entrepreneurs. Relatively speaking, I go to work later. Left very late. Then there will be many decision-making items every day. Most expectations are very fast. Communicate and think everywhere. This is a typical day.

What books have you read recently?

I don't really read books that are too specific right now. There are many books on my desktop. I might just turn one chapter and pass it. I think the rules summarized in the book — the current combination changes too quickly. It's hard to summarize. After he writes a book and prints it, the world may have changed by another 10%. Within the scope of his book, it is assumed. So I think reading books should be done in a more stable (mental) state.

How to absorb knowledge?

In fact, absorbing knowledge is the easiest. I think it is absorbed through practice. In the past, I summarized and absorbed knowledge by myself — read thousands of books, traveling thousands of miles, chat thousands of times, roll up sleeves thousands of times and practice.

I think the most important thing now is — many things you see, how to get on a big enough platform, make time for your quick cycle. The internal organization of the enterprise is doing PDCA. But in the AI system, it relies on a fully automatic cycle. You can't even do C in PTC. That is, it should be as low as possible — the consumption of any aspect of human life.

Have you made any regretful decisions in the past?

No. There is no need to think about regretful decisions in this world. Because I made too many mistakes. Why should you regret making a mistake? We often feel that — it turns out that something was done wrong at this time. Then let's think about why it's wrong now. But don't regret it.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反常识的发现：何小鹏在"用人脑的算力"做最贵的赌注
你注意到一个奇怪的悖论了吗？整场对话里，何小鹏反复强调一件事："我自己不用 AI Coding"、"很多计划不会跟人讨论"、"任何角度都只代表一个朋友的思考"。一个把 AI 押注到底层的人，自己的决策过程反而是**反 AI 化**的——高度依赖直觉、单点判断、不可复述。
这背后藏着一个隐秘的认知：**在物理 AI 这种没有先例可学的领域，CEO 的核心价值不是"决策正确率"，而是"承担孤独的能力"**。当你能讨论的人都只看到局部，讨论本身就变成噪声放大器。所以他的工作方式越来越像围棋顶尖棋手——不复盘、不解释、靠直觉落子。这与他大谈"AI 重塑组织"形成的反差非常有意思：组织被 AI 化，但**决策的最高节点反而越来越像前 AI 时代的工匠**。
## 二、跨领域连接：物理 AI = 缝纫机时代向纺织厂时代的跃迁
何小鹏反复用"缝合怪"（Stitch Monster）来描述传统智能驾驶。这个词其实精准击中了一个工业史的隐喻：
- 18 世纪英国的缝纫机改良了"做衣服"，但真正改变世界的不是缝纫机，是后来的**纺织

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] The Fragile Thing That Keeps Republics Alive – Ada Palmer
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

共和国作为"集体注意力的产物"

帕尔默与德瓦凯什的对话表面在谈意大利城邦，实际上揭示了一个反直觉的政治物理学：共和制不是制度建出来的，而是被"在乎"撑住的。马基雅维利的核心洞见令人意外——他不把共和国的存续归因于宪法设计、法律精巧或权力制衡，而归因于一种近乎心理学的变量：人民对"什么更重要"的排序。米兰新生的共和国不是被法国人推翻的，是被居民自己对内斗的执着推翻的。换句话说，共和国死于注意力的错配，不死于敌人的强大。

跨领域连接：海滩上的石头与生态学的"灭绝债务"

帕尔默用"海滩上的石头被一颗颗磨损"来解释为什么千年后还有共和国存在——这个比喻在生态学里有个精确对应词叫 extinction debt（灭绝债务）：一个生态系统看上去仍生机勃勃，实际上"该死的物种"已经在缓慢偿还过去栖息地破坏所欠下的账。文艺复兴的共和国就是这种"政治灭绝债务"的活样本：你看到的"还活着的几个"，是统计学意义上的幸存者偏差。这也解释了一个让历史学家头疼的悖论：为什么共和制在思想上越来越成熟、文献越来越丰富的时候，实际数量却在减少？——因为思想是从濒危物种身上抽取出的"标本"，越精致往往越接近灭绝。

一个让人不舒服的观察：派系仇恨其实是"自由的副产品"

最反直觉的一点：圭尔夫派和吉伯林派之间的仇恨，是在君主专制下被冷冻了一个世纪的。一旦自由解冻，仇恨先于自治苏醒。这意味着共和制对人类有一个隐藏代价——它会先把所有被压制的内部分歧释放出来，而能不能撑过这个"解冻期"决定了它能否长久。这个机制可以跨域映射到很多场景：

  • 后殖民独立：殖民统治结束后部族冲突反而爆发，常被误读为"他们不配自治"，实际上是被压抑的多样性的重新表达。
  • 企业创始人离开后：强权创始人离开的公司，内部派系会立即爆发，许多观察者归咎于"群龙无首"，但马基雅维利会说这是自由的诚实代价。
  • 个人心理层面：一个人摆脱了外部权威（父母、宗教、老板）后，内心冲突反而加剧——因为以前是被外力压住的。

所以共和国的脆弱性不是"民主太软弱"，而是自由先放出冲突、再考验你能否爱共和国胜过爱赢。这把一个老问题翻了个面：问题从来不是"你支持哪一派"，而是"当你的派系开始屠杀对方时，你愿不愿意为了制度本身去阻止你自己的同伴"。这是一个测试，而几乎所有共和国都没通过。

📝 转录文本

千年悖论：共和国为何还在？

So all of the Italian city republics are terrified constantly of monarchal takeover, and working on developing complex institutions and balances of power to try to prevent monarchal takeover.

The thing I find striking or weird is that you have a thousand years between the fall of the Roman Empire and then these republics are still standing.

Yeah.

And you would expect that if you become a monarchy, it's hard to become a republic again. It sort of feels like a one-way function. And so how is it for a thousand years?

海滩上的石头：两种解释

Two answers to that.

Yeah.

One is, at the beginning there were a lot more republics, and they go one by one — in the same way that rocks on a beach wear away a few at a time, and the largest and strongest stay. And you wonder, how are there these rocks on the beach? And the answer is: there used to be more rocks on the beach. There used to be more republics.

But the other half of the answer is, you do sometimes have things go from monarchy to republic to monarchy to republic, because people will hate the ruling family. And when they have the opportunity to rebel against the ruling family, sometimes what they'll institute is a republic. Often that republic will then be itself overthrown and another monarchy will develop. And so you have rapid alternation.

Interesting.

米兰的教训：维斯孔蒂之后的权力真空

Well, Machiavelli says — and I think his assessment in this is right — that it depends on how much the population of the city really cares about being a republic and is invested in being a republic.

So one thing Machiavelli got to watch from a distance was a moment when Milan was briefly freed from being a monarchy. Milan had been a republic. It then was taken over by a monarchal family. They ruled for quite a while, and then the last of the Visconti dukes died with a complicated will that sort of left power to his illegitimate daughter, but sort of left power to the king of Naples because he was having a row with his son-in-law.

And there was a power vacuum for a moment, and so the people had an uprising and declared a new republic. They made a new republic and they created a city council.

派系仇恨吞噬共和国

And then immediately one faction in the city — the Guelphs — wanted to persecute the other faction in the city, the Ghibellines. And these are two factions that had been in the city for hundreds of years, but hadn't actually been free to persecute each other under the monarchs for a century. But they still hated each other. And as soon as they had a republic, they immediately started murdering and massacring each other. And they cared more about murdering each other than they did about the Republic.

And so the one faction that was in power started massacring the other. The others said, "Oh no, we're going to be massacred. Quick — exiled son-in-law of the Duke, please come back and help us and restore the monarchy in return for saving our lives from being persecuted."

And they — this faction, Machiavelli says — both factions cared more about fighting each other than they cared about protecting the republic.

Yeah.

马基雅维利的诊断：共和国靠"在乎"撑住

And if instead they had cared more about protecting the republic, and had solidarity and patriotism, that republic, he says, might have survived. In fact, it would have been conquered by the French. But it might have survived longer than it survived. It might have lasted decades. And decades isn't nothing.

So Machiavelli is very prudent in observing that republican institutions are only as strong as the people's belief in them and the people's willingness to rise up and defend them. And if the people care more about something else — whether it's their side winning over another side, whether it's wealth and affluence; if people can be bribed by offers of wealth from those corruptly who want power — whatever it is, if people care more about other incentives than they care about the liberty of their republic, then Machiavelli says the republic will fall. If people are deeply invested in defending the liberty of the republic, the republic will stand.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 共和国作为"集体注意力的产物"
帕尔默与德瓦凯什的对话表面在谈意大利城邦，实际上揭示了一个反直觉的政治物理学：**共和制不是制度建出来的，而是被"在乎"撑住的**。马基雅维利的核心洞见令人意外——他不把共和国的存续归因于宪法设计、法律精巧或权力制衡，而归因于一种近乎心理学的变量：人民对"什么更重要"的排序。米兰新生的共和国不是被法国人推翻的，是被居民自己对内斗的执着推翻的。换句话说，**共和国死于注意力的错配，不死于敌人的强大**。
## 跨领域连接：海滩上的石头与生态学的"灭绝债务"
帕尔默用"海滩上的石头被一颗颗磨损"来解释为什么千年后还有共和国存在——这个比喻在生态学里有个精确对应词叫 **extinction debt（灭绝债务）**：一个生态系统看上去仍生机勃勃，实际上"该死的物种"已经在缓慢偿还过去栖息地破坏所欠下的账。文艺复兴的共和国就是这种"政治灭绝债务"的活样本：你看到的"还活着的几个"，是统计学意义上的幸存者偏差。这也解释了一个让历史学家头疼的悖论：**为什么共和制在思想上越来越成熟、文献越来越丰富的时候，实际数量却在减少？**——因为思想是从濒危物种身

item.title: [Latent Space] 🔬 The Bitter Lesson is Coming for Proteins - Alex Rives, BioHub
item.dc:creator: Latent Space
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最让我意外的观察：进化压力的「方向悖论」

整段访谈里最反直觉的一击，是 Alex 顺手提到的一个事实：抗体的进化压力方向，恰好与其他蛋白质相反。

绝大多数蛋白质在亿万年里被进化逼向"收敛"——同一个功能位点反复出现，比如 nucleophilic elbow 在毫不相关的家族里独立演化出几乎一样的结构。这就是 MSA（多序列比对）能给 AlphaFold 喂养信号的根本原因：进化在"投票"。

但抗体不一样——免疫系统的存在意义就是主动制造序列发散，去覆盖一个连进化都没见过的抗原空间。于是 MSA 范式在抗体面前几乎失效。这就解释了为什么 ESMC 这种"暴力覆盖整个进化空间"的世界模型，反而在抗体上比 AlphaFold 表现更好——它根本不依赖共识，它依赖的是对"可能性流形"本身的压缩。

换句话说：当数据的本性是"反共识"时，越是放弃归纳偏置的模型反而越占优。这是 Bitter Lesson 在生物学里一个非常隐秘但深刻的版本。

二、跨领域连接：Zellig Harris、Claude Shannon 与"细胞作为信息机器"

Alex 在访谈中两次跳出生物学，引用了两位语言学/信息论的祖师爷：

  • Zellig Harris (1954)：词的意义由它出现的上下文集合决定。
  • Claude Shannon：英语的熵 = 给定无限上下文后下一个字符的不确定度。

他真正在做的事，是把这套「语境即语义」的框架硬移植到生物学。氨基酸不是字符，它是被进化在无数蛋白质语境里"选择"出来的 token。然后他把同一逻辑往上推一层——细胞，也是一台"信息处理机"，基因被转录、蛋白被翻译、表型涌现，本质上和一篇文本被生成是同构的。

这里有个隐藏的洞见：他在押注的并不是"AI 能解决生物学"，而是"生物学其实是一个被错误抽象层级困住的信息论问题"。半个世纪以来，生物学家试图从第一性原理（物理、化学）自下而上模拟细胞，但 Alex 的立场是：抽象层级选错了——细胞不应该被建模为"分子的容器"，而应该被建模为"程序的执行环境"。

这和软件工程史上一个著名转折惊人地相似：编译器一开始也想"模拟硬件"，直到大家发现，正确的抽象层是「语言」而不是「电路」。

三、藏在数据策略里的范式翻转：PDB 时代结束了

最容易被错过的一点：Alex 在回答数据问题时，悄悄宣布了一个时代的终结。

过去半个世纪，蛋白质数据库 (PDB) 是怎么来的？每一个晶体结构都是一个博士生几年的命，每一个数据点都是为某个具体的科学假设而生的副产品。AI 是在"拣别人的剩饭"。

但 Biohub 的 Virtual Biology Initiative 投了 5 亿美元，第一次反过来——不是为了某个特定问题去采集数据，而是为了训练一个能预测任何未见干预的世界模型，去专门采集数据。

这意味着实验生物学的"目的论"被改写了： - 旧范式：实验 → 发表论文 → 数据偶然累积 → 训练模型 - 新范式：模型的盲点 → 设计实验 → 采集数据 → 反哺模型

这其实就是把 LLM 后训练阶段的「RLVR / 主动学习」反向投射回湿实验室。Alex 自己也说了一句几乎被埋没的话：「something like RLVR, but with experiments」。

这是一个尚未被命名的范式，但它意味着：未来 10 年，最有价值的生物学实验，可能不是"为了发 Nature"做的，而是"为了填补 ESM6 的损失函数"做的。这种"为模型而做的科学"，对整个学术激励体系都是一种结构性挑战。

四、彩蛋观察：他的「不耐烦」是一种系统性信号

注意 Alex 在两个地方流露的微妙不耐烦： 1. 谈到 ESM3 引入归纳偏置时，他几乎是在为"绕了点路"辩护。 2. 谈到 virtual cell 现有模型时，他直接说"它们是好的数据表示，但泛化能力极差"。

这两处合起来揭示了他的真正信念：任何"看起来合理"的归纳偏置，都是在透支未来的可扩展性。他不是在做研究，他在做对赌——赌"暴力 + 数据 + 压缩"会再一次羞辱所有手工设计的精巧方法。这正是 Sutton 当年写《The Bitter Lesson》时的语气。

所以播客题目里的「is coming for proteins」其实带刺：它不只是"即将到来"，它是"即将清算"。

📝 转录文本

开场：ESMC 与可编程生物学

So ESMC is also approaching programmable biology, but I would say in a very different way. It's approaching it from this kind of world modeling perspective where the idea is basically you have a predictive model and you know you're going to search the world model to find protein molecules that satisfy kind of whatever design criteria that you have. So we've been able to use this to actually now go and design many protein binders. But I think sort of most excitingly, we've been able to use this to actually design antibodies, SCFVs.

嘉宾介绍

Hello, welcome to the latent space AI for science podcast. I'm R.J. Haneki, CTO of Muromix.

Yeah. And, uh, I'm Brandon today. It's a pleasure to have Alex Reeves, uh, head of science at Biohub. Yeah. Would you like to introduce yourself real quick?

Yeah. Yeah. Thank you for having me here. It's great to be here. Um, I'm head of science at Biohub. I'm a computer scientist uh and I work on AI for biology and a lot of my work has been on language models for biology.

Bitter Lesson 与蛋白质生物学的 Scaling Laws

By the time this podcast is released, you will have put out several new exciting interesting models. Going over them, I couldn't help but have the kind of thought that you might be the most bitter lesson person in protein biology right now. Can you give a little context about what that means for biology and you know why you're so committed and excited to this route?

Well, I'll take that. Um, I believe in scaling laws. So, you know, I guess I've been working on this for, you know, since the summer of 2018. Um, and so my team when we were at Meta FAIR trained really the first transformer language model for protein biology. And so I guess you know I I've always thought that there would be kind of emergence of biological information as you train a model to predict the next token that evolution creates. So our team has really explored that idea over a number of different years and we've really kind of I think seen the scaling curve and really seen as we have increased models by an order of magnitude kind of in each generation that you know there's this emergence of new capabilities.

为什么蛋白质语言模型会 work？

Yeah. So you've been you say emergence of capabilities scaling over generations. You've been working at this as you said for I guess it would be 8 years now or something like that. It didn't always work that way right like there was signs that scaling might work. You know we'll be getting to some new results where I think really you've kind of clearly demonstrated this hypothesis in a way that hasn't happened before. But you seem to have like a strong commitment to this in a way that I'm not necessarily sure I would have been so convicted that it would work in the same way. I mean proteins are not the protein language is not the same thing as natural language. There are similarities but if you start sampling a transformer at you know a normal language transformer at temperature you're going to get gibberish. you sample a protein language model at infinite temperature, you're going to get something which is a valid protein if not a not interesting protein despite the fact that is a different domain for a different reason. I'm not necessarily sure that I would I primarily assume the natural language model insight would transfer over. So what is specifically about proteins that you thought was special or you you know that would make this also valid?

Yeah, I mean it's a really interesting question. I think kind of a deep question across AI right now more broadly and you know I think you know what's what's so interesting is AI right now is is such an empirical science and so we don't have you know theory that can always guide us in these things but we have this really strong empirical evidence of scaling. The thing that I was motivated by is you know if you think about evolution and you know you think about the data that we we have around proteins we have databases that have billions of protein sequences. And you know, those sequences contain patterns and you know it had had been long been known so that you know this is going back you know decades kind of before you know we started working on this with language models — but that there are patterns the sequences of protein families that come there because of the constraints that evolution is operating under.

So you can think about, you know, like a um a protein sequence that folds into a three-dimensional structure in space. And you can, you know, imagine that there are two residues or amino acids that are in this sequence that might be in contact in that folded structure. And so evolution isn't free to choose those independently from each other. If it makes a choice at at one position, it kind of has to make another choice that's going to be compatible at the next position. So going back, you know, all the way to the beginning of gene sequencing when people first began to be able to look at this and kind of look at different related, you know, the same protein and related organisms, you could start to see these kind of patterns that are reflecting the fundamental underlying biology.

So the idea behind ESM, kind of the thinking behind ESM was, okay, what if you were to apply this principle of across all of evolution, across kind of the vast diversity of proteins that have been generated across all of life and, you know, basically have a language model kind of predict the amino acids that evolution will choose to place in proteins across all of those biological contexts. So you can think that there's just this kind of like incredible amount of information in that total picture about the underlying biology of proteins. And so that was really the idea that sparked this is is you know as as a model is having to predict the next token — and actually we train these models with masked language modeling, so they're predicting kind of tokens that are masked out of various parts of the sequence — that it would have to learn something about those kind of underlying constraints that are shaping which tokens evolution can choose.

ESM 系列的演进与 ESMC Cambrian

Yeah. So maybe for a bit of history um so you know you have you you just released um evolutionary scale modeling Cambrian, right? Is that what it's called? Yeah. And this is like the maybe fourth or fifth in a series of models. I think maybe even more if you go back before they were called ESM.

Well, they they were called ESM from the start. Yeah. We had sort of various branches of the different models. Yeah. So, so this one I would say is is kind of a a fourth generation model. Um it's actually a model that we trained a little over a year ago. Now that we're at Biohub, we're um we're we're open sourcing this this model fully under MIT license for the first time. So, we're really excited to do that.

But kind of the big thing that is new here is that we've really kind of built a world model of protein biology. So the foundation of that is ESMC. But you know using the representations of ESMC, we've kind of now built a a structure prediction model. Um and this is the next generation ESM fold model. And then we've also used the techniques of of of mechanistic interpretability and sparse coding to really start to look deeply into the representation space of the language model and kind of be able to pull out the underlying features that the model actually uses to represent protein biology.

So bringing all of this together, we're able to, you know, really make predictions for protein structure, um predictions about kind of the underlying features that that proteins are made out of that allows us to build linkages across evolution. We're able to take this model and invert it to design proteins. And we've we've we've used this to kind of create a comprehensive picture of protein biology. So we we put together kind of all the world's largest protein sequence databases. And so that kind of amounts to 6.8 billion non-redundant proteins. And then we've we've resolved predicted structures for 1.1 billion of those. And and we've also computed features across all of those so that we can make these linkages basically all across um evolution and protein biology.

6.8 亿 vs 1.1 亿：聚类与覆盖

6.8 billion of which you've resolved structure for 1.2 is that

1.1

1.1. So what about the others?

Well, so so basically what we did is we took that database and we clustered it at 70% sequence identity. So it's it's really resolving structures for everything in the sense that for each cluster we kind of have a cluster center. We're predicting the structure there and then we can expect that the other proteins are going to have a similar template structure. There be be small variations but they have the same fold.

1.2 billion or so clusters

that are that are kind of covering the 6.8 billion. Yeah.

Okay. Interesting. And yeah, maybe since we're talking about scaling, how do you know that um this is the right number, right? Like uh how do you know that focusing on these 1.1 billion and that's the right resolution for this model?

Well, we've chosen them so that they really cover that entire space. So, I think what I can say about this database is it's really the most comprehensive picture of protein structure and function that's been created. It's adding, you know, hundreds of millions of structures to our knowledge of of kind of protein the diversity of protein structure and it's also creating this uh feature space that allows us to find these linkages between proteins across evolution. So we can see kind of really interesting themes emerging across evolution. you know linking for example um gene editing systems which are very far apart in sequence but you know they share some kind of underlying functional um patterns structural homology that the model's able to bring together and find those connections.

机制可解释性：SAE 与特征层级

Now we're talking about the mechanistic interpretability part so you have if I understand correctly you use sparse autoencoders and other techniques maybe to understand okay what are the when I activate the network using a protein then what are the patterns of outputs that I'm seeing and how do they relate to each other. If I understand correctly is that you have these sequences that are unrelated or only partly related based on the actual sequence but in terms of behavior they have similar behavior and therefore they are activating similar networks. Is that kind of the summary of what you just said?

Yeah. So I mean basically what we've done is we've trained sparse auto encoders across all the different layers of the ESMC model family. So there's actually three models in that family. There's a 300 million parameter model, a 600 million parameter model, and a 6 billion parameter model. And then we've looked really we've done kind of a very deep analysis of the feature space of that 6 billion parameter model, which is really the state-of-the-art protein language model.

And so what we find what's really interesting is there's there's kind of this um you know this hierarchy of features that emerges. What's really interesting about it is it's it really kind of corresponds to the reductive picture of biology that has been developed over you know many decades a century of of um biological experiments. But but what's so so cool is this is emerging you know without any prior knowledge. It's been learned by the language model.

So the interesting thing about SAEs, right, is they're really just revealing the intrinsic structure of the representation space. So this model's been trained on protein sequences. It's been trained just to predict the amino acids that evolution will choose. And then you know somehow this is leading to the emergence of this like kind of very ordered feature space that has this hierarchical structure where you can really see everything from the basic biochemical properties and kind of the basic structural building blocks of proteins to these very large kind of functional themes, these kind of abstract concepts that you know connect to to how kind of the human picture of of protein function.

Nucleophilic elbow 与压缩、潜变量

And do you have a hypothesis or feel for why if there are relationships between the sequences themselves even if they're like shifted and and cut up and recombined in different ways like I can imagine that might work because you have these you know proteins are kind of hierarchical in their nature as well. So maybe the hierarchy moves around but the same sequence but the functional units I guess those have related structures. What is the hypothesis here?

I mean it's it's a really interesting question, right? I think I think I can speculate about it. I don't think we we kind of completely understand this, right? But let me give a concrete example. So, you know, the nucleophilic elbow is this kind of like core kind of functional motif that people have thought that you know maybe this actually has emerged independently in evolution, you know, different times in different protein families. But it has this you know very very clear um structural motif that you can kind of see in a in a crystal structure. You know what we found basically is that the model has a kind of a single feature for this nucleophilic elbow and it's activating across these like very evolutionarily diverse families. You know really completely different structural topologies — proteins that probably evolve like entirely independently from each other. But you know the model is kind of using this one feature to represent that.

So why does it do that? I mean I I think it's a really interesting question. I mean, I think one answer is sort of the idea of of compression and the idea that, you know, the model needs to have some kind of underlying latent variables that it develops to help solve this kind of sequence prediction task because the nucleophilic elbow, you know, it's going to be a function of what's so what's so interesting, right, is the choice of any amino acid is kind of like completely entangled with the choice of all the other amino acids in the sequence. So this is a very complex task to try to predict what amino acids should be where in a protein. But to really do this well, you know, the model would start to have to have these kind of hidden variables that are representing the biology that allow it to, you know, look look at a protein and say, okay, what amino acids should be there in all these different contexts? So, you know, that's sort of I think the intuition.

Zellig Harris 与"语境即语义"

I mean, I would draw the parallel to language modeling, right? And so I guess I was I was like very influenced by a paper by um uh Zellig Harris. It's called Distributional Structure from from 1954. And I think that a paper that influenced a lot of people in the language modeling field as well, you know, but I I think it has so it focuses on language and it really articulates this idea that um the set of contexts in which a word appears are determined by the meaning of that word. And so what Zellig Harris kind of imagined is that you know it would be as you looked at the statistical patterns of you know what words appear in what context sets you would be able to derive the meaning of language. You know you would you would have this kind of statistical structure that would mirror the underlying meaning of language.

For me at least, that's one of the most convincing explanations for why, you know, a language model that's trained on the text of the internet is going to learn something about meaning. It's going to learn something deeper and more fundamental. And so so I think you know you can think about the same thing in biology where the contexts in which an amino acid can occur are really determined by you know the structure the function of the protein its biological roles you know these I mean very complex phenomenon u both the intrinsic biology of the protein and its relation to all of the other proteins and the function and evolution. And so but but those are what determine the context sets and so you would imagine that then those statistical patterns in the use of amino acids, they directly reflect those underlying hidden variables and so the model is going to learn something about those hidden variables.

数据偏差 vs Bitter Lesson

I definitely buy that — seems plausible. Um maybe just I mean I in fact I I want to clear I actually do really believe in this direction but there are a lot of like ways I think about this where maybe I could say maybe I would imagine maybe it wouldn't work and one of them is like data availability right. Like what type of data do we normally uh have what type of sequence data do we normally get. I think that ESMC in particular has some new data sources compared to like previous models which might be helpful but often times the type of sequences we have available have like a very strong bias towards certain specific needs for medicine or you know human biology or disease biology right. So it's not necessarily that if you take just a naive data set you're going to necessarily get an interesting scaling law. So I'm curious about like what in particular was the sort of breakthrough in ESMC. So I mean maybe we can go back a bit and talk about some of the other ESM you know predecessors which got here before ESMC and how like you know they were, you know, their strengths but also maybe some of the limitations that ESMC overcame and like what the developments there were.

Yeah. Well, I I'll admit that I am I am better less in film. I am scaling film and so

I do think that I mean you know just just kind of increasing the data and increasing the parameters and having that compression is is going to just lead to more powerful models. But you know it is also true and I think you're you're absolutely right the structure the underlying kind of structure and distribution of the data is really critical and so you know some data sets will be far more valuable for kind of learning these these general principles than than others.

But I think it goes against a lot of biological intuitions about collecting data I guess is what I'd say because normally when you think about what data do you want you're trying to answer a very specific scientific hypothesis you want you know a very well-controlled experiment you know, you really want multiple replicas, you know, it's it's something that's very focused, you know, is the way that I would put it. So, I think the change in the way of thinking is to think, okay, what you really want if you want to learn a general representation of proteins is you want to see amino acids in as many evolutionary contexts as possible. That's really what you want. That's really kind of how I think about data.

ESM2 → ESMC：从 compute-bound 到 data-bound

And I think if you what changed between ESM2, which was kind of the previous generation model, and ESMC, which is this new generation model, because they're both at the approximately the same scale.

The same scale of compute.

Same scale parameters. Yeah, ESM2 got a lot of compute, but ESMC got even more compute. But it's not just the compute. The data was was really the critical thing here, actually. So when we trained ESM2 we observed two things. The first was that as we increased the number of parameters and compute you know we saw improvements. So we had a model kind of at the billion parameter scale. We had a model at the 10 billion parameter scale and you know the larger scale model is better than the smaller scale model. But if you kind of look at a plot of of uh parameter scale, you know, sort of a log plot of parameter scale versus capability — and so for capability, you know, we're looking at kind of the representational fidelity, how well does it capture protein structure — you could see that there's there's kind of diminishing returns in ESM2. ESM2 is trained on UniRef. And for ESMC, we added metagenomics. So we added billions more sequences to the training data.

什么是 UniRef、什么是 Metagenomics

Could you explain what UniRef and uh metagenomics mean?

Yeah. Yeah. So UniRef is um I'd say sort of the the gold standard data set of sequence biology is kind of you know taking sequences from across a wide variety of different sequencing resources. It's clustering them you know to kind of remove some of this redundancy that that you were mentioning. and and so it kind of creates a definitive coverage of of of protein biology.

What has happened in parallel to the classical gene sequencing is is this idea of metagenomic sequencing where people go out into, you know, all kinds of different biomes and environments and collect samples from the world and just kind of sequence the the natural diversity that's present there. So, you know, proteins from a hydrothermal vent or proteins from a from a a frigid environment near the South Pole, you know, or or the deep ocean or, you know, soil or the human gut, you know, all all kinds of different environments.

So, this is a very different way of collecting data. Instead of you are trying to understand a specific genome of a specific organism or trying to understand a specific protein, you just collect a bunch of stuff, mix it up in a pot, get the sequences out. You have no idea what organisms these are from. You don't necessarily even know if a given sequence is a protein, but you can guess based upon certain contexts and you say, "Okay, we throw these together. These are likely protein sequences we find. We're not assigning them to an organism. We're not assigning them to like a larger context. We're just saying this is probably a protein. Let's train on it."

That is right. Yeah. And you you don't even get the full genomes. You just get these kind of contigs that often are broken and have even partial proteins. So, the data is really noisy.

测的是 DNA 还是 蛋白质？

One more like little nerdy question that I have here is that if I understand correctly, you're not actually looking at using a device that sequences proteins. You're sequencing the DNA that would manufacture those proteins. So, you're finding DNA and then looking for markers that indicate the beginning and end of a protein sequence. Is that kind of

Yeah, that that's exactly right. Yeah. Basically sequencing, you know, genetic sequences and then you translate the the proteins from those sequences.

So you're digging up like sewers and not you but

me personally there are sewers like probably many York City subway all kinds of things.

蛋白质宇宙到底有多大？

Yeah. So the natural question to me is so you built this model and you think that you've kind of deduplicated it so that you have a good representational set without a lot of redundancy in it. How much more is there? Like if we had an order of magnitude more resources, do you think that there is an order of magnitude more proteins to discover?

I think so. I I I'm not entirely sure, but there are a lot of proteins and um I I think we've we've barely scratched the surface of measuring Earth's biodiversity. So there are core proteins that are conserved across all of life. So we we I think know that, right? But but as you go into these different environments, there's just, you know, new new genes and no new proteins constantly being created by evolution.

And this is a lot of my understanding is a lot of this is viruses and bacteria and other microorganisms or so those and these guys are in this basically long-running conflict with each other that causes them to recombine their DNA in ways that help them to survive in these extreme or whatever environment. And so that that's what's causing this incredible diversity of proteins.

That's right. Yeah. And just 4 billion years of of life running experiments in parallel all across the earth in all kinds of different ecological niches. And we just we see the outcome of all of that.

And so the combinatorial that's why you believe that yeah there's there's going although that maybe from a macroscopic perspective when we look at it there's maybe not that not even nearly as much diversity as there will be at the microscopic scale because you have this incredible combinatorial effect.

Yeah. I mean there's just I think tremendous tremendous diversity there.

ESMC 的 Scaling Law 与 Vanilla Transformer

So so kind of going back then there.

Yeah. I know it's it's great, right? Yeah. So there's kind of this dual view of biology, this dual complimentary view of biology. One one view is is kind of that kind of first principles reduction, you know, where where kind of all of biology is explainable in, you know, more basic terms in in basic physical, chemical, biochemical terms. And I think historically there's a long research line of research that's really sought to to understand biological phenomena and simulate biological phenomena in that way. So I think I think historically it has been productive to understand biology through information theory through information and you know you can think about the cell as a computer as an information processing machine. Informational terms, there are, you know, these very basic principles that link the information coded in the genome to the genes that are transcribed to the phenotypes of the cell that will result. And so, you know, if we could model and understand the cell at the level of its underlying programs, you know, that sort of gives, I think, the right abstraction.

And so you know what would that predictor be for biology and that's kind of the idea of of ESM — it would learn kind of the the the underlying structure of of all biological phenomena. And so it's kind of similar to the principle of training a language model on the internet or training a protein language model across all of evolutionary diversity. What does that look like for cellular biology? And so we have to scale interventional biology. So that looks like things like perturbation biology — Perturb-seq measurements where we can look at combined transcription imaging other layers of the cellular information hierarchy and there you know number of groups our teams are working on this there are a number of groups across the scientific world that are working on problems like this that are ready I think to scale.

原则三：空间生物学

The second is spatial biology. I think that's going to be really important. And so that's going to help us to really understand the cell in context and I think you know understanding the cell in isolation is is really not what we need it's not the goal right. The cell is part of an incredibly complex system in the body and you know to be able to understand disease we have to understand how cells interact the systems that they form the circuits that they form. So we need to see that. So spatial biology I think is undergoing rapid progress and you know is an area that's really ready to scale up. That's kind of what can scale now.

And um Biohub has actually over the last 10 years really I think made kind of pioneering funding commitments in those those areas. And so we've we've funded efforts like the Human Cell Atlas and we've built uh Tabula Sapiens which looked at uh built built large cell atlases and we've built CELLxGENE which is kind of a database of single cell transcriptomics. And so we're you know we're we're really looking to kind of build on that. And you know I don't know how many cells there are in kind of the the largest efforts — we're probably around like a billion cells or something like that today. So that we've got to go you know multiple orders of magnitude from that.

So you know that involves scaling the technologies that we have now but it also involves you know a new the next generation of technology. So we're also funding and supporting efforts in that area. And there you know we really want to look more at cross modality. You know can you simultaneously see the phenotype observe the transcriptional layer understand what's happening proteomically link that to the genome. You know we'd like to and the epigenetic state. You know we'd like to be able to see all of that. And so really pushing technology to be developed faster that can reveal more of those connections and more of that biology and do that in a more scalable way.

数据采集的 scaling 与 assay 的折中

It's interesting because when I hear most of those ideas, they're often times the things that people already think about in terms of scaling biology. What is the next technology that is going to allow for like enabling data collection technology? Um going back to the theme of bitter lesson for biology. You don't have just scaling laws on you know compute and parameters but now the scaling laws probably in data collection in some meaningful sense. Where are the next big opportunities there like in true. So you're talking about developing new technology is sort of like the um as part of this initiative.

Yeah. So I mean I think it's basically the things that I'm saying is scaling what we have now kind of being able to expand the number of interventions that that we can look at expand the number of parameters that we can measure. So really kind of more and more multi-dimensional measurement um and you know drive down the cost and all of that. Better gene sequencing kind of better ways of encapsulating cells and being able to measure what's happening not just the transcriptome but but other layers simultaneously.

There's an interesting Pareto frontier there about uh if you have a fixed budget, how much time do you spend on improving your assay versus how much do you spend on actually scaling it? You know, where do you uh weight out there?

We have to do both of those things, right? Cuz I think with current technology, you know, we can definitely kind of get data 10x to 100x where it is today with like relatively reasonable investments, you know, but but then to get another 10x or more there, that's going to require require a lot more technology development.

But the other the other really big principle is going to be feedback. And so I think that's going to be really critical. And I think you can see that as a layer of technology development that's going to need to occur. And I think there's a lot of great things happening right now kind of automation, flexible robotics that's going to accelerate um where where that can go and experimental design as well.

瓶颈：Compute 与数据

So we typically ask our guests what is a bottleneck that you would remove that would you know sort of unlock things but we just spent a long time talking about that.

Yeah I answer that question.

So, but I want to ask it but I'm going to give it a spin which is like so maybe a little bit outside of your domain like so language modeling or supply chain something that is a bottleneck that is maybe nonobvious and not directly something that you are working on but that maybe has impact on the work of biology or Biohub in particular.

I mean it's a hard question to answer because there's just so many bottlenecks. I mean the one that you know that I always think about is compute but I think that's a pretty obvious one. It's the bottleneck for all of AI in many ways right now. But, you know, especially because we're training these large scale models, you know, our we're always focused on compute. And I think we're you kind of limited both by the data and compute. I think we're in a you we're in a position where I think we have, you know, incredible compute resources for a team working in biology. But I think like like all teams working in AI right now, really the limit is is just how much compute power you...

So if you could 100x your compute, you think that ESMC would like be way better?

I mean, it would definitely be way better. We also need to scale data. So both of those things would have to happen in tandem.

Have you basically exhausted what's available right now for

I don't think so. No. No, I don't think so.

Okay. The large data sets out there or you could

well I mean more parameters, you know. So we trained ESMC up to six billion parameters.

Oh, but I'm saying in terms of data available, like have you exhausted most of what's publicly available in terms of like

No, no, not not yet. And you know, the atlas that we just built actually has more sequences and structures than ESMC was trained on.

So definitely have a little room to go.

So like what's is that an order of magnitude jump or twice as much? Like how does that how does that work?

Yeah, I mean I think the SMC is trained on you know say order a billion sequences. So there's there's definitely probably order 100 billion sequences.

This is large a lot of them are largely redundant.

100 billion.

Yeah.

Oh, okay. To get that billion, you whittle down from six billion 6.8 billion. Right. So of those 100 billion, if you were to similarly cluster and find unique ones, what do you think? Where do you think it would land?

The sequences aren't actually redundant, right? It really depends on what you mean by redundancy because there's I think a tremendous amount that you can learn from small genetic variations, right? Cuz these are these are really revealing of you know kind of the the very basic determinants of protein structure and function at a very fine level. So I think that you know as we think about protein space you know having a vast diversity of sequences across a wide range of protein families is you know really critical for the emergence of this kind of structure prediction capability because I I think kind of large diversity is what trains the model to understand to develop a representation of structure but I actually think that to develop a representation of function it's these very small variations that are important. And so I I do think that there is probably a lot more you know it's like the models haven't yet been trained at that level of kind of just like really deep understanding of these very small but critical patterns in sequence. I mean a single a single mutation is enough to destroy the the function of a protein.

So you could you could conceivably actually take all 6.8 billion of those retrain everything's the same but

yeah you could train on more than that there even I mean that's kind of clustered down so

yeah maybe the question is how far wouldn't you hit the law of diminishing returns here. I mean it sounds like you have plans for an ESM4 or an ESM whatever you want to developing.

Yeah but I'm just wondering it's you know at some point is this actually something that you could exhaust you know people talk about exhausting the pre-training data in on

Yeah. At some point. Yeah. At some point.

Yeah. But it's not actually something you could conceivably imagine doing in the next few years or even if you don't exhaust it, you hit a lot of diminishing returns for you know the applications that you're trying to predict here where maybe your resources are better spent somewhere else.

I mean it's it's basically it's an empirical question, right? It's truly an empirical question.

And so we just we just don't know, you I mean with ESM2 we weren't sure because there were some diminishing returns. With ESMC you know now now there aren't right. So you can kind of look extrapolate from from the scaling law there and you know there is enough data to train that next model.

Call to Action

And the other question that we usually ask is any call to action or what what do you want people to go take action on if the listeners want to get involved, get hired, get build things. What would you ask people to do?

Well, we just announced or or I should say we are we are going at the time that this podcast comes out, we will have announced ESMC and this world model for protein biology. Um, it's going to be open source. It's it's going to be MIT licensed and we want people to use it. You know, we want this to be a tool that can unlock science. We're excited to collaborate. We have a team that that works on that and we want to hear from people and understand, you know, what what we can build that can help to accelerate their science.

Yeah, we might have a um a demo slash paper club of some sort on this channel. So stay tuned.

Yeah, stay tuned for that. We'll we'll invite you and your team um whoever can make it. We'll feature this paper once it's in final preprint form and spend some time on it for an hour on the latent space paper club.

Yeah. Uh, thanks for chatting with us.

Awesome. Yeah, great to meet you guys.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最让我意外的观察：进化压力的「方向悖论」
整段访谈里最反直觉的一击，是 Alex 顺手提到的一个事实：**抗体的进化压力方向，恰好与其他蛋白质相反**。
绝大多数蛋白质在亿万年里被进化逼向"收敛"——同一个功能位点反复出现，比如 nucleophilic elbow 在毫不相关的家族里独立演化出几乎一样的结构。这就是 MSA（多序列比对）能给 AlphaFold 喂养信号的根本原因：进化在"投票"。
