二十六、AI Native 产品的三个阶段

Where is the product of AI Native?

I'm thinking that AI is a bit like an alien. It came to the human world. There are several stages in this process.

The first stage is to allow humans to have more and better agents. For example, three years ago, there were no agents in human society. Now there are some agents, but there are not enough agents. So how can we let more people have good agents? I think no matter it's Cloud Code, Codex, Open Cloud, Manage, Hermes, they are all doing this. Installation is getting easier and easier. Then you can manage it more and more easily. I think this is the first step, which is to let humans have more and better agents.

The second wave is to make the agents in the human world better adapted to the human digital world. Because the human digital world is basically designed for humans. For example, GUI, verification code, credit card, etc. AI is usually the target of the host. It's supposed to stop bots. Now we have a bunch of good bots. AI can effectively use human software and technology to make it more important.

For example, we all find payment is a problem. So, like Stripe or Coinbase, they want to send human credit cards to AI. For example, Cloudflare used to block AI. They blocked AI outside. They recently launched a service that allows agents to register and use various services equally with people. Then, for example, various computer use, various sandbox, and sandbox, in fact, AI can use human-made software. It's like a foreigner coming to China. He needs to have a SIM card. You need to get him a WeChat account. You need to get him a Alipay. This is a way for agents to stay and use human-made facilities.

But when the number of agents is increasing, when the number of agents is becoming more important, there will be a special digital world tool and its infrastructure. For example, humans use credit cards, but human payment has some characteristics. Human payment is relatively low-value, it is difficult for you to pay 10,000 times a day. Human payment is usually point-to-point, I pay this merchant, but human payment is often relatively large, I rarely pay 0.0001 yuan. So, human cards are a behavior that meets the characteristics of human payment.

But the agent may be very high-level. For example, if my agent helps me write a report, he may need to check the data in 100 databases. He may have to pay 100 times, maybe in the future. At that time, it will be very high-level. But at the same time, it may be very small. At the same time, it may be a one-to-one payment.

So, sending cards to agents is just to let agents adapt to the system of people. 让agent用人类的GUI也是让AI吸引人。 最后如果我们认为AI非常强大, 我们认为AI无处不在, 那AI应该有完全自己内部的 native [agent-native infrastructure].

The third stage is to build a powerful Agent. It's the most suitable digital world for it. Because each of these is actually a product opportunity.

What kind of opportunities will there be if the coding ability is unlimited? In fact, there are many places where humans are not enough because of the programming. For example, if you have a lot of programmers serving you, what kind of software can you use?

I think the first one is that we can use better software. For example, If you have a smart home, smart curtains, smart hot water pot, you will find that many software is still because the person who wrote it is not the top programmer. The top programmer may write the promotion method in their own way. But if you make a smart hot water pot, a floor machine, your programmer is just an ordinary programmer. So we often feel that many of these apps are quite difficult to use. But in fact, if each software is written by the top programmer, its performance, application, and safety have all become very good. It may be a big improvement to our existing applications.

Secondly, I think it's a personalized software. This is something that can be improved. For example, I am a senior user, so I may need to have a more efficient way of managing contacts, or group communication, or management of voice. But I may not need any functions that allow me to "blah blah blah". But my grandma may have used another thing.

Now, actually everyone, different people, use the same WeChat. Maybe in the future, my wonderful dream is that such an application will provide you with a base. But you can change the many functions on it. You can meet your personal needs. For example, for you, you can do an interview or a blog on WeChat. This is a more personalized software.

So when you have infinite programmers at a very low price, what can you do? I think there are actually a lot of new needs to be born. But this new need to be born is a gradual process. Because it needs someone to imagine. It also needs a good base to carry out this kind of personalization. For example, we just said that we want to reform WeChat. But WeChat has to give me a new interface. So in this, it's not a process that comes and goes.

二十七、AI Native 公司的组织形态

We just talked about AI Native entrepreneurs and the new opportunities. Do you have any observations about the organizational form of AI Native entrepreneurs? Do you have any good examples that you have seen that are different from the previous generation?

And in the past, in fact, many times software development is a waterfall-like division. There is this structure, there is this back end, front end, test, UI, operation. But now it may become a full stop more and more. Because I'm a commanding AI agent anyway, I can do all these things. At this time, it may be from the process of pool division to a small independent operating organization. These few people are in charge of a product function module. From its underlying design to its previous online testing to operation, they are all done by the same batch of people.

Now, in fact, many companies are trying to use agents to improve their own efficiency. But for a company that has been operating for 10 years, the biggest problem is that your context, your data is not in AI. It's actually very difficult to get these contacts in.

So we also see some new companies. From the first day, their contacts are all visible to AI. So AI knows very well what they want to do. For example, we invested in a company called Slack. It's actually a platform for people and agents to cooperate. It's a bit like Slack for people and agents to work together. Their entire company's operation, task management, and programming are all on their own platform. So they build the Slack using Slack. In fact, Cloud Code and Codex have shared that Cloud Code is written in Cloud Code. Codex is also written in Codex.

It's actually a new product, a new company. It can start from the first day, and then AI participates in the management and guidance of most of their companies' software development. This is very obvious to improve efficiency.

What about the structure? The structure will change. There will be a structure. I think the result of the organization I just mentioned is that the division of labor will usually become blurred. Because there are many divisions based on the context of people. Why is there a front-end and a back-end UI? It's because one person's skills are limited, one person's context is limited, and one person cannot do front-end and back-end UI at the same time. But now AI is okay. So your division of labor may become more blurred. There will be more full-time people, not more people who are clearly organized and divided into departments.

So this will bring a conclusion. That is, if a company has a very clear department wall, it is actually difficult for AI to adapt. So that's why many big companies push AI. It's not simple to say that everyone just installs a cloud code and that's it. It actually requires organizational change. This is also why I think it's not simple. You have a good model, you will reduce the cost-effectiveness. In fact, many times it has to solve people's problems. This is quite difficult.

It's all about organizing the change of AI.

Yes, I want to say more about organizational change. There was an article that was very good. I forgot who wrote it. It says that during the industrial revolution, there was a process from steam engines to electric engines. The steam engine factory was built around a main shaft. Because steam engines have a shaft that rotates, so it has a very long shaft. Then the shaft is pulled off a lot of belt, and the main shaft's power is transmitted to each machine. So at that time, the factory was a narrow-gauge type. The whole factory was built around this shaft.

But when the electric engine came out, because it was a wire, the wire didn't need that main shaft. So the factory in the era of electric engines could be made bigger and flatter. After this kind of bigger and flatter factory came out, a invention called the flow line was brought. It was done by Ford at that time. The emergence of this flow line can only be done when the company can build a very large and flat factory building.

If all the forces you mentioned come from one axis, then it is actually very difficult to make a large flow line factory building. The birth of the flow line brings a significant increase in the production rate.

So what this story is about is that from steam engines to electric engines, it does not naturally bring about a growth rate improvement. You need to have physical organizational changes in the factory and how to organize production. From doing a lot of things in the original position to doing one thing in the position, the flow line is pushed. This thing will bring such changes.

I think the technology is the result of the end. It is a process of gradual penetration. The change of organization required in this way is actually a difficult place. Because organizational changes are all human changes. Human changes don't change in a minute. It may take ten years to change. Even if the old organization is replaced by the new one, or the new organization is replaced by the old organization, it will only happen.

And then Devin actually has more than $400 million. And then OpenCore, these apps are also used by a lot of people. So I feel that Gui Gu is actually very focused on models and verticals. In fact, I think there are still a lot of opportunities for horizontal.

Do you personally invest in apps in China? I invested in Kimmy for the model. And then the rest of the companies are all app companies. Is the world model a model or an app? The world model is a model. You invested in two models. Yes. Three models. From the perspective of training models, yes. OK. Yes, you can understand it that way.

Is the world model a machine model? It's a robot technology model. A robot-based technology model.

You laugh because the stubbornness will be higher? I think the word "world model" is too vague. Because it has to be summed up with a hype word. But maybe because it's convenient to communicate, right? Maybe everyone says this is a world model. But every world model company, many times, the approach he talks about will be very different. For example, Xie Sai Ling would think that these virtual models are not virtual models. But for example, is VLA a virtual model? You can also think that it is a virtual model. But Sai Ling might think that there are so many languages in it, this is not good. Sai Ling also thinks that video generation is not a world model. But now some people don't think so. So I think there is still an open problem in this. There is no chain.

三十二、AI 硬件：值得乐观吗？

Will there be a lot of domestic hardware opportunities? What AI-native hardware products do you think are good?

You mean the kind of portable recording equipment? Not the kind of robot equipment, right? Yeah, the consumer electronics.

I actually have a discussion. I think many consumer electronics companies will repeat the same process of new consumption. If you remember, the new consumption was very popular in 2020. At that time, all the categories were made at once, from skin care products to cream to toothpaste to everything, but later on, many people found that it was not a traditional category to make some small innovations, and then find a live broadcast on the Internet, find a product to bring goods, it became a completely different new category.

I think there are many AI hardware now, in fact, it has not solved some fundamental problems. It may add a chatbot to the hardware, or some AI sensing capabilities, communication capabilities. But I'm a little worried about whether or not we can create a new demand category.

And in general, it's harder to make hardware than software. Because hardware has a supply chain, it needs to be opened, designed, and delivered in a slower way. It needs more capital and use. It's more troublesome to sell. So hardware is actually more difficult than software.

In fact, many people are discussing it now. For example, is there a special machine for A-Series? Some people also sell lobster machines, right? Special lobster machines. But in fact, I think why did everyone buy a Mac mini or a mobile phone? In fact, existing computers and mobile phones are quite balanced in terms of size, performance, power consumption, and heat dissipation. So I would rather think that in a relatively long time, the good body of agent is actually the mobile phone and cloud.

因为现在有很多, 可能几十个各种可穿戴, 然后录音, 然后比如说给你更多的context, 或者这些应用。 我觉得在这里面, 所谓做可穿戴的, 其实都会面临问题, 就是说你要把一个东西穿戴起来, 真的是一个很大的一个, 就是你要很坚持的习惯。 尤其是你别在衣服上什么的时候, 你今天换件衣服, 你要把它又要别上去。 这个里面, 它得跟你解决很大的[问题].

So I have some cautious views on this area, especially the impression of the wearable belt. No head? We can say that there is basically no head. OK, like Lucky. Anyway, we don't have heads. Not to mention that there are some companies, but I don't think we really see Of course, we are from the perspective of the head of the company. I think it's not easy to do from the perspective of the market.

But on the contrary, for example, I have this whoop in Aura. They are actually a bit like AI. But it's not actually an AI hardware. It's a health hardware. I think it's really useful. If it wasn't for the AI I use, I would have made a lot of money.

How much did you invest in the robot?

We invested in the main company of robots, such as 5G's Lin Xiaoshou and Fangzhou Robot. Two world-class companies. We also invested in Feixi before, and it was divided into the middle. But we didn't invest in the humanoid robot.

But I think the humanoid robot is still in a very early stage. It's mainly scientific research. And then what is the story he told? I think it's useful by manipulation. That is to say, it can be realized by manipulation. So I think it is indeed a model. No matter who makes it or how to do it. But the ultimate goal he has is that robots can control these things. He needs it very much. But I think human robots are definitely in a very exciting stage of capital. So his valuation is relatively high for large sales. I don't know if this can continue to be so high.

But I think at least we can see whether human resources are useful or not. I think it's still in the process of development. I think human resources may be useful eventually. But it needs to have good hands. So we stole hands. It may need a good arm or a good mechanical structure. It also needs better models. So we also stole models. We must have missed a lot of opportunities. But I think we are also observing.

三十三、跟风、博客、个人 AI 工作流

What was the most popular project last year?

I think it's the follow-up project. Because everyone is more formal. and there are many information flows that become very convenient. So it's really fast, for example, VC pays attention to the field or the field he likes, he becomes very consensus. So we will see, for example, many do various things, of course, that can be passed on to recording, right? Because everyone will say, because agent needs a lot of context, so I will record the context of the recording. and then there are many people who do various fields of medicine.

I think we want to vote for the first-tier. We hope that he has a different view. He does something different. Even if this thing sounds very strange, it's not that this thing is already a track. For example, it's called the record track. For example, the plug is actually very powerful because it is a product model. But later I found that many people also do various plugs. It grows in various shapes and sticks to the back of the phone. I think that way it becomes a follow-up. This is a bit of a follow-up.

So This is one of the reasons why we passed the test. We saw that it was really because of the fire. There was a VC who talked about it and a broker who talked about it. So it was just a better product.

So can brokers make a judgment in investment? I think it's definitely a very good channel for information transmission. So many people listen to Xiaojun's blog. I think everyone is coming to the conclusion of the front page faster and faster.

Will you finish listening? I actually don't listen to blogs. You are all turning... I'm reading. You are turning into text with an app. Yes. Oh, you think it's more efficient?

For some very good ones, for example, the one you talked to Sally about, I just listened to it. You listened to it for seven hours. Why didn't I believe it? I listened to it over and over again. Because I like to read more than listening. I don't drive, I don't cook. I don't have any common scenes where my hands can't reach out. Now I have my own AI app. So the bloggers I'm interested in will check every day if there's a new transcript. Then they will automatically pull it down and put it in my Notion. So I go to Notion every day to see if my AI has made me a new blogger's text.

This text is so long. Yes, it's very long. Can you finish it? It must be shorter than what I heard. I can finish it. For example, your two-hour blog, I definitely don't need to read it for two hours. You are indeed a person who can read 100 books a year. This should be like this. For example, your seven-hour broadcast, I should not need seven hours to read it.

三十四、AI 改变了自己什么：思考的健身房

What have you changed in the past six months? AI has developed so fast. What is the change in the investment?

First of all, I did a lot of small tools with agent-based coding. I used to be a person who didn't know how to write code. Then I used Cloud Code at the end of last year. I found that it could do 80% of the tasks. But it is not as good as the online posting. I use it every day. But I found that the completion rate is completely different in the early years of this year.

Of course, a lot of things I do now are still about getting all kinds of information. For example, my blog, my newsletter, my meeting notes, and all kinds of content I write. I have several passwords to organize them together every day. Then I organize a daily newspaper for myself. For example, the person I meet every day. Maybe there is a person I meet three times in three hours. Maybe the notes on his content will be organized under this person's bar. and build a long-term tracking system for a person, a company, and a stock.

I think this has really improved my acquisition and collection of information. So I think AI is indeed a great improvement in the investment industry. Because the investment industry is indeed looking at the information and analyzing it.

Then I have been thinking about the problem lately. More and more naturally, we put a lot of thinking outside the bag to add LGBT or AI. For example, if there was a problem before, maybe everyone would go to understand it and think about it. Now many people are asking AI, right? What's going on? I think this is a lot of benefits for you, but there may be a lot of problems. Because if you get the answer directly and you don't think about it, your quality of answer and how you think about it later may be relatively... Your brain hasn't changed, your whole body hasn't updated, so you may not really understand this thing.

I later thought that if a person has been riding a wheelchair, his leg muscles may be weakened and shrink. If a person uses AI to bring this kind of so-called thinking for a long time, in place of his own thinking, then this thinking ability may also be weakened.

So you see, we used to export physical labor to machines, so we are now estimated to be a thousand years younger than humans. Many people may be weaker physically because we sit for a long time or in the car, right? Then we export knowledge and memory to the Internet. So now a lot of things, but I can't remember things, but I'm going to search. Now we are thinking about exporting to AI.

Andrew Kibasi said one thing, he said you can export thinking, but you can't export understanding. But I think thinking may not be completely exported. I thought about it later. You see, we have a gym to exercise. I'm going to exercise deliberately. Maybe when you think a lot about VivaLog AI, you may need a gym for your thoughts. You deliberately think.

For example, in order to prepare our e-book, I also have a point of view on many things. But this point of view has no arguments. It's actually a process of forcing me to think. When I think, I don't let AI summarize it. I can talk more clearly about things. Thinking requires deliberate practice. And innovation is also something that can be practiced.

Because there is a very obvious characteristic now that young people have a lot of ideas. But as a middle-aged person who is almost 40 years old, your ideas may be much less than when I started my business at the age of 20.

I feel like I'm now using the subscriptions of all major coding agencies. There are a lot of AI waiting to work for me every day. What do you want to do?

In fact, I think this is a problem that many people encounter. I'm thinking that now there are a lot of AI programmers waiting for you to drop the money. But But what to do? Including myself, I have this problem. Maybe it's not a problem, but a problem. This also needs to be practiced deliberately.

For example, maybe do some simple web apps first. Right? Sync my meeting, sync my schedule, sync my subscription, and start. I think this is not a matter of one-off. It's hard for you to write a complicated application as soon as you get up. You don't know what to write. But maybe you can start from a simple one. Step by step. It's actually the same as fitness training. Start from simple, then stretch, then do push-ups, and then gradually become complicated.

So I feel that in the era of AI, learning to use AI is actually a deliberate practice. For many people, they don't know what to do with AI. But I think this matter can be gradually improved.

I have a friend who doesn't want to reveal his identity. Maybe it's Mr. Lin. He has to burn a thousand dollars of tokens every month. He is very hard to solve various problems with AI. What did he even do? He made a scanner. Scanning which people are out of date every day and no one is registered. Then he would see what the AI registered back today and what the domain name was. Then he would see what the domain name was made for. I said, "You're a bit like Omar Khasee, right?" "I went to the fish market today and saw some fish, so I made some dishes." He was a bit like forcing himself to do it deliberately.

For example, he bought a cat-related thing last time. He made a real-time monitoring system for his cat at home. This thing is very fun anyway. Because I admire him very much. Because I think he is deliberately practicing his own AI innovation.

What do you think he does? For example, my family, including my wife and my parents, all have smart keys, such as OOP, AURA, and I made a dashboard to put all the data APIs together to compare and see. And recently, I, Mr. Lin, and our other friends are using the SLOCK we voted for to create a tool that encourages friends to compare their health data. And their original goal was to make me lose weight. They said, "I want to make a dashboard that shows everyone's sleep well. So you have to sleep more." I said, "Okay, I'll develop it together." So we also used agentic to collaborate. But all of these things don't seem to have any direct value. But I think in this process, for example, we know how difficult it is to collaborate with agents. In fact, I think it's a very interesting experience.

三十五、AI、失业与社会的消化

How do you think society should digest the changes and impacts of this technological change, including unemployment?

I think unemployment is really inevitable. Because the technology is expanding faster now. It will be faster than many people learning new technologies and adapting to it. So I think from a personal perspective, learning AI, thinking about the ability to use AI, and the safety of your profession are actually very important. Because it's harder to test who can handle AI better.

But at present, it is true that AI destroys value faster than creating value. When work is replaced quickly, the company will become more efficient. But for me personally, one is learning AI, the other is in the field where AI is more difficult to replace. For example, things that are talked about between people are actually difficult to replace with AI. Because it requires trust between people or people to take responsibility.

In this case, I guess it's hard to say that because there will be unemployment, AI will not develop. Because no one can afford the price of not developing. It may indeed be better with some kind of UBI or tax form to distribute to a certain degree of interest. Recently, someone in South Korea has been proposing that Samsung and Hades make too much money. In fact, this is also a move for social stability.

But I think what we see now is the first-hand or direct impact of AI. AI can write programs, so programmers may lose their jobs. But in the long run, I am very optimistic because we look at it from the point of view of new work. It takes time, but it will occur in a lot of places you don't want to go.

I gave an example before. The industrial revolution started with the invention of steam engines in order to draw water from the mine. Because the coal mine was getting deeper and deeper, the deeper it was, the easier it was to get water, and the water was easy to collapse, so it was very dangerous. At that time, steam engines could only do straight lines, so they could only draw water in this scene. Later, Watt actually invented the cooling system and the propeller, and he turned this network-referral movement into a round-trip movement. So steam engines can do more things, especially to drive the steam engine.

At that time, the invention of the Stirlitz steam engine was the first step from the invention of steam engines to the development of the steam industry. After the progress of the fashion industry, everyone has changed their clothes. This leads to a demand for color. Because you didn't need to distinguish colors when you made a few clothes But how to distinguish colors when you have dozens of clothes The dye at the beginning was natural dye For example, various colored rouge grass, this kind of worm, etc. Very expensive, very little production For example, purple was the most expensive color Because purple is relatively rare in nature.

At that time, the chemical synthesis dye process began to develop. Now the chemical dye factory like Bassefou and Bayer, In fact, it was a dye factory at that time. It's a human-made dye factory. Of course, it can't be a direct comparison. But historically, the development of the textile industry has been greatly affected by the growth of the dye industry. The dye industry also brought about the birth of the chemical industry. After the birth of the chemical industry, later the chemical industry produced gasoline, plastic, and fertilizer. This series of very important chemical industry products. It also brought about the improvement of the production of agricultural products in humans. Then the plastic brought about many new applications.

So with the invention of a steam engine, many of these things spread. At first, we didn't expect that the real estate industry would bring about an improvement in the housing industry or in the chemical industry. This requires time and a lot of companies to find the right time. When the ability to supply is greatly improved, it will bring about a new opportunity for production.

So there will be a lot of new work in this. So I think in the long term, there is no doubt that new work will be produced. But it will take a little time to spread. In the process, maybe someone wants to learn to use AI. Another aspect is that I want to explore what new opportunities AI brings.

But the current problem is that the change is too fast. Indeed, the industrial revolution is a few decades of time. But AI is now one year. Everyone feels that writing a program has really been replaced a lot. So maybe the degree will be bigger. Whether it's investment opportunities or thinking about society, thinking about people and their professional relationships, there will be a big change.

The biggest change may be how humans think about the relationship with AI. I don't know if you use Cloud, will you have a strong sense of trust and dependence on it? After it can help you do a lot of things well, you will naturally trust it. Then you will have a cooperative relationship with it, because you have done a lot of things with it.

At this time, I can't help it. It's more. Yes, you will be more in a very hard way. Then this kind of dependence will actually make a lot of changes in your life. We actually didn't prepare this at all.

But in fact, many people, you see, I observed it with crayfish at that time. Many people actually did not do any important work at that time. Sometimes it's like a training program. I found that he remembered a lot. Then he can help you do a lot of things. You have your memories and can help you do a lot of things. At the same time, OpenCloud also has a feature. It's that the file is written in a more human way.

三十六、孩子学什么专业：Agency 与 OOD 能力

I think a lot of people have been asking a question lately. We don't give any suggestions because there are many things we have experienced in the past. Many people say that my child should learn some major in the future. What do you think? Because my child just went to kindergarten.

I think this is actually a very difficult question. I think it's really important to train his agency. In my blog two years ago, I said that people will have the taste of agency.

This is what many people say. You have the taste of working in AI. But I realized that it's hard to test people. AI is so smart. It has seen so much good data. Its test is definitely far superior to that of ordinary humans. So I realized that TEST may not be a special place for humans. AI-made PPT is very good. I saw it. So I don't think TEST is reliable.

Maybe for now, it's still an agency. No matter how well it does, what you do is what you tell it. So I just said that innovation should be deliberately practiced. Do what you do deliberately. Many people, including myself, if you ask me what I want to do, Actually, you may not know. You may not know what you are going to do. But if you think about it, right? You may find that there are many things that can be better.

I think this is still a good question to ask. If someone answers all your questions, but you know you have to ask questions. You need to have problems that you want to solve, problems that you want to change. But now the next step is also Proactive Aging. Proactive Aging means that I need to have stronger AI. I'll help you. Yes, at this time, it may be more challenging.

So I thought later that if the current AI is based on the current model structure, it can solve the data that humans already have, that is, within distribution. But humans are interesting. There are a few humans who can create things out of the distribution. They can create things that humans have never seen before. You will find that AI can't tell a original joke now. It can only refreeze a joke that humans have already told. But it can't tell an original joke.

So this is interesting. Why can some people create OOD content? For example, Jaloha can do a group theory at the age of 20. Why?

I think there are many studies on the human brain, including Yang Le Kun, or there are many Saturn. For example, the intelligence of the rat, we humans have not learned yet. The intelligence of the cat, how to explore the world to form a process of self-learning and evolution, this may not find a good answer.

So from this perspective, if you do things, your ability is all in a human distribution, a center of political distribution, it is actually easy to be replaced. But if a person can produce content that is out of distribution, it can create content that has never been created before, then this may not be easily replaced. But this may be curiosity, creativity, and how to reflect these in education. I think these are all very big topics.

But I think that in human history, people have been constantly accumulating the real important abilities of mankind. For example, after the industrial revolution, human physical strength became less important, and their brain power became very important. So at that time, people started to study to change their fate. At that time, people started to pay attention to education. Before that, if you didn't have education, you had physical strength.

But after the Internet was invented, I think it separated human memory and ability. Knowledge and ability. Because knowledge can be found. knowledge has been replaced by the ability to do things, such as writing codes and doing things.

But after AI came, I think it separated the ability from the execution and judgment. First, the execution power did something, and this became more and more AI-based. But what do I do with judgment? and TASTE. But maybe TASTE will be replaced by AI if it is done correctly. Because AI can go to the lab to get a lot of rewards.

So what to do? This action will become at least at present, AI can't move by itself. I think this is what I think. But I have no answer. I think it seems to make a person more agency, including myself. So I say start with more AI, do more strange things.

三十七、结语：继续被打脸，继续录制

Will you continue to record when you are often hit in the face after recording?

I told Mr. Wa that I would not record it next time. Because it would limit my ability to make a flag. Mr. Wa immediately said that he was convinced by me. Because if you don't record it after you feel that you are hit, you are really beaten.

So I think to keep summarizing my thoughts is a process to let myself think about some unthinkable problems and maybe think about them more clearly.

You can practice. You can practice. Because when you express it, you will find that some places you know very clearly that you understand or you think you understand. But some places are confused. This is very clear when you say something. So I think this is also a process of my own organization. I think it's very valuable.

And I still think that as an early investor, being hit on the face is often happy. Enjoy being slapped.

So will we continue to do this?

Yes, we can. Next time, we may be hit again. I don't know what other guests will think. Anyway, I feel this is normal. Because early investment is really a habit of being hit. I think they have also accepted it.

好了,今天的节目就是这样。 这里是商业访谈录,是一档由语言及世界工作室出品的深度访谈节目。 你可以到公众号关注我们的工作室获取更多的信息。 我们的公众号是语言及世界,languageisworld。 我们希望和你一起从这里探索新的世界。

item.description:
## 一、雨森自己就是他正在分析的那个"Harness"
最让我意外的观察是：**雨森本人正在以"Harness"的方式重构他作为投资人的认知系统**——而他自己并没有完全意识到这一点。
注意这个隐藏对称： - 他描述 Cloud Code 的核心价值在于"data flywheel"——通过用户使用产生高质量反馈信号，回流到模型训练。 - 他描述自己录播客的价值在于"市场不会对你说好话，如果市场说你错了就是错了"——通过公开表达产生高质量负面反馈信号，回流到自己的判断系统。
他在节目里说自己"被博客绑架"、不敢加仓——这恰恰证明了他正在经历一个**未经设计的强化学习循环**：公开表达 → 仓位约束 → 真实金钱损益 → 反馈修正。这比任何一个 AI agent 的 RLHF 都更残酷、更真实。换句话说，**他这档节目不是内容产品，是他自己的 RLHF 训练管道**。而他选择继续录制，就是选择继续把自己暴露在 OOD（out-of-distribution）的市场反馈里。
## 二、"返"的真正陷阱：把"输入→产出→结果"误读成"输入→产出"
雨森拆出来的三段式（投入 → 产

item.title: [Business of Sport] The Real Finances of a Tennis Player | Kyle Edmund
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

三个让人意外的观察

1. 网球的"灰色幽灵": Edmund 的"伤退-保护排名-50% 支票"漏洞，揭示了一个被忽视的系统性真相

表面上，访谈在讨论奖金、分配、PTPA 诉讼。但真正的"阿哈时刻"藏在 Edmund 随口提到的细节里——球员曾大规模利用"受保护排名"漏洞，上场即退赛、拿全额支票，又不消耗 PR 名额。这不是个别球员道德问题，而是整个生态系统在向球员发出的真实信号：大满贯首轮支票就是球员一年的现金流命脉，制度本身在引诱寻租行为。

跨领域连接：这与硅谷创业圈的 "ZIRP 时代僵尸独角兽" 惊人相似——当资本（在网球里是大满贯奖金）与日常运营（巡回赛收入）严重脱钩时，整个产业链就会演化出"靠一次大融资 / 一次大满贯吊命一年"的扭曲模式。Sabalenka 和 Sinner 现在的诉求不是涨工资，他们是在替低排名球员重新设计现金流曲线——这本质上是一场"VC 后期估值 vs 早期种子轮"的财富再分配讨论。

2. Edmund 把"打破球拍"的态度逆转，泄露了一个职业身份的深层真相

他承认：当球员时认为摔拍 = 失控、不专业；退役后却觉得"挺好看的"。这个 180 度反转不是矛盾，而是一种残酷的元洞见——职业运动员的"自律"很大程度上是一种被身份绑架的表演，而非真实信念。当他卸下"球员"这层身份，他对同一行为的判断完全反转。

这与心理学里的 "角色一致性偏误" (Role-congruent reasoning) 完美对应：人不是先有信念再有行为，而是先被身份要求规训行为，然后用信念合理化它。这也解释了为什么他在另一段说"我打球时对其他球员搞副业毫无兴趣，现在完全理解了"——职业巅峰期的"专注"，实际上是一种身份审查机制，而不是真的最优策略。这对所有"all-in 型创业者"是个尖锐警告：你以为你在专注，其实你在被身份禁锢。

3. 最深的隐藏模式：Edmund 整个故事的"非对称冲击点"

把 Edmund 的财务曲线展开看，会发现一个反直觉的事实：他职业生涯的财务结构与一个"赢家通吃"的内容创作者几乎完全同构。 - 2018 澳网半决赛 ≈ 一条爆款视频 - 2015 Tiebreak Tens 的 25 万英镑 ≈ 一次品牌植入合作 - 大量的 Futures / Challenger 年份 ≈ 没人看的日常内容 - 受伤的 20 个月 ≈ 算法降权期

这意味着：现代职业网球本质上已经变成"内容创作经济"，奖金只是基础订阅，真正的财富来自"那一刻能否成为热点"。这也是为什么他签下 Rolex、Jaguar、Evian 不是因为 ranking，而是因为澳网那两周的"热度窗口"。

跨领域的尖锐启示：当 Edmund 说"赞助合同的核心是 Instagram 曝光"时，他其实在说网球早就不是体育市场，而是注意力市场。Sinner 现在的统治力之所以"对运动不利"，不是因为没人能赢，而是因为叙事不再有悬念，注意力开始流失——这与 Netflix 砍掉续订率低的剧集是同一套逻辑。网球的"七大治理体"困局，本质上是一个还在用工业时代治理结构、运行在注意力经济上的产业。

一个隐藏的系统性真相

Edmund 在退役那段说："我花了 20 年做一件事，然后一个晚上做出决定就放弃了，感觉像失败。"

这句话表面是情绪，但背后藏着一个少有人提的结构性事实：所有"高承诺、低多元化"的人生路径，退出成本不是线性的，而是悬崖式的。运动员、博士生、长期创业者、长期军人——他们共享同一种"身份退出创伤"。Edmund 的解决方案（去读 MBA）也极具系统性意味：他在用"重新结构化的学习"来重新构建一个身份骨架，而不是仅仅"找份工作"。

这对任何一个在某条路径上 all-in 超过 10 年的人都是关键洞见：退出的难度不在于"做什么"，而在于"我是谁"必须重写。

📝 转录文本

开场: Novak 的气场

When you play Novak, the first thing you play all these guys is they have an aura and you feel it. And he even starts in the change room, walking to the court. He's not there waiting for you. I'm waiting for him. And even on the court, you're playing the match. He's dictating the crowd. You feel he has control of the situation.

There's no doubt anyone who wants to be a player has like an inner belief that they think they're going to make it. I didn't really say that out loud, but inside I felt like I had something — a bit of an X factor that I could keep improving.

So as a player, you want to get paid more. They are the product. The one thing I would say is the slams provide the most money to the sport for the players. Is it up to the tours to reach that standard? They need to provide more first before attacking the slams.

I find it hard initially because I spent all this time and effort for 20 years. Almost feels like failure that you then give up in sort of one day. And I struggled to get my head around that.

We are so excited for this. Now, I always considered myself the more talented tennis player between us. Sadly, he grew to like six foot ten and became a beast of a server and now kicks my ass. But it's so good to have a tennis legend in the studio. So thank you so much for joining us, man.

Yeah, thanks. Thanks for inviting me. It's great to be here. Excited to chat to you guys.

兄弟之间的网球战争 & 摔拍话题

It was actually the one thing that we used to really fight about. Really? Tennis. We're pretty close and we were pretty chill growing up, but the tennis court, I don't know what it did. I had serious anger management. I still do.

Something about it though, isn't it? Yeah, exactly. It's that sort of one-to-one personal battle.

How many rackets have you broken?

So, I think the short answer is two. So, when I first started playing, my dad, when I first time I went to court, he said: if you break a racket, you throw your racket, you're done. You're not playing again. And my dad was one of those guys — when he said that, he meant it.

And it was only until I was probably 24, 25 in an off-season, just remote court, that the off-seasons were going along, I was getting more tired, getting more agitated, less patience — and then just one drill, I just had enough and went. But I never did it, and then I did it one time in a match again. So yeah, I tried not to.

When you see Medvedev just absolutely destroy a racket, do you think that's acceptable? It's pretty disrespectful to the other player, puts everyone in a tough situation. Is that okay?

No, I think it's fine. When I played, I kind of viewed it as like: oh, he's lost his sort of patience, he's lost his composure. But now that I'm not playing, I think it's great. Because people actually love to see it. That's why, you know, they give the warning and it's like code violation, I guess, for the integrity of everything. But it's like — why? What has it actually done? The guy's — he's the problem. He's lost his racket. No one else has lost out and it's been good entertainment. And he's just showing you that he's rattled.

The only time where it is bad is the grass courts because it puts dents into the grass, which obviously you don't want. But hard court, clay court, I like to see it. I didn't do it loads myself, but it's fun to watch, isn't it? Rather than just walking to the other side and not showing any emotion.

表面与国籍背景

What was your favorite surface?

Growing up was clay. I played a lot and it was good for my game. The way I played, it rewarded shots. But the tour is predominantly played on hard courts. I'd say 70% — obviously start of the year through to spring before the clay and the grass court season. As soon as Wimbledon finishes, then you've got hard courts the rest of the year. So naturally, I found myself transitioning to hard courts and liking them. And my best results in terms of tour events and Australian Open came on hard.

Can we clear something up — the nationality question around you?

I know you as like the British number one that took Andy, you know, off his perch — when was it? Probably about 2018, 2019. Yeah, around that time actually. So you're a great British player, Davis Cup winner, Grand Slam semi-finalist, born in Johannesburg. Then lived in Yorkshire. And have kind of got a South African and Welsh parent.

Yeah, exactly. Dad born in Maesteg, I think that's right. And then, yeah, mum over in Durban. He is in Wales then he went to Zimbabwe and then South Africa. And I was born in Joburg, but family is predominantly from Durban area and Maritzburg. And then I moved to UK when I was three, so I don't remember much at all. But all our family's still over there — cousins and uncles and aunties. So yeah, I'm very, very British now and don't really have much connection there apart from family.

进入英国网球体系

So you went through the whole system, the British tennis system, came up through the ranks?

Exactly, yeah. I started when I was 10 in Hull in Yorkshire, Beverly in Yorkshire. Went to David Lloyd there with Richard Plews. Just started up as a summer tennis camp. Typical, you know — parents put me and my sister in, didn't want us at home during those six weeks. And then it was actually coach that sort of approached my parents and said, why don't you think about playing more, because he's doing okay. And just snowballed from there. Started doing a few squads after school, before school, 6:30 to late. And then at 13 I left home to come to London — and that was when you realized like, I need to commit to this and this is going to be a thing.

Yeah, especially moving away from home, because I loved playing other sports in school like rugby, football, cricket, athletics. And I did that around tennis. But when you're sort of moving away from school to go to an academy and get tutored, it's a commitment to do that. So I was like, I need to be on it now.

What's that like at 13? Are you playing tennis for like five hours a day and doing school for five hours a day?

Yeah, kind of. It's great at that age because you're suddenly shifting from a school focus to tennis on the side — where it felt like tennis was the focus and school on the side. At 13 years old, it's the dream.

And yeah, it didn't benefit my grades at the end or my mum wasn't too happy, but that was the reality. So you suddenly went up playing a lot more tennis, your body's obviously adapting to it. Initially, I didn't enjoy it just being away from home. I think I even had a conversation and said: I want to come home. But my dad just said: no, no, stay there and you'll get over it. And kind of before I knew, I did. Thanks, Dad. Emotionally understanding. Exactly.

13 岁的人生豪赌

So you're really having to make a call on this being a potential career option at that early stage?

Yeah, which is crazy to think about. And I think at 13, you don't fully understand the call you're making. You're just making a decision because in that moment, it's like — this is what I find fun and I want to do. But you don't really think about everything beyond that — as in, if it doesn't happen, what do I have to fall back on? What's the backup plan? It's just: this is it and hopefully it goes all right.

Knowing all you know now, if you had a child at that age, would you say, let's roll the dice, you're going in the lotto and we'll see if you can make it to the top? Or would you go, we're sacrificing academia, sacrificing potential career and professional life — and it's very unlikely?

Yeah, I would say, well, yes — because I got the opportunity to do it. I don't feel like I'd ever want to take away that opportunity. I think there's a balance of: it's really hard to know at that age whether you are going to make it. So there's got to be an element of being rational and practical and being like, well, is this actually going to happen, or are we just kind of delaying the inevitable?

So there's that side of it, but I would definitely say you need something to fall back on. But even with my career that I had, I certainly paid back the sort of sacrifice, I think — with moving away and financially and the time and effort. But even with that, I still feel now that I've retired, you're still a little bit in the unknown of like, what am I doing? What do I have to fall back on? So I think that's important.

资金、联邦协会与私人投资模式

Sorry, you said about something to fall back on. We had Dom Thiem on the show and he was like, it costs a million bucks to get your child through the formation stages and the training ages to become a pro.

Yeah, no, absolutely. And I wouldn't have been able to do it without the Federation. There's no doubt about that. Like, parents put in money. I was very lucky I was in private schooling and I had to come out of private schooling and go into Beverly Grammar School purely because of tennis — just can't do tennis and the private schooling. And it got to a point where then I did get some funding to go to an academy and probably till the ages of 18 I received funding. But without that, there was no chance it would happen.

So in the UK, you can be a pro without having very rich parents?

You can, but you need the help and you kind of need to be recognised. So it is that whole give and take: I start tennis at 10 years old so it was quite late — and that's a good thing. So to get the funding you need to get recognised, which means you need to be good and you need to win some matches. It's very hard to get funding just purely off like "oh, we like you and you look like you could do something." So there's that balance of: I'm trying to play for the future and the long term and improve for this end goal, but in the meantime you need some short-term success to make sure you're keeping on track along the way.

I don't know if you — I mean, I'm sure you will have seen this — but we've had a few conversations where now you've got a model almost like it's an equity investment in some players. So private investment coming in, you'd be 100,000, 150,000 pounds, let's say, and you'll secure 15% of future earnings after the age of 18 for five years. And it almost becomes an investment in an asset, which sounds hugely transactional and maybe slightly immoral to some standpoints, but actually if done right and given at the right stage of that career development, and it allows you to go to the Mouratoglou Academy or similar...

Exactly. And you do see that. I know when I was growing up and on tour, there were certain players that in that time — it's very appealing because you're getting the finance power and availability to fund your career. But obviously for them, when you do make it, you're then having to give up percentage of your paycheck. And then lots of times you see people trying to get out of that. And maybe sometimes it's only the way to do it.

We never really had that at the LTA. It was just purely: we're funding your career, we don't — we're not asking for anything back when you make it. Let's say because you're doing it on your own. But there are some private backers that go down that route. And it depends, I think, how savvy you are or your parents of all may help you making decisions of: yeah, let's take it because we really need the money, but actually do we back ourselves enough that when we do make it, we've then got to accept that we're going to be forking out the whole time, percentage-wise. Depends how much in the short term you want or need the cash, basically.

"X 因素" 与晋级心法

Do you remember a moment when you're kind of coming up through those junior ranks and you've made those sacrifices and you're committing the way you did, where you were like, yeah, I'm going to be a top player?

I was always quite reserved in that way. I never liked to think too far ahead. There's no doubt I think anyone who wants to be a player has like an inner belief that they think they're going to make it. Like they think they have something. I didn't really say that out loud, but inside I felt like I could do something — like I had something, a bit of an X factor that I could keep improving. And you could see that maybe with evidence on results, but also evidence on feeling. Like you feel yourself improving, you feel yourself getting closer to players on court, competing with them harder.

And that was always my way of doing it. Every stage I went from under 14 Tennis Europe to the ITF Juniors, from the ITF Juniors to Futures, Challengers, on the tour — I'd always go into the next level, dip my toes in, not break through immediately, sort of get a feel for it, and then probably six months down the line, then I'd get results and really get momentum to kick through that.

And they say in tennis, you never want to be at a particular level for more than 18 months. So in the futures, you don't want to get stuck there for 18 months — otherwise you're stuck there a long time. You really want to punch through those levels to get onto the tour. And that's the way I did it. But I had an inner belief that I had the game or personality or confidence, drive and stubbornness to really get through and get to the top.

第一笔奖金与温网外卡

Do you remember your first paycheck or prize money?

I don't really. I mean, I had some opportunities with the LTA through wildcard, so it probably would have been that. Certainly the Wimbledon wildcard was — when you look at the numbers, it's obviously even better now.

What was the Wimbledon wildcard for you when you did it?

My first one was in qualies, so it probably would have been something around 10,000. First round loser obviously goes up each time to probably like 18 or 20. And then first round prize money these days, you're looking at 70, 80.

Is that right now? For losing first round?

70, 80, something like that. I know the US Open, Australian Open, it's $100,000. I mean, Australian dollars is quite different, but US dollar, 100,000. I'm not 100%.

I remember a few years — and I say a few years ago, time suddenly started moving really quickly, so it's probably about 10 years ago — I remember when it was about 30 grand and that was like, whoa, okay, 30 grand to make it. And then there was a lot of discussion around a couple of years where that money had jumped. I think maybe there was like 10 withdrawals in the first round where players who weren't fit were coming and playing a minimum because that was such important money to them to make, even just from retiring in that first match.

"Come out with crutches." Yeah, I mean, genuinely. Exactly. That's what was happening.

大满贯漏洞与生态裂缝

That's like a deeper issue in the ecosystem in terms of being in the Grand Slams — because how different they are to the tour and financially, it's huge. And it pays for your year. So if you have the ranking where you know, as a minimum, I'm getting first round of these slams, I know I've got a guaranteed, say, 250 grand coming in, which is huge. So I have to turn up at those events. I can't afford not to.

They've done it differently now where they obviously realised what players were doing. Even players that — there's a loop on the system where if you get injured you get protected ranking, and someone was exploiting it a lot where you turn up, go on court, pull out, and because you pulled out it wasn't counted as one of your allocated protected rankings. So you kept your allocated protected rankings, but you took the cheque, and you just found the loophole. And eventually they changed it.

So now if you're on site, you can take 50% of your prize money. So the incentive is: a lucky loser can get in the draw — so someone can play, they pick up 50%, but you also take, by doing nothing, you take home 50%. So I think a lot better.

真实开销：选手到底净赚多少？

You're paid $100,000, say, for a US Open first round loser now. Everyone's like, oh my God, $100,000 to lose the first round. Sounds nice. But you have coaches, you have travel, you have hotels. What is that at tax? Straight away withholding tax, 30%?

Okay, and so you're now down to 70%. What happens then? How much of it goes back to you, a player?

Yeah, you're right. So the US Open is — they call it US Open Series. So you're going from the grass courts to first week is like Atlanta and Washington, then on to the Canadian Masters, which is like Montreal, Toronto, Cincinnati. So it could be like a six-week trip.

So obviously you're paying flights, first of all, to get over there, internally with your team. Let's say if you're on the tour, you probably have a team of a coach and a physio. That minimum. I mean, people do it different ways, but something like that. If you've got the cash, you do it. You're paying them weekly expenses. Hotels are on you for them as well. Generally, coaches are wanting business class flights if you're of a certain level. And you've got all that stuff. You're paying them. You get to New York. So yeah, you've got all that stuff. It adds up.

Obviously, the better you do, in the end you're going to make profit. But it isn't literally like "that's what you get, that's what I'm taking home" — there's all that stuff in between. And you kind of don't see that. Of course, if you're successful on court, you are going to be successful in your bank at the end of the day.

For your coaches and your physios, you're paying for their flights and accommodation. Do you have — are they on a salary? Or is it a balance between a salary and a performance-related bonus based on your winnings?

Yeah, you can do it different ways. As in, it's whatever you individually agree with that person. A lot of my career I had a salary, and as I went on, when I was on the tour, the coach would generally ask for a percentage of prize money, which was probably between 0 and 15%. You can go back at them and debate on that or try and arrange a deal.

Sometimes physios and trainers ask for prize money percentage, but I was not as much there. It was keen there, but at the end of the year as well in the contract was like: if you're 40, 50, 30 to 40, they get another bonus as well. So they always protect themselves, which you totally get. But yeah, some people had prize money — particular agents as well with players, I think do that as well, depending on what you do.

600 万美元的真相

If we're contextualizing it, you earn just over $6 million in prize money throughout your career. So that's prize money on court. That's a 12 to 13 year career. So again, very conscious that these are numbers and putting into context of life, but you're like, wow, okay, that's incredible. But then if we're immediately just on the total package, bringing some of those down, plus then a consistency challenge — when you made an Australian Open semifinal, right? So I'd imagine in that one semifinal, if you lose, you're making as much in that one tournament as you would do in other years.

So that's obviously throughout my career. And for the first few years, the futures, you're not earning money. Unless you're, I would say, 150 to 200, you're not making profit. You're kind of breaking even at best. Below that, you're losing money. That's what I see.

And also, when I was injured with my surgeries from the ages of, let's say, 25, 26 to when I stopped, I didn't make money because I wasn't playing the top events. And during the period of 20 months when I wasn't playing, I was paying coaches' salaries, physios, fitness trainers. So there's a shorter period where I earn a bulk of my money. And of course in that time you can afford to invest in yourself, pay people a bit more, have a travelling — say strength and conditioning, player trainer — rather than at home. So there's all those little things.

But I was lucky my dad's a chartered accountant, so he looked after all my expenses, did all my tax returns, which obviously playing in all these different countries is pretty important. So he was on that — but he was always managing that and I trusted and left all that to him. And I liked to just focus on the tennis. He would always say "this is what's happening." I left him to sort of negotiate with coaches as well. I didn't personally want to do that, but I was aware of in the end what happened in the end.

What year did you make the most and how much did you make in a year?

It would have been 2018 for sure, because I reached the semis far as Australia, and I think that was about ballpark 600,000 Australian dollars for that one tournament. And then throughout the year I won my first tour event, won some Masters matches, Masters series matches, some other Grand Slam matches as well.

一夜暴富的 Tiebreak Tens

The crazy thing is one year, 2015, that we used to have a competition called Tiebreak Tens. And I was still — I was 20. We'd just played Davis Cup. It was the off-season and we played at Royal Albert Hall. It was the Tiebreak Tens, basically a tiebreak to 10. And it was on the sort of pro side: me, Andy and David Ferrer. And on the legends was McEnroe, Tim Henman and Mailís. And I ended up winning it. And the prize to win it was 250 grand for, you know, four tiebreaks, which is crazy.

And then I said to my dad quite a few times: tennis is — or I said life in general — you can have those nights and that financial sort of high. And then I said for how many ever months, nothing. You know, net nothing. So that's the difference in terms of being in the right place, right time with your ranking, your career. And tennis is pretty tough in that way.

It's also so funny, the luck that comes from Tiebreak Tens at Royal Albert Hall versus the slugging it out on tour and delayed lounges.

And this is when I played those guys — obviously, the legends, they retired, they're not playing full time. And we had just won the Davis Cup and Andy had a long year. He played a lot. So he had gone on holiday or rested, and Ferrer had just come back from his honeymoon. And when we came up from Davis, I still — I carried on training, because I was like, it's a lot of money here. I need to give myself a chance. So they sort of turned up a bit more chilled.

And the other thing that was interesting is you got an appearance fee. I think my appearance fee was 10. And the other guys would have been a lot more, especially Andy wouldn't have done that for something low. And the 250 grand was the difference. So let's say someone got paid 150, 200, they only have to pay 50. So the owner obviously has to pay me 240 more, and I was on the lowest for sure to be there. So he had to fork out the most for the guy who was on the lowest appearance.

So you winning was a disaster.

Yeah, it was like — the story was great in terms of the young British guy winning, but the financial was probably no good for him. That was the last year they did it.

收入分配之战：Sabalenka 与 PTPA

So those types of things increasingly have started coming into sport where players need more financial opportunity. We're lucky to be having this conversation now because as of recording only last week, the pay around tennis players has been in the news when Sabalenka brought the conversation that's really been bubbling for quite a long time around the distribution of revenue into tennis.

For those that don't know, the Grand Slams generally pay about 15% to 17% of total tournament revenues to the players, which is netting out, should we say it, around maybe between 60 to 80 million across the different events to all of those players. Now, that again seems like a lot, but actually when distributed all the way across the field, isn't a huge amount when baked into how much money they're actually turning over. And a lot of other sports go far more towards the kind of 40 to 50%, particularly if you're going into some of the American sports, going into the players.

Now, from what I've seen, the players aren't actually asking for massive recalibration. They're asking for about 22% of the revenues to go to the players. And the top players are the ones that are carrying a lot of this fight, which again from people has led to critique going: oh, well, it's not like they need the money. But that's not really the point of what, from my understanding, they're doing. They're doing this because if you have Yannick Sinner and Aryna Sabalenka arguing the case for more money, it's far more impactful than having player number 150 in the world who can easily be dismissed.

For sure, yeah. My point being: we've talked about how money has impacted players and the importance of this. We also talk about the prestige and history of these events and the assumption that, oh, people will always want to play. As someone that's been in this, how do you view this situation?

网球治理的"七头怪"问题

Yeah, I mean, you said right — where do we begin? Should they be — so as a player, yes, you want to get paid more. They are the product of the sport. They're the ones that's going out there.

So it's really like tennis is a very muddled system, as in you have seven governing bodies essentially, which is the four slams, ATP, WTA, and ITF. And it's really hard for them to work all together in their own way. The slams can do what they want. They're not obliged to follow ATP rules or tour rules or whatever that is. They can decide what they want to do, and that's it. The ATP as well — the tournaments are owned by the actual tournament. The ATP don't own the license, the tournament. So Rome, Madrid, they own their license. They can also essentially do what they want to do. And if they don't meet standards, they will get fined. But there's nothing you can really say.

In terms of an actual number, I don't know the answer. Like, what is the answer? If it's 22, then in 2018, 5 or 10 years, it's going to be 25. I don't know how that will work. The one thing I would say is the slams provide the most money to the sport for the players. That is where they're getting the most benefit. Is it up to the tours to reach that standard, as in they need to provide more first before attacking the slams in some way?

And the other side of it — like, I mean, so Wimbledon last year, they actually had a net loss on their whole thing, I think, of 70 million because they're probably putting in money in projects and building, hopefully, the golf course and all those kind of things. But I don't know what the answer is.

The other side of it is I've heard arguments in the tournament side where, as Wimbledon, as a brand, you get to play — if you win Wimbledon, whoever's won it, the benefits you get post that, you won't get through playing a challenger. If you win a challenger 250, you don't get a million pound contracts with a clothing company or a jewellery company from that. So the opportunities you get from winning by playing Wimbledon is also down the line. And it's not even winning. By playing on centre court against a top seed, the exposure you get is probably 50,000 to 100,000 Instagram followers.

The other debate is: when you get the top guys asking for money, is it for them or is it for the benefit of the sport further down? So is someone who earns maybe 10 grand — no, more like six or seven grand at a challenger for winning — should they be earning 10 grand, which for them is a big difference in that way? But is someone who wins 2 million, is it 2.1? So where is it going as well? Is it a big difference?

现代体育的真实成本结构

That's what makes me think that maybe it's rose-tinted glasses, but again, if it's Sabalenka or Djokovic or Sinner having these discussions and we're talking about another 100, 150 grand of what maybe they would get, they can go and get that for a branded post somewhere or they could do it in another way. So to put this fight in there, I do believe that this is far more about the sustainable top to bottom ecosystem.

Because what we've really heard so far, right, is: to become a tennis player, it costs a ton of money to do it. And then when you become a tennis player, you're not guaranteed a ton of money. In actual fact, most of the time, for the majority of players that make it, you spend a ton of money to put yourself in a really difficult earning position where now I'm going, well, is this a viable career? The sport is going to suffer because gradually people just won't be picking up rackets in the same way that they would have done previously.

Revenue doesn't suggest health of revenue. And so if you think about the modern media product that's required today, it requires a lot more investment. Your cogs go up as an institution. And so, frankly, they could be losing more money than ever because they've got to provide a better product to the different media providers. You should actually do it as a percent of profit margin. That's what you should do. Because you could be having to invest — it's a worse financial decision for them to deliver that product. Do you see what I'm saying? Completely.

The players to me are actually being quite reasonable in this discussion. It is not an unachievable conversation. It just feels like there's a breakdown in whatever that communication line needs to look like, because players don't seem to have a really collective powerful union.

PTPA、自下而上的可持续性

No, that's the problem. And you're right — compared to other markets, what they're getting and the comparing. And that's the whole debate at the minute, that there's lawsuits with the PTPA, which I actually still don't know what it means, but it's essentially a voice for the players. And I know Australian Open settled with them, but the other three slams are still in a legal process. I don't know what the outcome is that they're trying to get to. But yeah, the players don't really have a voice in that way.

But at the same time, I'm pretty sure the tour is owned by some of the players, by some of the tournaments. There's a split.

In general, I would like to see more money lowered down. I don't like how limited it is, as in how high you have to be to make a living on the tour. How high is that? Top 50? No, like as in profit, it's somewhere in the 100 — so 150, 200 maybe. You could make money, but you're not making lots of money to have a sustainable career. And I think that should be way, way more — as in top 300 should be able to make a living on your own with the prize money and all that stuff.

双打的商业悖论

If you think about that prize money being, as we said, like kind of the publicly reported number of like 6 million, the idea then is that you have like brand partnerships and commercials. The problem is with the players that sell tennis is probably only a handful of players, which is crazy. And those are the priority for the rest of the game. And I think they're quite honest with that with the tournaments or governing bodies — that that is the situation.

Doubles doesn't do anything, even though it probably should. In the UK, we're very successful at doubles, but it just doesn't do any — it doesn't sell, as in doesn't make money. There's arguments internally about it. I actually think they should probably just sell it, and someone else come in and say, actually, I want to do, I want to make something of this. But I don't think it's within the ATP's interest because they want to keep hold of the market and control it all.

If it was a doubles — because again, really nice idea — because doubles is great to watch. When you actually sit down and watch it, you know, it's really fun. It's a great sport. Like, it's completely different to singles, the talent.

But if you separated a doubles tournament from a singles tournament — you've played doubles, right? You were successful at it in junior, you won two junior Grand Slams in your doubles. Right, so you take that out of the main tournament, you're probably not going to go and play those doubles tournaments if we're separating the products, because it reduces your... As singles players, no, they'll have no interest. And that's why in US Open they did the whole mixed doubles thing before tournament, and only singles players could play it. So it's a doubles event but only singles, they sell. People want to go and watch them.

And also, do you think if you had a doubles event — I don't think people would go. Like, you go to see the doubles match at Wimbledon because Kyle's playing next against Sinner or against Novak. Cool, I'm here. I'm here, great. But I'm going to go. But you do have Draper's paired with Pagula. Exactly.

And it's actually a really sad thing, like situation in that, because you don't want things to drop down or to disappear in the game. You're losing, but the reality is there's no money being made, as in the ATP have to fund it and put in the money. Darwin, survival of the fittest — it doesn't make money. People don't want it. I'm just calling a spade a spade. Let it drop.

Yeah, that's why there was a bit of a — well, I've had discussions with players at the MBA and stuff, as in get an independent person to take it on, maybe even run it alongside the singles events, but it's marketed and funded by an external, or they go off and do their own thing. Like they go to other venues where maybe you could go to Asia and there's interest because over there they don't get much live sports — in China, you know, you're just going to get lots of people, or India, I don't know.

商业窗口、Rolex 与赞助合同

To your commercial point — we've had a number of Olympians on the show. And the thing they continue to talk about is maximising the window that they're in the shop for, basically. That is very, very brief. They win a medal, they're the biggest news for a month, and then afterwards it can kind of collapse around. You're like, shit, that's really, really tough.

And our point really around the commercials, I think — and we spoke about this a little bit before — all the money accrues right to the top. The right at the top is already earning the huge cash. But it's also sport now is increasingly moments. You had a moment. You'd been around for a while when you got to the semi-finals in Australia. It wasn't as if no one ever heard of you before, but we hadn't seen you that far in a tournament. And also this country, we get so excited. What did that do for you? Did it suddenly create a wave of interest beyond the court? Did it feel like a flash? Did it sustain once you'd gone through? Like what was that like when you suddenly had that jump in achievement, especially in this country? It's up or you're down and you feel it.

Especially when I made that tournament run, you have lots more interest. Also, it benefited me in my career, like financially, sponsors.

Did it after that?

Yeah, big time.

What did you sign?

I signed that year — I signed with Rolex, Evian, Jaguar, British Airways, new Nike deal.

So pause, pause. We got Rolex. It's really shitty brand, snow. We're getting to my mula in the cooler. Okay, Rolex — yeah, they're never gonna pay me. Okay, so I want to live vicariously through you. What does that look like?

Amazing. First of all, the best thing is obviously the exposure, because Rolex is a very sort of premium — well, it was premium tennis. They invest quite a bit in the tournaments and the slams. They have the rights to all the timekeeping, the slams now. And I think they do a few like Monte Carlo and Shanghai.

But for me, it was the exposure it gives you. In terms of financially, it wasn't huge, but you're getting the timepieces.

How much is it?

So you get the credit, and the credit is like — I think it's about 50,000 in my first year contract. And then I renewed and it was a bit more — not 50, but you get more budget to purchase more watches. So yeah, in the end, how many have you got? I got in the end I got six. And you can purchase one as well.

The Rolex was not — yeah, not designed of course. Our crows and Roger Federer are on another different kind of contract for sure, and Sinner. But more being associated with such a premium brand is amazing what you do, the hospitality as well.

This is where Charlie's like, oh God, Harry.

So, Evian.

Yeah. I mean, give you credits. I hydrate the shit out of you. They're, I've been peeing for four years. Exactly. Seriously, what — so that was, yeah, that was financial. I don't often ever know the financial. But again, they do very sort of niche campaigns. Not lots of athletes are with them. Like now they have Emma, Alcaraz, I think Francis Tiafoe. At the time, like Stan Wawrinka was there, Maria Sharapova as well. So that was very cool.

And you obviously get unlimited sort of stock of water. And every tournament you play, when you walk into your hotel, I just had boxes of Evian in the corner. So they sent it all around the world and you come into your hotel.

And you also had to use it at the tournaments. So even if the tournament was on Spring or whatever, Vittel, you had to use Evian on court, which was a bit of a battle. I was going to say — how does that work? Because normally tournaments usurp players. They don't want you to use it, but you just have to really fight with it. And the give and take, you just have to take off the plastic wrapper, which obviously defeats the point. But yeah, sometimes you get away with it. Sometimes they're very insistent. US Open and Wimbledon is FIJI, so you were fine.

Appearance days, I didn't mind doing, but I was always scheduling them in. It was a night, you've got to do it back home. When you're at the tournaments, they want time. But that's all part of being a professional athlete. And when you're younger, you don't think about that. But as you get older, you realise that's what it entails.

But that was very black and white, those things. Be like, well, if you don't want to do it, don't sign a contract. You sign a contract, you've got to do it. Fulfill your agreement, relationships. And yeah, Jaguar was the same. You had to do days, but I got a nice car to do it. So didn't complain. British Airways gave me quite a lot of nice business class flights, which also was for my team. So going back to expenses, they were getting business class flights. I was — there was no money involved, but of course I'm not spending the money. So actually that's a really good deal for you. Really beneficial. As in, not something that maybe doesn't benefit — like maybe a yogurt brand, like it's not... But the flights were really beneficial. We'll send you a shitload of Oikos.

But this, you know, coming back to that point — so that really, Australia 2018 was the moment where this type of deal suddenly became a reality.

Yeah, exactly. And that — my agents had a big thing to do with that. And then the British Airways thing happened straight away. Literally, I finished the tournament and he said: you're flying back British Airways, because when you land, they're going to do a thing with you. So in the pipeline while the tournament was going, he was already arranging that relationship, which I didn't know, but he was doing that.

And the idea was trying to align myself with very sort of British brands. And I guess yeah, Evian is not quite that, but they were very premium in the tennis world. And obviously Jaguar and British Airways and Rolex is such a — you don't tone that down. So yeah, that was the philosophy.

重温澳网 2018 的奇迹周

Can we have a bit of fun with that — like proper tennis side? You start every Grand Slam with hope. You then start winning a few games. You then get into stages you've never been in before. The next thing you know, you're in semifinals. I mean, you beat Dimitrov right on the way and he was number three in the world that time. So you're really pushing through. What was that whole kind of couple of weeks like?

Yeah, it's incredible because you're just gaining momentum. I had a really good off-season. I remember that year, the end of 2017, we did a shed-load of physical work. I'd just got a new coach. I did my off-season in Bahamas. So basically every day, you know, blazing sun, getting ready for the Australia heat. And I had two juniors with me and we just put them in a corner and moved me side to side doing two-on-one drills for three, four minutes at a time, interval stuff running the track. So I was in really good shape.

And I turned up to Brisbane. I won two matches. I beat Shapovalov and Chung, who actually made semifinals that year as well. And then I rolled my ankle against Dimitrov. And it bruised, was blue. I meant to play Auckland, but I pulled out because of that. And actually in hindsight was a blessing because I actually got some rest in that week before Australian Open. Luckily it wasn't that bad. I stayed, the swelling went down, I could play.

And I played Kevin Anderson first round, who had made the final of US Open the year before. So it was a really tough match, tough draw. But in that moment, I'd never gone deep in a slam like that before. So my mind wasn't thinking about it. It was just thinking: I've got this tough match against Anderson, big server. And I won in five sets — probably the biggest win in my career, one of the biggest. And that was like a real turning point of: I'm here. I'm in this tournament.

Won my second round straight sets, felt great against Istomin. And throughout the tournament — third round against Basilashvili, five sets, I can't remember, but we had a 20-minute game in the fourth set, blazing sunshine. Like it reached 40 degrees that day. And I started cramping, and I was like: this is no good. I'm in the fourth set. I'm two sets to one down. I need to win the next two.

I get through that game and luckily for me, he tanks the rest of that set, because he probably starts feeling it as well. He's like, I'm just going to leave this set and we're going to go for a fifth set shootout. And I was cramping throughout, and I just said to myself: I'm going to focus on my service games. It's not in tennis matches often you tell yourself to back off. It's always like every point you give it your all, like it's your last point of your career type vibe. But I knew I couldn't sustain that.
